topics.title: Introduction to Biomedical Transport Phenomena: Role of Diffusion and Convection in Physiological Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT863 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Biomedical Transport Phenomena: Role of Diffusion and Convection in Physiological Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Transport Phenomena — The Foundational Definition

In the context of the **PEBMT863 — Biomedical Transport Phenomena** syllabus (KTU 2024 Scheme), **Transport Phenomena** is formally defined as the unified engineering science that quantitatively describes the transfer (or *transport*) of three fundamental physical entities: **momentum**, **energy (heat)**, and **mass**. It applies the conservation laws of physics (mass, momentum, and energy) to derive governing differential equations that predict the rates at which these entities move through any system — biological or industrial.

In living systems, every physiological function — from a single cell absorbing glucose to the entire human body regulating temperature — is governed by transport phenomena. The course treats the human body as a complex chemical/bioreactor and applies the same rigorous mathematical framework used in chemical engineering to analyze it.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Transport

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — The Three Sectors of Transport Phenomena**

| Pillar | Quantity Transported | Physiological Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| **Momentum Transfer** | Momentum ($\rho \vec{v}$) | Blood flow, cerebrospinal fluid circulation, airflow in lungs |
| **Heat (Energy) Transfer** | Thermal energy ($q$) | Thermoregulation, cryotherapy, hyperthermia treatment of tumors |
| **Mass Transfer** | Chemical species ($N_A$) | Oxygen delivery, drug distribution, nutrient absorption, renal filtration |

### 1.3 Defining Diffusion — Molecular-Scale Transport

**Diffusion** is the net movement of molecules (or ions, atoms) from a region of higher chemical potential (or concentration) to a region of lower chemical potential, driven entirely by **random thermal (Brownian) motion** — no external bulk fluid motion is required.

**Formal KTU Definition:** *Diffusion is the spontaneous transport of matter arising from the random molecular motion of individual particles, quantified by Fick's Laws, in the absence of any bulk convective flow.*

### 1.4 Defining Convection — Bulk-Scale Transport

**Convection** is the transport of mass, momentum, or energy by the **bulk, macroscopic motion of a fluid** (liquid or gas). It does not depend on molecular randomness — it is driven by an external force (a pressure gradient, a pump, gravity) that moves entire fluid elements from one location to another.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Sub-Classification of Convection**
> - **Forced Convection:** Bulk flow is imposed by an external agent (e.g., the heart's pumping action driving blood, mechanical ventilation).
> - **Natural (Free) Convection:** Bulk flow is induced by density differences within the fluid caused by temperature or concentration gradients (e.g., buoyancy-driven flow in a feverish limb).

### 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**The Swimming Pool Analogy:**
Imagine a swimming pool with one end filled with warm water and the other end with cold, dyed water.

- **Diffusion:** Even if the pool pump is OFF, the dye molecules will slowly, randomly wander into the warm side. The colour will eventually equalize, but it will take hours. This slow, random, molecule-by-molecule mixing is **diffusion**.

- **Convection:** When you turn ON the pool pump (or blow across the surface with a fan), whole parcels of water are physically pushed across the pool. The colour equalizes in seconds. This is **convection** — bulk motion doing the transport.

**Physiological Analogy:**
- **Diffusion in the body:** Oxygen molecules crossing the thin alveolar–capillary membrane in the lungs (a distance of less than 1 micrometre).
- **Convection in the body:** The same oxygen molecules being swept, in seconds, from the lungs to your toe via the arterial blood flow — a journey of over one metre that diffusion alone could never accomplish within a human lifetime.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for Examinations:** Diffusion is effective over *microscopic distances* (μm to mm), while convection is the body's strategy for *macroscopic transport* (cm to m). The human body uses **convection for long-distance delivery** and **diffusion for the final short-range crossing into cells**.

### 1.6 Why This Topic Matters in Biomedical Engineering

- **Drug Delivery Engineering:** Pharmacokinetics and controlled-release devices are designed by coupling diffusion (release from a matrix) and convection (carried by blood).
- **Artificial Organs:** Oxygenators, dialyzers, and heart-lung machines are designed by applying the very same mass-transfer equations.
- **Tissue Engineering:** Designing scaffolds requires quantifying nutrient transport (diffusion) and waste removal (convection via perfusion).
- **Diagnostic Imaging:** Contrast agent distribution is governed by coupled diffusion–convection equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Framework — How Diffusion and Convection Work Together

To systematically analyze a physiological transport problem, the KTU framework requires breaking it into the following logical steps:

**Step 1 — Identify the Species Being Transported.**
Is it O₂, CO₂, glucose, a drug, urea, an ion, or heat? Each species has its own diffusion coefficient and boundary conditions.

**Step 2 — Identify the Driving Force.**
- Diffusion is driven by a **concentration gradient** ($\nabla C$).
- Convection is driven by a **pressure gradient** ($\nabla P$) or an **imposed velocity field** ($\vec{v}$).

**Step 3 — Identify the Transport Mode.**
- If a bulk fluid velocity is present $\Rightarrow$ convective term is active.
- If species must cross a stationary membrane $\Rightarrow$ diffusive term dominates.

**Step 4 — Identify the Geometry and Length Scale.**
- Capillary (length scale $\sim 10^{-3}$ m) $\Rightarrow$ convection dominant in the lumen, diffusion dominant across the wall.
- Cell membrane (length scale $\sim 10^{-8}$ m) $\Rightarrow$ diffusion is the only realistic mode.

**Step 5 — Write the Constitutive Flux Equation.**
The total molar flux of species $A$, $N_A$, is the **sum of the convective flux and the diffusive flux**:

$$N_A = \underbrace{C_A \, \vec{v}}_{\text{convective flux}} \quad - \quad \underbrace{D_{AB} \, \nabla C_A}_{\text{diffusive flux}}$$

**Step 6 — Couple with a Conservation Law.**
Apply mass conservation to a differential control volume to obtain the **General Convection–Diffusion Equation**:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \left( C_A \vec{v} \right) = \nabla \cdot \left( D_{AB} \, \nabla C_A \right) + R_A}$$

where $R_A$ is the rate of generation/consumption of $A$ per unit volume (e.g., O₂ consumption by mitochondria, or CO₂ production).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Convection–Diffusion Equation is the single most important equation in this course.** Every transport problem in the body — oxygen delivery, drug distribution, hemodialysis — is described by some form of this equation.

### 2.2 Fick's Two Laws of Diffusion — The Foundation

**Fick's First Law (Steady-state, 1-D):**
States that the diffusive flux is proportional to the concentration gradient. The constant of proportionality is the **diffusion coefficient** $D_{AB}$ (units: **m²/s**).

$$J_{A,x} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

The negative sign indicates transport occurs *down* the gradient (from high to low concentration).

**Fick's Second Law (Unsteady-state, 1-D):**
Derived by combining Fick's First Law with the continuity equation. It describes how concentration evolves in time and space.

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master the following table — it is a minimum-requirement checklist for the ESE.**

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application in Physiology |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's First Law (1-D) | $J_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ | O₂ flux across alveolar wall |
| 2 | Fick's Second Law (1-D) | $\partial C_A / \partial t = D_{AB} \, \partial^2 C_A / \partial x^2$ | Drug release from a polymer matrix |
| 3 | Total Molar Flux (dilute) | $N_A = C_A v - D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ | Capillary blood transport |
| 4 | General Convection–Diffusion Eqn. | $\partial C_A/\partial t + \nabla \cdot (C_A \vec{v}) = \nabla \cdot (D_{AB} \nabla C_A) + R_A$ | Whole-body pharmacokinetics |
| 5 | Fick's First Law (Vector) | $\vec{J}_A = -D_{AB} \, \nabla C_A$ | 3-D tissue diffusion |
| 6 | Peclet Number | $Pe = vL / D_{AB}$ | Dimensionless ratio of convection/diffusion |
| 7 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho v L / \mu$ | Flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent) in vessels |
| 8 | Schmidt Number | $Sc = \mu / (\rho D_{AB})$ | Ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity |
| 9 | Sherwood Number | $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$ | Convective mass-transfer coefficient |
| 10 | Biot Number (Mass) | $Bi_m = k_c L / D_{AB} = Sh$ | Internal vs. external resistance in tissue |
| 11 | Solute Flux Across Membrane | $J_s = P \, (C_{\text{plasma}} - C_{\text{tissue}})$ | Capillary-tissue exchange ($P$ = permeability) |
| 12 | Permeability (Resistance form) | $1/P = 1/P_m + 1/(D \cdot \text{Porosity})$ | Kedem–Katchalsky barrier model |
| 13 | Hagen–Poiseuille Flow | $Q = \pi \Delta P \, r^4 / (8 \mu L)$ | Volumetric blood flow rate |
| 14 | Shear Stress at Vessel Wall | $\tau_w = 4 \mu Q / (\pi r^3)$ | Endothelial mechanotransduction |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe / Vertical Bar Rule:** In your answer book, never use a raw `|` symbol for absolute value inside equations — it can be confused with conditional probability. KTU's preferred notation is $\vert x \vert$ or, when set in display, $\mid x \mid$.

### 2.4 Physiological Examples — Engineering Reality

- **Alveolar Gas Exchange (Lungs):** O₂ diffuses across the $\sim 0.5 \, \mu m$ alveolar–capillary membrane. Fick's First Law applies directly; $D_{O_2, \text{membrane}} \approx 2 \times 10^{-11} \, m^2/s$ (effective).
- **Systemic Oxygen Delivery:** Once O₂ enters the bloodstream, **convection** dominates. Bulk blood flow (typical cardiac output $\sim 5$ L/min) carries O₂-bound hemoglobin to every capillary in the body.
- **Tissue–Capillary Exchange:** At the capillary wall, **diffusion re-emerges** as the dominant mechanism, since flow within the capillary is slow and the wall is thin.
- **Drug Delivery (Oral → Bloodstream):** Drug dissolves in the GI tract (diffusion through unstirred boundary layer), is swept by blood flow (convection), and finally diffuses across the capillary wall into tissue interstitium.
- **Hemodialysis:** Diffusion across the artificial membrane removes urea from blood; convection (ultrafiltration) removes excess water.

### 2.5 The Peclet Number — A Student's Best Friend

The Peclet number is a **dimensionless** ratio that tells you, at a glance, whether convection or diffusion dominates the transport in a given system.

$$Pe = \frac{\text{Rate of transport by convection}}{\text{Rate of transport by diffusion}} = \frac{v L}{D_{AB}}$$

- $Pe \gg 1$ (e.g., $Pe \approx 10^{6}$ in the aorta): **Convection dominates.** Diffusion is negligible.
- $Pe \ll 1$ (e.g., $Pe \approx 10^{-3}$ across a cell membrane): **Diffusion dominates.** Convection is negligible.
- $Pe \approx 1$: Both modes are equally important — this is where the **coupled convection–diffusion equation must be solved in full**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fick's First Law from Elementary Kinetic Theory

**Objective:** Derive the relation $J_{A,x} = -D_{AB} \, dC_A / dx$ from a molecular-level model.

**Assumptions:**
- The gas/liquid is dilute and ideal.
- Molecules are identical spheres undergoing random thermal motion.
- No net bulk flow in the $x$-direction (pure diffusion only).
- No chemical reaction.

**Step 1 — Define an Imaginary Reference Plane.**
Consider an imaginary plane perpendicular to the $x$-axis located at position $x$. Molecules approaching this plane from the left have, on average, just travelled a distance equal to the **mean free path** $\lambda$ from their last collision.

**Step 2 — Determine the Number of Molecules Crossing per Unit Area per Unit Time.**
For an isotropic gas, exactly one-third of all molecules move along the $x$-axis at any instant. Of these, half move in the $+x$ direction and half in the $-x$ direction. Therefore, the fraction of molecules crossing the plane from the left is $\frac{1}{6}$, and from the right also $\frac{1}{6}$.

The average molecular speed in the $x$-direction is denoted $\bar{v}_x$. The flux of molecules crossing the plane from the left (at position $x - \lambda$) is:

$$J_{\text{left}} = \frac{1}{6} \, \bar{v}_x \, C_A(x - \lambda)$$

Similarly, from the right (at position $x + \lambda$):

$$J_{\text{right}} = \frac{1}{6} \, \bar{v}_x \, C_A(x + \lambda)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Net Flux.**
The net flux in the $+x$ direction is the flux from the left minus the flux from the right:

$$J_{A,x} = J_{\text{left}} - J_{\text{right}} = \frac{1}{6} \bar{v}_x \left[ C_A(x - \lambda) - C_A(x + \lambda) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Taylor-Series Expansion.**
Expand the two concentration terms around position $x$ using a Taylor series, retaining only the linear (first-order) term because $\lambda$ is microscopically small:

$$C_A(x - \lambda) = C_A(x) - \lambda \frac{dC_A}{dx} + \mathcal{O}(\lambda^2)$$

$$C_A(x + \lambda) = C_A(x) + \lambda \frac{dC_A}{dx} + \mathcal{O}(\lambda^2)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify.**
Substituting these expansions into the net-flux expression:

$$J_{A,x} = \frac{1}{6} \bar{v}_x \left[ \left( C_A - \lambda \frac{dC_A}{dx} \right) - \left( C_A + \lambda \frac{dC_A}{dx} \right) \right]$$

**Step 6 — Algebraic Simplification.**
The $C_A$ terms cancel exactly:

$$J_{A,x} = \frac{1}{6} \bar{v}_x \left[ -2 \lambda \frac{dC_A}{dx} \right]$$

$$J_{A,x} = -\frac{1}{3} \bar{v}_x \lambda \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

**Step 7 — Define the Diffusion Coefficient.**
Define the proportionality constant between flux and concentration gradient as the **diffusion coefficient**:

$$D_{AB} \equiv \frac{1}{3} \bar{v}_x \lambda \quad (\text{units: } m^2/s)$$

**Step 8 — State Fick's First Law.**

$$\boxed{J_{A,x} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** $D_{AB}$ increases with temperature (faster molecular motion) and decreases with molecular size (shorter mean free path). This is why diffusion is faster in hot gases than in cold liquids.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fick's Second Law from Mass Conservation

**Objective:** Derive the time-dependent form $\partial C_A / \partial t = D_{AB} \, \partial^2 C_A / \partial x^2$.

**Step 1 — Apply Conservation of Mass to a Differential Slab.**
Consider a thin slab of thickness $dx$ and unit cross-sectional area. Let $J_A(x)$ be the molar flux entering the left face and $J_A(x + dx)$ be the flux leaving the right face.

**Step 2 — Net Rate of Accumulation.**
The rate of change of moles inside the slab is the difference between inflow and outflow:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx = J_A(x) - J_A(x + dx)$$

**Step 3 — Taylor Expansion of the Outflow Term.**

$$J_A(x + dx) = J_A(x) + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} dx + \mathcal{O}(dx^2)$$

**Step 4 — Subtract and Cancel.**

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx = - \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} dx$$

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Fick's First Law.**
Assuming $D_{AB}$ is constant in space:

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 6 — Final Equation.**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Peclet Number from First Principles

**Objective:** Show that $Pe = vL / D_{AB}$ is a meaningful ratio of convection to diffusion.

**Step 1 — Define a Characteristic Length Scale $L$.**
In a typical transport problem, the geometry (e.g., capillary diameter, tissue depth) defines a length scale $L$ over which the concentration changes appreciably.

**Step 2 — Express the Convective Flux.**
The convective flux of species $A$ is approximately $C_A \cdot v$ (concentration times bulk velocity).

**Step 3 — Estimate the Diffusive Flux.**
Using Fick's First Law, the diffusive flux is of order $D_{AB} \cdot C_A / L$.

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio.**

$$Pe = \frac{\text{Convective flux}}{\text{Diffusive flux}} = \frac{C_A v}{D_{AB} \, C_A / L}$$

$$Pe = \frac{v L}{D_{AB}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always report $Pe$ as an order-of-magnitude number ($10^{-3}$, $10^{0}$, $10^{3}$, $10^{6}$). This shows the examiner you understand the physical regime, not just the formula.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Coupling in a Real System (Capillary Oxygen Delivery)

| Anatomical Region | Length Scale $L$ | Velocity $v$ | Dominant Mode | Typical $Pe$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aorta | $\sim 10^{-2}$ m | $\sim 0.4$ m/s | Convection | $Pe \sim 10^{5}$ |
| Arteriole | $\sim 10^{-4}$ m | $\sim 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s | Convection | $Pe \sim 10^{1}$ |
| Capillary lumen | $\sim 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m | $\sim 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s | Mixed (convection $\sim$ diffusion) | $Pe \sim 10^{-1}$ to $10^{0}$ |
| Capillary wall | $\sim 10^{-6}$ m | $\approx 0$ | **Diffusion only** | $Pe \approx 0$ |
| Tissue interstitium | $\sim 10^{-5}$ m | $\approx 0$ | **Diffusion only** | $Pe \approx 0$ |
| Cell membrane | $\sim 10^{-8}$ m | $\approx 0$ | **Diffusion only** | $Pe \approx 0$ |

This table demonstrates a fundamental physiological design principle: **the body uses macroscopic convection (heart, vessels) for long-range transport and microscopic diffusion for the final cellular delivery.**

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Biomedical Transport Phenomena

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Biomedical Transport Phenomena] --> node2[Momentum Transfer]
    node1 --> node3[Heat Transfer]
    node1 --> node4[Mass Transfer]

    node2 --> node2a[Blood Flow in Arteries]
    node2 --> node2b[CSF Circulation in Brain]
    node2 --> node2c[Airflow in Lungs]
    node2 --> node2d[Lubrication in Joints]

    node3 --> node3a[Cutaneous Thermoregulation]
    node3 --> node3b[Hyperthermia Tumor Ablation]
    node3 --> node3c[Hypothermia in Surgery]
    node3 --> node3d[Heat Exchange in ECMO]

    node4 --> node4a[O2 and CO2 Exchange in Alveoli]
    node4 --> node4b[Glucose Absorption in Intestine]
    node4 --> node4c[Drug Distribution]
    node4 --> node4d[Renal Filtration]
    node4 --> node4e[Hemodialysis]
```

### 4.2 Coupling of Diffusion and Convection Along the Oxygen Pathway

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Alveolar Air PO2 approx 100 mmHg] -->|DIFFUSION across alveolar wall| stepB[Pulmonary Capillary Blood]
    stepB -->|CONVECTION via pulmonary vein| stepC[Left Heart and Aorta]
    stepC -->|CONVECTION via arterial tree| stepD[Systemic Arterioles]
    stepD -->|CONVECTION via capillaries| stepE[Systemic Capillary Lumen]
    stepE -->|DIFFUSION across capillary wall| stepF[Tissue Interstitium]
    stepF -->|DIFFUSION across cell membrane| stepG[Cell Cytosol]
    stepG -->|DIFFUSION across mitochondrial membrane| stepH[Mitochondrial Matrix]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — A Pharmacokinetic Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    block1[Drug Dosage Form] -->|Dissolution DIFFUSION| block2[GIT Lumen]
    block2 -->|Absorption DIFFUSION| block3[Blood Plasma]
    block3 -->|Distribution CONVECTION| block4[Systemic Circulation]
    block4 -->|DIFFUSION across capillary wall| block5[Target Tissue]
    block5 -->|Cellular Uptake DIFFUSION| block6[Receptor Site]
    block3 -->|Metabolism| block7[Liver]
    block7 -->|Excretion CONVECTION| block8[Kidney Filtration]
    block8 -->|DIFFUSION| block9[Urine Output]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow — When Does Diffusion vs. Convection Dominate?

```mermaid
graph TD
    q1[Is there bulk fluid motion?] -->|Yes| q2[Compute Peclet Number]
    q1 -->|No| q3[Pure Diffusion: Apply Ficks First Law]
    q2 -->|Pe greater than 1| q4[Convection Dominant: Solve Convective Flux]
    q2 -->|Pe less than 1| q5[Diffusion Dominant: Solve Ficks Second Law]
    q2 -->|Pe approx equal to 1| q6[Solve Full Convection Diffusion Equation]
    q4 --> q7[Validate with Reynolds Number for Flow Regime]
    q6 --> q7
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The KTU examiner may ask you to draw a similar block diagram of any physiological transport system. The key is to clearly identify *where* diffusion occurs (across stationary interfaces) and *where* convection occurs (in moving fluid streams).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
*Define diffusion and convection in the context of biomedical transport phenomena. State Fick's First Law of diffusion with its mathematical form.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Diffusion:** Diffusion is the net transport of mass arising from the random thermal (Brownian) motion of individual molecules in the absence of any bulk fluid motion. It occurs down a concentration gradient and is governed by Fick's Laws.

**Convection:** Convection is the transport of mass (or heat, or momentum) due to the **bulk, macroscopic motion of a fluid**. It is driven by an external pressure gradient or an imposed velocity field (e.g., the pumping action of the heart).

**Fick's First Law of Diffusion (1-D, steady-state):**

$$J_{A,x} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

where $J_{A,x}$ is the molar flux in the $x$-direction (mol/m²·s), $D_{AB}$ is the diffusion coefficient (m²/s), and $dC_A/dx$ is the concentration gradient. The negative sign indicates transport from high to low concentration.

**Physiological Example:** O₂ diffusing across the alveolar–capillary membrane in the lungs.

*(Self-Assessment: 1 mark for each correct definition, 1 mark for the law with units.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
*What is the Peclet number? Explain its physical significance with one physiological example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Peclet number is a **dimensionless group** that quantifies the relative importance of convective transport to diffusive transport in a given system. It is defined as:

$$Pe = \frac{v L}{D_{AB}}$$

where $v$ is the characteristic bulk velocity (m/s), $L$ is the characteristic length scale (m), and $D_{AB}$ is the diffusion coefficient (m²/s).

**Physical Significance:**

- If $Pe \gg 1$: **Convection dominates** diffusion.
- If $Pe \ll 1$: **Diffusion dominates** convection.
- If $Pe \approx 1$: Both mechanisms are equally important.

**Physiological Example:** In the human aorta, $v \approx 0.4$ m/s, $L \approx 0.01$ m, $D_{O_2,\text{blood}} \approx 10^{-9}$ m²/s, giving $Pe \approx 4 \times 10^{6}$. Convection overwhelmingly dominates, which is why O₂ reaches the foot in seconds rather than years.

In contrast, across a cell membrane ($\sim 10^{-8}$ m), $Pe \to 0$, and diffusion is the only viable transport mechanism.

*(Self-Assessment: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for criterion, 1 mark for the physiological example with numerical justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE — Module 1, Internal Choice]`

**(a) Derive Fick's First Law of diffusion from kinetic theory, clearly stating all assumptions. (7 marks)**

*Step-by-step Model Solution:*

1. **Assumptions (1 mark):** Dilute system, isotropic random motion, steady-state, no bulk flow, no chemical reaction, single mean free path $\lambda$.

2. **Imaginary reference plane and flux from each side (2 marks):** State the expressions
$$J_{\text{left}} = \frac{1}{6} \bar{v}_x C_A(x - \lambda), \quad J_{\text{right}} = \frac{1}{6} \bar{v}_x C_A(x + \lambda)$$

3. **Net flux expression (1 mark):**
$$J_{A,x} = \frac{1}{6} \bar{v}_x \left[ C_A(x - \lambda) - C_A(x + \lambda) \right]$$

4. **Taylor series expansion and simplification (2 marks):** Expand, cancel the $C_A(x)$ terms, define $D_{AB} = \frac{1}{3} \bar{v}_x \lambda$, and obtain:
$$J_{A,x} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

5. **Final statement and units (1 mark):** Identify $D_{AB}$ in m²/s and mention the negative sign convention.

*(Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 for line-by-line mathematical details.)*

---

**(b) Discuss the roles of diffusion and convection in oxygen transport from the lungs to peripheral tissues. Identify the locations where each mechanism dominates. (7 marks)**

*Step-by-step Model Solution:*

| Region | Dominant Mechanism | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Alveolus → pulmonary capillary | **Diffusion** | Distance $\sim 0.5 \, \mu m$ is too small for convection; $Pe \ll 1$ |
| Pulmonary vein → left heart | **Convection** | Bulk blood flow; $Pe \gg 1$ |
| Aorta → arteries → arterioles | **Convection** | High flow velocity ($\sim 0.4$ m/s); $Pe \sim 10^{5}$ |
| Systemic capillary lumen | **Convection** (slow) | $v \sim 10^{-4}$ m/s; $Pe \sim 10^{-1}$ to $10^{0}$ |
| Capillary wall → tissue | **Diffusion** | Stationary membrane; $Pe \to 0$ |
| Tissue → cell membrane → cytosol → mitochondrion | **Diffusion** | Length scale $\sim 10^{-8}$ m; convection impossible |

**Key Conclusion (1 mark):** The human body uses a *hybrid* transport strategy — **convection for long-range delivery** (powered by the heart) and **diffusion for short-range cellular delivery** (driven by partial-pressure gradients). This is a fundamental design principle of all vertebrate circulatory systems.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE — Module 1, Internal Choice]`

**(a) Starting from mass conservation, derive the general convection–diffusion equation for the concentration of a chemical species $A$ in a flowing fluid. State the physical meaning of every term. (7 marks)**

*Step-by-step Model Solution:*

1. **Apply conservation of mass to a differential control volume (1.5 marks):**
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A + R_A$$

2. **Express total flux as sum of convective and diffusive contributions (1.5 marks):**
$$\vec{N}_A = C_A \vec{v} + \vec{J}_A = C_A \vec{v} - D_{AB} \nabla C_A$$

3. **Substitute into the continuity equation (1.5 marks):**
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (C_A \vec{v}) = \nabla \cdot (D_{AB} \nabla C_A) + R_A$$

4. **For constant $D_{AB}$ (1 mark):**
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla C_A = D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A + R_A$$

5. **Identify each term physically (1.5 marks):**
- $\partial C_A / \partial t$: local rate of accumulation
- $\vec{v} \cdot \nabla C_A$: convective transport
- $D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A$: diffusive transport
- $R_A$: generation/consumption (e.g., metabolic reaction)

6. **Note the special cases (0.5 mark):** No flow $\Rightarrow$ Fick's Second Law; No diffusion $\Rightarrow$ pure plug-flow reactor equation.

---

**(b) An intravenous drug bolus is injected into a large artery. Develop a 1-D convection–diffusion model for the drug concentration $C(x, t)$ in the bloodstream. State the governing equation, initial condition, and two boundary conditions suitable for an infinite vessel. (7 marks)**

*Step-by-step Model Solution:*

**Governing Equation (3 marks):**
For a 1-D vessel aligned with the $x$-axis, with constant blood velocity $v$ and constant diffusivity $D$:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + v \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Initial Condition (1.5 marks):**
At $t = 0$, the entire drug mass $M$ is concentrated at the injection point $x = 0$ (idealized as a Dirac delta):
$$C(x, 0) = M \, \delta(x)$$

**Boundary Conditions (2 marks):**
For an infinite vessel, the drug concentration vanishes far from the injection point:
$$C(-\infty, t) = 0, \quad C(+\infty, t) = 0$$

**Solution Form (0.5 mark — KTU Bonus):**
The well-known Gaussian solution is:
$$C(x, t) = \frac{M}{\sqrt{4 \pi D t}} \exp\left[ -\frac{(x - v t)^2}{4 D t} \right]$$

This solution clearly shows the drug bolus moves at velocity $v$ (convection) while spreading (diffusion).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always quote $D_{AB}$ in m²/s, flux in mol/m²·s, concentration in mol/m³. A correct equation *without units* loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Sign Errors in Fick's Law:** The negative sign in $J_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ is *crucial*. The KTU key allocates a full mark for it.
> 3. **Confusing Convection with Diffusion:** Many students write "blood carries O₂ by diffusion" — this is **wrong**. The arterial bulk transport is **convection**; only the final trans-membrane crossing is diffusion.
> 4. **Skipping Assumptions:** The KTU key for derivations awards 1–2 marks specifically for a list of assumptions. Never omit them.
> 5. **Mere Formula Memorization:** If the question asks "discuss the role of…", writing only the equation without a physiological example will earn a maximum of 50% marks. Always couple theory with a clinical/biological example (e.g., a numerical value of $Pe$ for a real vessel).
> 6. **Missing the Peclet Insight:** A complete answer to any "diffusion vs. convection" question must end with a numerical order-of-magnitude estimate of $Pe$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, Introduction**

- **Transport Phenomena = Momentum + Heat + Mass transfer** in a unified framework based on conservation laws.
- **Diffusion = molecular-scale, random-motion transport** driven by $\nabla C$. Governed by **Fick's First Law** ($J = -D \, dC/dx$) and **Fick's Second Law** ($\partial C/\partial t = D \, \partial^2 C/\partial x^2$).
- **Convection = bulk, macroscopic transport** driven by an imposed velocity field. Total flux $N_A = C_A v - D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$.
- **The Convection–Diffusion Equation** is the central PDE of this course:
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla C_A = D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A + R_A$$
- **Peclet Number** $Pe = vL/D_{AB}$ is the *master dimensionless group*; it tells you which mechanism dominates.
- **Physiological Strategy:** Long-range delivery = **convection** (heart, vessels). Final cellular delivery = **diffusion** (membranes, interstitium).
- **Key Examples:** Alveolar O₂/CO₂ exchange, intestinal nutrient absorption, drug pharmacokinetics, hemodialysis, tissue oxygenation.
- **Diffusion Coefficients (order of magnitude):** $D_{O_2,\text{air}} \sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s; $D_{O_2,\text{water}} \sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s; $D_{O_2,\text{tissue}} \sim 10^{-11}$ m²/s.
- **Permeability form** $J_s = P(C_1 - C_2)$ is the engineering form of Fick's Law across a membrane; $1/P = 1/P_m + 1/(D \cdot \varepsilon)$ for composite membranes.
- **Master three derivations** before the exam: (1) Fick's First Law from kinetic theory, (2) Fick's Second Law from mass conservation, (3) Convection–Diffusion equation from a differential balance.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — PEBMT863 — Biomedical Transport Phenomena**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Kinematics: Control Volumes, Velocity Field, Flow Rate, Acceleration, Stream Lines, Stream Tubes and Streak Lines, Conservation Relations and Boundary Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT863 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Kinematics: Control Volumes, Velocity Field, Flow Rate, Acceleration, Stream Lines, Stream Tubes and Streak Lines, Conservation Relations and Boundary Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fluid Kinematics — Formal KTU Definition

**Fluid Kinematics** is the branch of fluid mechanics that describes the motion of fluids *without reference to the forces causing the motion*. It treats fluids as continuous media (continuum assumption) and characterizes motion through geometric and temporal descriptors such as velocity fields, streamlines, pathlines, streaklines, and flow rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Kinematics precedes *Kinetics* (force analysis). Until the velocity field $\vec{V}(x,y,z,t)$ is known, the Navier-Stokes and continuity equations cannot be applied. Thus, Module 1 forms the **logical foundation** for all subsequent momentum, energy, and mass transport analyses in biomedical systems (blood flow, airway flow, dialysate flow).

---

## 1.2 Lagrangian vs. Eulerian Descriptions

There are two complementary mathematical frameworks used to describe fluid motion:

| Framework | Variable Tracked | Observation Point | Use in Biomedical Context |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **Lagrangian** | Individual fluid particle | Follows a tagged particle (e.g., one RBC) | Tracking a drug bolus through a vein |
| **Eulerian** | Field variables at fixed points | Fixed coordinate $(x,y,z)$ in space | Doppler ultrasound velocity mapping |

The **Eulerian approach** is dominant in transport phenomena, because in clinical/engineering systems we measure properties at *spatial locations* (e.g., pressure catheter, flow probe), not by chasing individual red blood cells.

---

## 1.3 Control Volume (CV) — The Heart of Transport Analysis

A **Control Volume (CV)** is a *fixed* (or moving) region in space through which fluid flows. It is bounded by a **Control Surface (CS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Analogy — The "Window" Concept:**
> Imagine standing by a river with a fishing net shaped like a cube. Water, fish, leaves, and sediment flow *through* the net. The net itself does not move — but you can count how much water (mass), how many fish (momentum), and how much salt (species) enter and leave per second. The net is your **Control Volume**. The mesh is your **Control Surface**.
> In biomedical engineering: the control volume might be a section of artery, a dialysis cartridge, or a section of the lung alveolus.

**System vs. Control Volume:**

- A **System** is a *fixed mass* of fluid (Lagrangian).
- A **Control Volume** is a *fixed region* in space (Eulerian).

These are linked mathematically by the **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)**.

---

## 1.4 Velocity Field

The **velocity field** assigns a velocity vector to every point in a flow domain:

$$\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t) = u\,\hat{i} + v\,\hat{j} + w\,\hat{k}$$

where $u, v, w$ are the components along the $x, y, z$ axes respectively.

> [!TIP]
> **Biomedical Insight:**
> In Poiseuille flow through a blood vessel, the velocity field is purely axial: $\vec{V} = u(r)\,\hat{k}$, where $u(r)$ is a parabolic function of the radial coordinate. In contrast, flow in the aortic arch is fully three-dimensional with secondary (Dean) vortices.

---

## 1.5 Streamlines, Pathlines, and Streaklines — The Geometric Trio

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Walking in Snow:**
> - **Streamline** = the *direction of the wind* at this instant (a snapshot of the velocity field).
> - **Pathline** = the *trajectory* of a single snowflake carried by the wind.
> - **Streakline** = the *continuous line* of dye released from a point — like ink dropped into a stream.

| Line Type | Definition | Mathematical Condition |
|-----------|------------|------------------------|
| **Streamline** | Curve instantaneously tangent to $\vec{V}$ | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ |
| **Pathline** | Trajectory of a single fluid particle | $\dfrac{d\vec{r}_p}{dt} = \vec{V}(\vec{r}_p, t)$ |
| **Streakline** | Locus of all particles that previously passed through a fixed point | Built by integrating over past times |

In a **steady flow** ($\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$), all three lines coincide.

---

## 1.6 Stream Tube

A **stream tube** is the tubular surface formed by all streamlines passing through a closed curve. By definition, **no fluid crosses the stream tube surface** — fluid inside stays inside.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A stream tube is the *kinematic* precursor of a *streamline of flow rate* used later in deriving Bernoulli's equation and conservation of mass for a 1-D flow.

---

## 1.7 Flow Rate and Conservation Relations (Preview)

- **Volumetric flow rate:** $Q = \int_{CS} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{A}$
- **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \int_{CS} \rho\,\vec{V} \cdot d\vec{A}$
- **Conservation of mass (continuity):** $\dfrac{d}{dt}\int_{CV}\rho\,dV + \int_{CS}\rho\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A} = 0$

---

## 1.8 Boundary Conditions (Preview)

The two fundamental kinematic boundary conditions are:

1. **No-penetration:** $\vec{V}_{\text{fluid}} \cdot \hat{n} = \vec{V}_{\text{wall}} \cdot \hat{n}$
2. **No-slip:** $\vec{V}_{\text{fluid}} \cdot \hat{t} = \vec{V}_{\text{wall}} \cdot \hat{t}$ at solid walls

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly state "no-slip means velocity = 0 at the wall." This is true *only* for a **stationary wall**. For a moving wall (e.g., a contracting heart chamber or a peristaltic pump diaphragm), the fluid velocity must equal the wall velocity tangentially.

---

## 1.9 Acceleration Field

The **acceleration** experienced by a fluid particle is *not* simply $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$. It is the **material (substantial) derivative**:

$$\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local (unsteady)}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{Convective (advective)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Biomedical Intuition:** When a blood vessel suddenly narrows (stenosis), the local acceleration is large near the throat. In the aorta during systole, both terms are non-negligible. In fully developed steady flow, the local term vanishes, but the convective term persists in curved vessels.

---

## 1.10 Module-Level Connection Map

| Concept | Role in Transport Phenomena |
|---------|------------------------------|
| Velocity field | Input to momentum, energy, mass equations |
| Control volume | Framework for all conservation laws |
| Streamlines | Foundation for Bernoulli's equation (Module 2) |
| Material derivative | Connects Eulerian to Lagrangian physics |
| Boundary conditions | Required to close any PDE formulation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eulerian Velocity Field — A Field-Theoretic View

The continuum hypothesis allows us to treat the fluid as a continuous distribution of matter. Every macroscopic property is a function of space and time:

$$\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x,y,z,t), \quad p = p(x,y,z,t), \quad \rho = \rho(x,y,z,t), \quad T = T(x,y,z,t)$$

The collection of all such fields at a given instant is called the **flow field**.

### 2.1.1 Steady vs. Unsteady Flow

$$\text{Steady: } \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0 \quad \text{at every point}$$

$$\text{Unsteady: } \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} \neq 0 \quad \text{at some point}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steadiness is a property of the field at a point, not of the particle.** A flow can be unsteady (e.g., pulsatile arterial flow) yet still be *periodic*.

### 2.1.2 Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Flow

$$\text{Uniform: } \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0 \quad \text{(no spatial variation along streamline direction } s\text{)}$$

### 2.1.3 1-D, 2-D, 3-D Flow

- **1-D:** $\vec{V}$ varies in magnitude only along one coordinate (e.g., Poiseuille flow $u(r)$).
- **2-D:** Two non-zero components, no variation in the third (e.g., flow in a planar contraction).
- **3-D:** All three components non-zero (most physiological flows).

---

## 2.2 Flow Rate — Rigorous Definitions

### 2.2.1 Volumetric Flow Rate

The rate at which fluid volume crosses a surface:

$$Q = \int_{A} \vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A}$$

where $d\vec{A} = \hat{n}\,dA$ is the outward-pointing area element.

**Sign convention:** Flow leaving the CV is *positive*; flow entering is *negative* (or vice versa — be consistent).

### 2.2.2 Mass Flow Rate

$$\dot{m} = \int_{A} \rho\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A}$$

For a **uniform** velocity and density over a cross-section:

$$Q = V_{avg}\,A, \qquad \dot{m} = \rho\,V_{avg}\,A$$

### 2.2.3 Average Velocity

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{A}\int_{A} \vec{V}\cdot\hat{n}\,dA$$

> [!TIP]
> **Biomedical Note:** Cardiac output (CO) is the volumetric flow rate from the left ventricle, typically $Q \approx 5\text{ L/min}$ at rest. The average velocity in the aorta (diameter ~ 2.5 cm) is approximately $V_{avg} = Q/A \approx 0.17$ m/s.

---

## 2.3 Acceleration — The Material Derivative

### 2.3.1 Full Mathematical Statement

For a particle at position $\vec{r}(t)$, the velocity is $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(\vec{r}(t), t)$.

Applying the chain rule of multivariable calculus:

$$\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x}\dfrac{dx}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y}\dfrac{dy}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}\dfrac{dz}{dt}$$

Since $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, $dz/dt = w$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (u\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z})\vec{V} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}}$$

### 2.3.2 Physical Interpretation of the Two Terms

| Term | Name | Origin | Magnitude Significance |
|------|------|--------|------------------------|
| $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$ | Local acceleration | Unsteadiness at a *fixed point* | Dominant near valves, in pulse waves |
| $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ | Convective acceleration | Particle moving to a *new location* with different velocity | Dominant in converging/diverging geometries (e.g., stenosis) |

### 2.3.3 Vector Identity (Useful in Module 2)

$$(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} = \nabla\left(\dfrac{\vert \vec{V} \vert^2}{2}\right) - \vec{V}\times(\nabla\times\vec{V}) = \nabla\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) + \vec{\omega}\times\vec{V}$$

where $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ is the **vorticity**. This identity is the bridge to Bernoulli's equation.

---

## 2.4 Streamlines — Mathematical Formulation

A streamline is defined by the property that the velocity vector is everywhere tangent to the line. If $d\vec{r} = (dx, dy, dz)$ is a differential displacement along the streamline:

$$\dfrac{dx}{u(x,y,z,t)} = \dfrac{dy}{v(x,y,z,t)} = \dfrac{dz}{w(x,y,z,t)}$$

This ratio is evaluated at a *single instant in time* — the streamline is a snapshot.

For a 2-D flow $(u, v)$ in the $x$-$y$ plane, streamlines satisfy:

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{v(x,y,t)}{u(x,y,t)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Streamlines cannot cross in a continuous velocity field.** If they did, the velocity would have two different directions at the intersection, violating uniqueness.

### 2.4.1 Stream Function $\psi(x,y,t)$

For an **incompressible** 2-D flow, the continuity equation $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$ guarantees the existence of a scalar stream function:

$$u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

Lines of constant $\psi$ are **streamlines**. The volumetric flow rate per unit depth between two streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ is $\Delta \psi = \psi_2 - \psi_1$.

---

## 2.5 Pathlines — Mathematical Formulation

A pathline is the trajectory $\vec{r}_p(t)$ of a single particle:

$$\dfrac{d\vec{r}_p}{dt} = \vec{V}(\vec{r}_p, t), \qquad \vec{r}_p(t_0) = \vec{r}_0$$

This is an **ordinary differential equation (ODE)** system that can be integrated given an initial condition.

---

## 2.6 Streaklines — Mathematical Formulation

A streakline consists of all particles that passed through a *fixed* emitter point $\vec{r}_0$ at some past time $\tau \leq t$. Each such particle has a trajectory:

$$\vec{r}_p(\tau \to t) = \text{solution of pathline ODE starting at } \tau \text{ from } \vec{r}_0$$

The streakline is the set of all such endpoints at time $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady flow identity:** If $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$, then streamlines = pathlines = streaklines. This is a key KTU short-answer question.

---

## 2.7 Stream Tubes and Control Volume

A **stream tube** is bounded by a stream-surface (a surface generated by streamlines passing through a closed curve). Its key property:

$$\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n}_{ST} = 0 \quad \text{on stream tube surface}$$

Thus, a stream tube is a **natural control surface** for mass flow — no mass crosses it.

**Volumetric flow rate** through any cross-section of a stream tube is constant:

$$Q_1 = Q_2 \quad \text{(along a stream tube)}$$

---

## 2.8 Conservation Relations (Kinematic Form)

### 2.8.1 Conservation of Mass — The Continuity Equation

For a **fixed control volume**, conservation of mass gives:

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho\,dV + \int_{CS} \rho\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A} = 0$$

Using the **divergence theorem** $\int_{CS} \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{A} = \int_{CV} \nabla\cdot\vec{F}\,dV$:

$$\int_{CV} \left[\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V})\right]dV = 0$$

Since the CV is arbitrary, the integrand must vanish pointwise:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V}) = 0}$$

Expanding $\nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V}) = \rho(\nabla\cdot\vec{V}) + \vec{V}\cdot\nabla\rho$:

$$\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho\,\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$$

**Special case — Incompressible flow** ($\rho = $ const, $D\rho/Dt = 0$):

$$\boxed{\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0}$$

In Cartesian: $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y + \partial w/\partial z = 0$.

In cylindrical: $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r u_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial u_z}{\partial z} = 0$

### 2.8.2 Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)

For any extensive property $B$ (mass, momentum, energy, species) with intensive density $b$ (per unit mass: $B = \int \rho b\,dV$):

$$\dfrac{dB_{system}}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}\int_{CV}\rho b\,dV + \int_{CS}\rho b\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **RTT is the master theorem** that converts a Lagrangian statement (rate of change of a *system* property) into an Eulerian statement (rates of change *inside* a CV + flux *across* the CS). It is the gateway to the momentum, energy, and species conservation equations of Module 2.

---

## 2.9 Boundary Conditions — Complete Kinematic Set

| Condition | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Form | Example |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|---------|
| **No-penetration** | Fluid does not enter a solid | $\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n} = \vec{V}_{wall}\cdot\hat{n}$ at solid surface | Blood flow over plaque |
| **No-slip** | Tangential fluid velocity = wall velocity | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}_{wall}$ at solid surface | Womersley flow in artery |
| **No-slip on stationary wall** | Special case | $\vec{V} = 0$ at fixed wall | Pipe Poiseuille flow |
| **Symmetry** | Field is mirror-symmetric | $\partial \vec{V}/\partial \hat{n}_{sym} = 0$ | Centerline of circular tube |
| **Free surface** | Shearless surface, fixed pressure | $\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n} = 0$, $\mu\,\partial \vec{V}_t/\partial \hat{n} = 0$ | Airway mucus layer (idealized) |
| **Interface** | Continuity of normal velocity across a discontinuity | $\vec{V}_1\cdot\hat{n} = \vec{V}_2\cdot\hat{n}$ | Blood-plasma interface (slippery RBCs) |
| **Inlet** | Specified velocity profile | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}_{inlet}(y,z)$ at inlet plane | Catheter entry flow |
| **Outlet** | Fully developed / zero gradient | $\partial \vec{V}/\partial x = 0$ at exit | Long vessel exit |

---

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table before attempting any Module 1 problem.**

| # | Equation | Name | Conditions |
|---|----------|------|------------|
| 1 | $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$ | Velocity field | General |
| 2 | $Q = \int_A \vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A}$ | Volumetric flow rate | General |
| 3 | $\dot{m} = \int_A \rho \vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A}$ | Mass flow rate | General |
| 4 | $V_{avg} = Q/A$ | Average velocity | Uniform $\rho$ |
| 5 | $a = DV/Dt = \partial V/\partial t + (V\cdot\nabla)V$ | Material derivative | General |
| 6 | $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$ | Streamline | At fixed $t$ |
| 7 | $dr_p/dt = V(r_p, t)$ | Pathline ODE | Lagrangian |
| 8 | $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y + \partial w/\partial z = 0$ | Continuity (incomp., Cart.) | $\rho = $ const |
| 9 | $(1/r)\partial(r u_r)/\partial r + (1/r)\partial u_\theta/\partial \theta + \partial u_z/\partial z = 0$ | Continuity (incomp., cyl.) | $\rho = $ const, cylindrical |
| 10 | $\partial \rho/\partial t + \nabla\cdot(\rho V) = 0$ | Continuity (general) | Compressible OK |
| 11 | $D\rho/Dt + \rho\,\nabla\cdot V = 0$ | Continuity (material form) | General |
| 12 | $dB_{sys}/dt = d/dt\int_{CV}\rho b\,dV + \int_{CS}\rho b\,V\cdot dA$ | Reynolds Transport Theorem | General |
| 13 | $V_f \cdot n = V_{wall} \cdot n$ | No-penetration | Solid wall |
| 14 | $V_f = V_{wall}$ | No-slip | Solid wall (viscous) |
| 15 | $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | Stream function (2-D incomp.) | $\nabla\cdot V = 0$ |
| 16 | $(V\cdot\nabla)V = \nabla(V^2/2) - V\times(\nabla\times V)$ | Convective identity | General |
| 17 | $V\cdot\hat{n}_{ST} = 0$ | Stream tube property | Kinematic |
| 18 | $Q_1 = Q_2$ along stream tube | Stream tube mass conservation | Steady, incomp. |
| 19 | $\nabla\times V = 0$ | Irrotational flow | Potential flow |
| 20 | $\nabla\times V = 2\vec{\omega}$ | Vorticity definition | General |

---

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Real-World Application |
|---------|------------------------|
| Control volume | Cardiac output measurement, kidney clearance, mass balance on a dialysis unit |
| Velocity field | Doppler ultrasound, MRI velocimetry, CFD in stent design |
| Material derivative | Computing wall shear stress in curved vessels (aortic arch) |
| Streamlines | Visualizing airflow in airways for asthma drug delivery |
| Stream function | Efficient 2-D flow analysis around a vena cava contraction |
| No-slip | Origin of *Fåhræus–Lindqvist effect* in microvessels |
| Continuity | Calculating peak velocity from vessel diameter (Bernoulli coupling) |
| RTT | Foundation of Navier–Stokes, energy, and species equations (Module 2) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Material (Substantial) Derivative

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a fluid particle whose position is $\vec{r}(t) = (x(t), y(t), z(t))$. Its velocity is the time-rate of change of position:

$$\vec{V} = \dfrac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$$

Now, the velocity field is an Eulerian object: $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$. The particle samples the field along its trajectory, so:

$$\vec{V}_{\text{particle}}(t) = \vec{V}(x(t), y(t), z(t), t)$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Differentiation

Taking the time derivative of both sides using the **chain rule for multivariable functions**:

$$\dfrac{d\vec{V}_{\text{particle}}}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}\,\vec{V}\big(x(t), y(t), z(t), t\big)$$

$$\dfrac{d\vec{V}_{\text{particle}}}{dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x}\dfrac{dx}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y}\dfrac{dy}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}\dfrac{dz}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}\cdot\dfrac{\partial t}{\partial t}$$

### 3.1.3 Identifying Each Term

Recognize that $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, $dz/dt = w$ are the *particle's* velocity components (which equal the *Eulerian* velocity at the particle's instantaneous location):

$$\dfrac{d\vec{V}_{\text{particle}}}{dt} = u\,\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x} + v\,\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y} + w\,\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$$

### 3.1.4 Compact Vector Form

Define the **convective operator** as:

$$(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla) = u\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} \equiv \dfrac{d\vec{V}_{\text{particle}}}{dt} = \boxed{\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **defining identity** of transport phenomena. Every momentum, energy, and species conservation equation in Module 2 begins with $D/Dt$ applied to velocity, temperature, or concentration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Continuity Equation

### 3.2.1 Integral Form (RTT for Mass)

Conservation of mass for a *system* (fixed mass):

$$\dfrac{d m_{sys}}{dt} = 0$$

By the Reynolds Transport Theorem, with $b = 1$ (mass per unit mass):

$$0 = \dfrac{d}{dt}\int_{CV}\rho\,dV + \int_{CS}\rho\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A}$$

This is the **integral form of the continuity equation**.

### 3.2.2 Differential Form — Step 1: Apply the Divergence Theorem

Convert the surface integral to a volume integral:

$$\int_{CS}\rho\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A} = \int_{CV}\nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V})\,dV$$

Substituting back:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\int_{CV}\rho\,dV + \int_{CV}\nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V})\,dV = 0$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2: Bring the Time Derivative Inside (for a Fixed CV)

For a CV that is *fixed* in space, the time derivative commutes with the volume integral:

$$\int_{CV}\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\,dV + \int_{CV}\nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V})\,dV = 0$$

$$\int_{CV}\left[\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V})\right]dV = 0$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3: Arbitrariness of the CV

Since the integrand is continuous and the CV is **arbitrary**, the integrand must vanish at every point:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V}) = 0}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4: Material Form

Expanding $\nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V}) = \rho\,\nabla\cdot\vec{V} + \vec{V}\cdot\nabla\rho$:

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{V}\cdot\nabla\rho + \rho\,\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$$

Recognize the first two terms as $D\rho/Dt$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho\,\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0}$$

### 3.2.6 Incompressible Special Case

If $\rho$ = constant (e.g., water, dilute aqueous solutions, blood plasma at low shear):

$$\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0}$$

This is the **most-cited continuity equation in physiology** (blood plasma treated as incompressible).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Streamline Equation

### 3.3.1 Tangency Condition

A streamline is by definition tangent to $\vec{V}$. If $d\vec{r}$ is a differential vector along the streamline, then $d\vec{r}$ is parallel to $\vec{V}$:

$$d\vec{r} \times \vec{V} = \vec{0}$$

In Cartesian components:

$$(dx, dy, dz) \times (u, v, w) = \vec{0}$$

### 3.3.2 Cross Product Expansion

$$\begin{aligned}
(dx)(w) - (dz)(v) &= 0 \\
(dz)(u) - (dx)(w) &= 0 \\
(dx)(v) - (dy)(u) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.3 Rearrangement

Each equation reduces to the chain of ratios:

$$dx\,w = dz\,v, \quad dz\,u = dx\,w, \quad dx\,v = dy\,u$$

Dividing pairwise:

$$dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note carefully:** This is a *single-instant* relation. The time $t$ is **frozen** when integrating a streamline. For 2-D, integrating $dy/dx = v/u$ at fixed $t$ gives the streamline.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Stream Function (2-D Incompressible)

### 3.4.1 Starting Point

For 2-D incompressible flow, $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$.

### 3.4.2 Introduce a Scalar $\psi(x,y,t)$

We *postulate* the existence of a scalar field $\psi$ such that:

$$u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

### 3.4.3 Verify Continuity

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \dfrac{\partial^2\psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} - \dfrac{\partial^2\psi}{\partial y\,\partial x} = 0$$

The mixed partials are equal (assuming smoothness of $\psi$), so continuity is automatically satisfied. ✓

### 3.4.4 Show Streamlines = Iso-$\psi$ Lines

Along a streamline at fixed time: $dy/dx = v/u$. Compute the gradient of $\psi$:

$$d\psi = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}dx + \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}dy = -v\,dx + u\,dy$$

Set $d\psi = 0$ for constant $\psi$:

$$u\,dy - v\,dx = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{v}{u} \quad \checkmark$$

This matches the streamline equation. Hence **streamlines are isolines of $\psi$**.

### 3.4.5 Volumetric Flow Rate Between Two Streamlines

The flow rate per unit depth between streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$:

$$Q' = \int_{\psi_1}^{\psi_2} d\psi = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

> [!TIP]
> This makes $\psi$ an excellent diagnostic quantity in CFD post-processing of biomedical flows.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Steady Planar Flow with $u = ax$, $v = -ay$

**Verify continuity, find streamlines, and the velocity potential.**

### 3.5.1 Verify Continuity (Incompressible)

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = a + (-a) = 0 \quad\checkmark$$

### 3.5.2 Streamlines

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{-ay}{ax} = -\dfrac{y}{x}$$

Separate variables:

$$\dfrac{dy}{y} = -\dfrac{dx}{x}$$

Integrate:

$$\ln\vert y\vert = -\ln\vert x\vert + C \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \ln\vert xy\vert = C$$

$$\boxed{xy = K \quad (\text{family of rectangular hyperbolas})}$$

### 3.5.3 Stream Function

Find $\psi$:

$$u = \dfrac{\partial\psi}{\partial y} = ax \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \psi = axy + f(x)$$

$$v = -\dfrac{\partial\psi}{\partial x} = -ay - f'(x) = -ay \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f'(x) = 0$$

Hence $\psi = axy$. Streamlines are $\psi = axy = $ const $\Rightarrow xy = $ const ✓

### 3.5.4 Physical Interpretation

This is a **stagnation-point flow** or **2-D linear stretching flow** (hyperbolic streamlines) — a model for flow near a vessel bifurcation or a 90° saddle point in a 2-D channel.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Material Derivative in a Converging Nozzle

A nozzle has axial velocity $u(x) = U_0(1 + x/L)$ (linear acceleration), $v = w = 0$, steady flow.

**Compute the axial acceleration of a fluid particle.**

### 3.6.1 Identify the Terms

- Local acceleration: $\partial u/\partial t = 0$ (steady)
- Convective term: $u\,\partial u/\partial x$

### 3.6.2 Compute Convective Term

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{U_0}{L}$$

$$u\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = U_0\left(1 + \dfrac{x}{L}\right)\cdot\dfrac{U_0}{L} = \dfrac{U_0^2}{L}\left(1 + \dfrac{x}{L}\right)$$

### 3.6.3 Total Acceleration

$$a_x = \dfrac{Du}{Dt} = 0 + \dfrac{U_0^2}{L}\left(1 + \dfrac{x}{L}\right) = \boxed{\dfrac{U_0^2}{L}\left(1 + \dfrac{x}{L}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows that even in *steady* flow, fluid particles *do* accelerate (convective acceleration). This is the fundamental difference between local and material time derivatives — a favorite KTU conceptual question.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: Mass Flow Through a Bifurcating Artery

The common carotid artery (inlet) has diameter $D_1 = 8$ mm with mean velocity $V_1 = 0.4$ m/s. It splits into internal and external carotid branches, each with diameter $D_2 = D_3 = 5$ mm.

**Find the average velocity in each branch assuming equal division.**

### 3.7.1 Continuity

$$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 + \dot{m}_3$$

For incompressible flow with equal branch division:

$$V_2 A_2 = V_3 A_3 = \dfrac{1}{2}V_1 A_1$$

### 3.7.2 Compute Areas

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.008)^2}{4} = 5.027\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2$$

$$A_2 = A_3 = \dfrac{\pi (0.005)^2}{4} = 1.963\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2$$

### 3.7.3 Compute Inlet Flow Rate

$$Q_1 = V_1 A_1 = 0.4 \times 5.027\times 10^{-5} = 2.011\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

### 3.7.4 Branch Flow Rate

$$Q_2 = Q_3 = Q_1/2 = 1.005\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

### 3.7.5 Branch Velocity

$$V_2 = V_3 = \dfrac{Q_2}{A_2} = \dfrac{1.005\times 10^{-5}}{1.963\times 10^{-5}} = \boxed{0.512\,\text{m/s}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Clinical Insight:** The branch velocity is *higher* than the parent vessel despite smaller diameter — the velocity *increases* because the area ratio (1.96:1) more than compensates for the flow split (2:1). This is the basis for *carotid Doppler* stenosis screening.

---

## 3.8 Worked Example: Rotating Flow — Vorticity

For the 2-D flow $u = -\Omega y$, $v = \Omega x$ (solid-body rotation at angular velocity $\Omega$).

**Compute vorticity and discuss the stream function.**

### 3.8.1 Vorticity (z-component)

$$\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \Omega - (-\Omega) = 2\Omega$$

So $\vec{\omega} = 2\Omega\,\hat{k}$ — constant vorticity, as expected for solid-body rotation.

### 3.8.2 Stream Function

Integrate $u = \partial\psi/\partial y = -\Omega y$:

$$\psi = -\dfrac{\Omega y^2}{2} + g(x)$$

Then $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x = -g'(x) = \Omega x$:

$$g'(x) = -\Omega x \quad\Longrightarrow\quad g(x) = -\dfrac{\Omega x^2}{2} + C$$

$$\boxed{\psi = -\dfrac{\Omega}{2}(x^2 + y^2) + C}$$

Streamlines are circles $x^2 + y^2 = $ const. ✓

### 3.8.3 Irrotational vs. Rotational

This flow has $\omega_z = 2\Omega \neq 0$ (rotational). In contrast, a *free vortex* $u_\theta = K/r$ has $\omega_z = 0$ everywhere except at the origin (irrotational outside the core).

---

## 3.9 Summary of Derivations

| # | Result | Method | Reference Section |
|---|--------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | $DV/Dt = \partial V/\partial t + (V\cdot\nabla)V$ | Chain rule | §3.1 |
| 2 | $\partial\rho/\partial t + \nabla\cdot(\rho V) = 0$ | RTT + divergence theorem | §3.2 |
| 3 | $\nabla\cdot V = 0$ (incomp.) | Setting $D\rho/Dt = 0$ | §3.2.6 |
| 4 | $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$ | Cross product tangency | §3.3 |
| 5 | $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | Vector identity ansatz | §3.4 |
