# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish clearly between the Lagrangian and Eulerian descriptions of fluid motion. Give one biomedical example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Lagrangian | Eulerian |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| **Tracking** | Individual fluid particles | Field variables at fixed spatial points |
| **Variable** | Properties as function of *particle label* | Properties as function of $(x,y,z,t)$ |
| **Mathematical object** | Ordinary differential equation (ODE) for trajectory | Partial differential equation (PDE) for field |
| **Biomedical example** | Tracking a single RBC through a capillary network | Measuring velocity at a fixed point in the aorta with a Doppler probe |

*(1 mark for clear distinction of tracking; 1 mark for biomedical example; 1 mark for stating the Eulerian is preferred in transport analysis.)*

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define: (i) streamline, (ii) streakline, (iii) pathline. Under what condition do all three coincide?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(i) Streamline** (1 mark): A curve that is everywhere instantaneously tangent to the velocity vector field. Mathematically, $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$ at a *fixed instant* $t$.

- **(ii) Streakline** (1 mark): The locus of all fluid particles that have *previously* passed through a given fixed point in the flow field. It is the "ink-line" produced by continuous dye injection.

- **(iii) Pathline** (1 mark): The actual trajectory of a single marked fluid particle, given by integrating $d\vec{r}_p/dt = \vec{V}(\vec{r}_p, t)$ from an initial position.

**Coincidence condition:** All three coincide in **steady flow** ($\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **continuity equation** in differential form for a compressible fluid, starting from conservation of mass applied to a fixed control volume. State clearly the assumptions made. Hence deduce the special form for an **incompressible** fluid.

**(b) [7 marks]** A blood vessel of cross-sectional area $A_1 = 2\,\text{cm}^2$ carries a steady flow with mean velocity $V_1 = 30\,\text{cm/s}$. Downstream, the vessel narrows to $A_2 = 1\,\text{cm}^2$ (a stenosis). Assuming the blood behaves as an incompressible Newtonian fluid:
- (i) Calculate the volumetric flow rate at section 1.
- (ii) Compute the mean velocity at section 2.
- (iii) Find the mass flow rate if blood density is $\rho = 1060\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Set up mass balance for a fixed CV.**

The mass inside a fixed CV is $m = \int_{CV}\rho\,dV$. The net mass efflux across the CS is $\int_{CS}\rho\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A}$. Conservation of mass requires:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\int_{CV}\rho\,dV = -\int_{CS}\rho\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A}$$

**Step 2: Bring time derivative inside (fixed CV).**

For a *fixed* control volume, the integration domain does not depend on time, so:

$$\int_{CV}\dfrac{\partial\rho}{\partial t}\,dV = -\int_{CS}\rho\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A}$$

**Step 3: Apply the divergence theorem to the surface integral.**

$$\int_{CS}\rho\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A} = \int_{CV}\nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V})\,dV$$

**Step 4: Combine and invoke arbitrariness of the CV.**

$$\int_{CV}\left[\dfrac{\partial\rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V})\right]dV = 0$$

Since the CV is **arbitrary**, the integrand vanishes at every point:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial\rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V}) = 0}$$

**Step 5: Assumptions made** (state at least two for full marks):
- The continuum hypothesis holds.
- No mass sources or sinks inside the CV (no nuclear reactions, no phase change).
- The control surface is closed.
- The fluid is a single phase.

**Step 6: Incompressible special case.**

If $\rho = $ const in space and time, then $\partial\rho/\partial t = 0$ and $\nabla\rho = 0$, so:

$$\nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V}) = \rho\,\nabla\cdot\vec{V} + \vec{V}\cdot\nabla\rho = \rho\,\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$$

Dividing by $\rho$:

$$\boxed{\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0}$$

In Cartesian coordinates: $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y + \partial w/\partial z = 0$.

*(Award 1 mark each for Steps 1–5, and 2 marks for the incompressible deduction and final boxed form.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Volumetric flow rate at section 1:**

$$Q_1 = V_1 A_1 = 0.30\,\text{m/s} \times 2\times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2 = \boxed{6\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}}$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Mean velocity at section 2** (using incompressible continuity $Q_1 = Q_2$):

$$V_2 = \dfrac{Q_2}{A_2} = \dfrac{6\times 10^{-5}}{1\times 10^{-4}} = \boxed{0.6\,\text{m/s}}$$

*(3 marks)*

**(iii) Mass flow rate** (constant for incompressible, single-inlet, single-outlet flow):

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q_1 = 1060 \times 6\times 10^{-5} = \boxed{0.0636\,\text{kg/s}}$$

*(2 marks)*

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Starting from first principles, derive the **material (substantial) derivative** of the velocity vector. Hence obtain the expressions for the **local** and **convective** acceleration components. Provide one biomedical example where each term dominates.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the 2-D velocity field:
$$\vec{V} = (3x + 2y)\hat{i} + (2x - 3y)\hat{j}$$
- (i) Verify that the flow satisfies the incompressible continuity equation.
- (ii) Determine the equation of the streamline passing through the point $(1, 1)$ at the instant $t = 0$.
- (iii) Compute the material (substantial) derivative of velocity at the point $(2, 1)$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Identify the particle's velocity as a function of time.**

For a fluid particle at $\vec{r}(t) = (x(t), y(t), z(t))$, the velocity is:

$$\vec{V}_p(t) = \vec{V}(x(t), y(t), z(t), t)$$

**Step 2: Apply the chain rule.**

$$\dfrac{d\vec{V}_p}{dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x}\dfrac{dx}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y}\dfrac{dy}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}\dfrac{dz}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Substitute particle kinematics.**

Since $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, $dz/dt = w$:

$$\dfrac{d\vec{V}_p}{dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}$$

**Step 4: Identify the two physical terms.**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{local (unsteady)}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{convective}}}$$

**Local acceleration** (3 marks): Arises from unsteadiness at a fixed spatial point. Dominates in **pulsatile arterial flow** during the rapid systolic upstroke, where the velocity at a fixed point in the aorta changes sharply with time.

**Convective acceleration** (4 marks): Arises from a particle moving into a region of different velocity. Dominates in **flow through a stenosis** or **aortic arch curvature**, where even in a steady mean flow, fluid particles experience acceleration as they traverse the geometry.

*(Award marks as: Step 1 = 1, Step 2 = 1, Step 3 = 1, Step 4 + interpretation + examples = 4.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Verify incompressible continuity:**

$$\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 3 + (-3) = 0 \quad\checkmark$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Streamline equation:**

At a fixed instant, $dy/dx = v/u$:

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{2x - 3y}{3x + 2y}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$(3x + 2y)\,dy = (2x - 3y)\,dx$$

$$3x\,dy + 2y\,dy = 2x\,dx - 3y\,dx$$

$$3(x\,dy + y\,dx) = 2x\,dx - 2y\,dy$$

$$3\,d(xy) = 2(x\,dx - y\,dy) = d(x^2 - y^2)$$

Integrate:

$$3xy = x^2 - y^2 + C$$

Apply initial condition $(x, y) = (1, 1)$:

$$3(1)(1) = (1)^2 - (1)^2 + C \;\Rightarrow\; 3 = 0 + C \;\Rightarrow\; C = 3$$

$$\boxed{x^2 - y^2 - 3xy + 3 = 0}$$

*(3 marks)*

**(iii) Material derivative at $(2, 1)$:**

Compute the velocity gradient matrix:

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 3, \quad \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2, \quad \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 2, \quad \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -3$$

At $(x, y) = (2, 1)$:

$$u = 3(2) + 2(1) = 8, \quad v = 2(2) - 3(1) = 1$$

Local accelerations: $\partial u/\partial t = 0$, $\partial v/\partial t = 0$ (steady flow).

Convective acceleration x-component:

$$a_x = u\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = (8)(3) + (1)(2) = 26$$

Convective acceleration y-component:

$$a_y = u\,\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\,\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = (8)(2) + (1)(-3) = 13$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}\bigg|_{(2,1)} = 26\,\hat{i} + 13\,\hat{j} \quad \text{units: m/s}^2}$$

*(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 1:**
> 1. **Material derivative confusion:** Students often write $a = \partial V/\partial t$ and forget the convective term. For full marks, *both* terms must appear with explicit justification.
> 2. **Continuity equation signs:** Some students write $\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$ as the *general* form, missing the fact that the general form includes the $\partial\rho/\partial t$ term. Be explicit about assumptions.
> 3. **Streamline ≠ Pathline:** In unsteady flow, they differ. Examiners will deduct marks for stating they are always identical.
> 4. **Boundary condition at moving wall:** Writing "$\vec{V} = 0$ at the wall" is only valid for *stationary* walls. For peristaltic pumps and contracting ventricles, the no-slip condition is $\vec{V}_{fluid} = \vec{V}_{wall}$.
> 5. **RTT missing the time derivative of CV contents:** When the CV is *deforming* (e.g., a piston cylinder), RTT has an *additional* term accounting for the motion of the CS. In this course, we restrict to *fixed* CVs unless stated.
> 6. **Unit consistency:** Use SI throughout — m, s, kg, Pa. Mixing cm with m loses ½ mark per question on average.
> 7. **Stream function validity:** $\psi$ exists *only* for 2-D incompressible flow. Don't apply it to compressible or 3-D flow.
> 8. **Average vs. local velocity:** $V_{avg}$ is the *spatial average* over a cross-section, not the centerline velocity. For Poiseuille flow, $V_{avg} = u_{max}/2$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Fluid Kinematics**

### A. Conceptual Anchors
- [ ] **Lagrangian** = track particle; **Eulerian** = track field point.
- [ ] **System** = fixed mass; **Control Volume** = fixed region.
- [ ] **Streamline** = snapshot of velocity direction; **Pathline** = particle trajectory; **Streakline** = dye locus.
- [ ] All three coincide *only* in **steady flow**.
- [ ] **Stream tube** = no mass crosses its surface; $Q$ is constant along it.
- [ ] **No-slip** = $V_{fluid} = V_{wall}$ (tangential). **No-penetration** = normal components match.

### B. Essential Equations to Memorize
- [ ] Material derivative: $D\vec{V}/Dt = \partial\vec{V}/\partial t + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$
- [ ] Continuity (general): $\partial\rho/\partial t + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V}) = 0$
- [ ] Continuity (incompressible): $\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$
- [ ] RTT: $\dfrac{dB_{sys}}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}\int_{CV}\rho b\,dV + \int_{CS}\rho b\,\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A}$
- [ ] Streamline: $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$ (at fixed $t$)
- [ ] Pathline ODE: $d\vec{r}_p/dt = \vec{V}(\vec{r}_p, t)$
- [ ] Stream function: $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ (2-D, incomp.)
- [ ] Vorticity: $\vec{\omega} = \nabla\times\vec{V}$

### C. Important Vector Identities
- [ ] $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} = \nabla(V^2/2) - \vec{V}\times(\nabla\times\vec{V})$
- [ ] $\nabla\cdot(f\vec{V}) = f\,\nabla\cdot\vec{V} + \vec{V}\cdot\nabla f$
- [ ] $\nabla\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{A}) = 0$ (always)

### D. Boundary Condition Master List
- [ ] No-penetration (solid)
- [ ] No-slip (viscous solid)
- [ ] Symmetry: $\partial\vec{V}/\partial n = 0$
- [ ] Inlet: $\vec{V} = \vec{V}_{inlet}(y,z)$
- [ ] Outlet: $\partial\vec{V}/\partial x = 0$ (fully developed)
- [ ] Interface: $V_{n,1} = V_{n,2}$

### E. Common Biomedical Applications
- [ ] Blood flow (Poiseuille, Womersley) — incompressible, viscous, pulsatile.
- [ ] Airway flow (trachea, bronchi) — variable cross-section, unsteady.
- [ ] Dialysis cartridge flow — 1-D approximation, control volume analysis.
- [ ] Drug delivery aerosols — streamline visualization in bifurcating airways.
- [ ] Heart valve flow — high local + convective acceleration.

### F. Numerical Quick-Facts
- [ ] Cardiac output: ~ 5 L/min = $8.3\times 10^{-5}$ m³/s.
- [ ] Aorta diameter: ~ 2.5 cm; $V_{avg} \approx 0.17$ m/s.
- [ ] Reynolds number for large arteries: ~ 1000 (pulsatile, transitional).
- [ ] Blood density: $\rho \approx 1060$ kg/m³.
- [ ] Water density: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.

### G. Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Don't equate streamlines and pathlines in unsteady flow.
- [ ] Don't drop the convective term in the material derivative.
- [ ] Don't use $\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$ for compressible flow.
- [ ] Don't use stream function for 3-D flow.
- [ ] Don't forget the divergence theorem in deriving the differential continuity equation.
- [ ] Don't mix sign conventions for $d\vec{A}$ (outward normal is standard).

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Fluid Kinematics**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | B.Tech Biomedical Engineering*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Statics: Surface Tension, Membrane and Cortical Tension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT863 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Statics: Surface Tension, Membrane and Cortical Tension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Surface Tension (γ)

**Formal Definition:** Surface tension is the work (energy) required to increase the surface area of a liquid by one unit area, or equivalently, the force per unit length acting along a surface, tending to minimize the surface area.

$$\gamma = \frac{F}{L} \quad \text{[N/m or J/m}^2\text{]}$$

**Physical Origin:** Molecules in the bulk of a liquid experience balanced cohesive (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding) forces from all directions. Molecules at the surface experience a net inward pull, creating a state of tension. The system minimizes its free energy by minimizing surface area — a droplet assumes a spherical shape.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Value:** The surface tension of water at **25 °C** is **γ_water = 72 mN/m** (≈ 0.072 N/m). Blood plasma is approximately **γ_plasma ≈ 50–60 mN/m** — a key parameter in hemorheology and pulmonary alveolar mechanics.

## 1.2 Membrane Tension (σ_m)

**Formal Definition:** Membrane tension is the in-plane isotropic tension sustained by a **lipid bilayer** (e.g., the plasma membrane). It represents the mechanical energy stored per unit area of the bilayer due to stretching and curvature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological Context:** Membrane tension regulates ion channel gating, endo/exocytosis, and cell spreading. Typical values in mammalian cells lie in the range of **0.001 to 0.1 mN/m**.

## 1.3 Cortical Tension (σ_c)

**Formal Definition:** Cortical tension is the contractile tension generated within the **actin cortex** — a thin (≈ 100–500 nm) meshwork of actin filaments, myosin motors, and crosslinkers located just beneath the plasma membrane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Membrane tension is *passive* (elastic stretching of the bilayer), while cortical tension is *active* (driven by ATP-dependent myosin-II contractility). The total apparent surface tension of a cell is the **sum** of both:
>
> $$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{membrane} + \sigma_{cortex}$$

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Inflated Balloon vs. the Living Cell:**
> - A **soap bubble** (two lipid-like surfaces with water between) demonstrates pure **surface tension** — governed by the **Young–Laplace equation** $\Delta P = 4\gamma/R$.
> - A **water strider** walking on a pond exploits the high surface tension of water (**72 mN/m**).
> - A **living cell** is a "balloon within a sweater" — the bilayer is the balloon (membrane tension), and the actin cortex is the sweater actively pulling it inward (cortical tension). This dual mechanism is what allows cells to round up during mitosis, migrate, and divide.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Origin of Surface Tension

The Helmholtz free energy of an interface is:

$$G = \gamma \cdot A$$

Spontaneous processes satisfy $dG < 0$. Hence, for an isothermal system:

$$dG = \gamma \, dA + A \, d\gamma = 0$$

- If $\gamma$ is constant, the surface must shrink ($dA < 0$).
- This is the thermodynamic basis for **droplet sphericity** and **capillary action**.

## 2.2 Why a Pressure Jump Exists Across a Curved Interface

- **Flat surface:** Cohesive forces cancel laterally → no net force → no pressure jump.
- **Curved surface:** Curvature creates a net inward pull → higher pressure on the **concave (inner)** side.
- This pressure jump is quantified by the **Young–Laplace equation**.

## 2.3 Why Membrane and Cortical Tension Coexist in Cells

- The lipid bilayer alone cannot sustain large tensions; it would rupture at ~3–5 mN/m.
- The actin cortex **reinforces** the membrane and actively contracts (like a "purse-string").
- Myosin-II motor activity generates active contractile stress within the cortex.
- This contractility is regulated by signaling pathways (RhoA/ROCK, calcium).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Surface tension definition | $\gamma = F / L$ | $F$ [N], $L$ [m], $\gamma$ [N/m] |
| 2 | Work to create new surface | $W = \gamma \cdot \Delta A$ | $W$ [J], $\Delta A$ [m²] |
| 3 | Young–Laplace (single sphere / droplet) | $\Delta P = 2\gamma / R$ | $\Delta P$ [Pa], $R$ [m] |
| 4 | Young–Laplace (soap bubble, two surfaces) | $\Delta P = 4\gamma / R$ | Bubble has inner + outer surface |
| 5 | Young–Laplace (general curved surface) | $\Delta P = \gamma \left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$ | $R_1, R_2$ principal radii of curvature [m] |
| 6 | Capillary rise | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | $\theta$ = contact angle, $\rho$ [kg/m³], $g$ [m/s²], $r$ = tube radius [m] |
| 7 | Laplace pressure in cell | $\Delta P = 2\sigma_{total} / R_{cell}$ | $\sigma_{total} = \sigma_m + \sigma_c$ |
| 8 | Total cell surface tension | $\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{membrane} + \sigma_{cortex}$ | $\sigma$ [N/m] or [mN/m] |
| 9 | Force balance at contact line (wetting) | $F_{net} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta = 0$ | Young's equation, subscripts s,l,v = solid, liquid, vapor |
| 10 | Excess cortical pressure (active) | $\sigma_c \approx \eta_{cortex} \cdot \dot{\epsilon} + \zeta_{myosin}$ | $\eta$ viscosity, $\dot{\epsilon}$ strain rate, $\zeta$ active stress |

## 2.5 Engineering & Biomedical Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Lung surfactant therapy** in neonatal respiratory distress — lowers alveolar surface tension to prevent collapse (Laplace's law for alveoli).
> - **Microfluidic device design** — droplet generation, capillary-driven flow in point-of-care diagnostics.
> - **Cell mechanics & drug screening** — micropipette aspiration, atomic force microscopy, optical tweezers.
> - **Tissue engineering** — controlling cell–surface interactions via contact angle and cortical tension.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Young–Laplace Equation for a Spherical Droplet

**Setup:** Consider an isolated liquid droplet of radius $R$ in a gaseous medium. The pressure inside the droplet is $P_i$ and outside is $P_o$. The pressure difference is $\Delta P = P_i - P_o$. We perform a force balance on a hypothetical hemispherical cut.

**Step 1 — Identify forces acting on the hemisphere:**

- **Pressure force** (pushing the hemisphere outward across the circular cross-section of area $\pi R^2$):

$$F_{pressure} = \Delta P \cdot \pi R^2$$

- **Surface tension force** (acting tangentially along the circular edge of the cut, circumference $= 2\pi R$, with surface tension $\gamma$ pulling the surface inward):

$$F_{tension} = \gamma \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two forces for static equilibrium:**

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2 = \gamma \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2 = 2 \pi R \gamma$$

Divide both sides by $\pi R^2$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Young–Laplace equation** for a **single-interface spherical droplet**.

**Step 4 — Extension to a soap bubble (two surfaces):**
A soap bubble has an **inner** and an **outer** surface, both contributing surface tension. Hence the tension force is doubled:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2 = 2 \cdot \gamma \cdot 2\pi R = 4\pi R \gamma$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{4\gamma}{R}}$$

**Step 5 — Generalization to a non-spherical (arbitrarily curved) surface:**
The result generalizes to the **mean curvature** $H = (1/R_1 + 1/R_2)/2$:

$$\Delta P = \gamma \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise)

**Setup:** A thin glass tube of inner radius $r$ is dipped into a wetting liquid (e.g., water). The contact angle is $\theta$. The liquid rises to a height $h$.

**Step 1 — Vertical force from surface tension at the meniscus:**
The liquid–air interface is a thin ring of circumference $2\pi r$. The vertical component of the surface tension force (upward) is:

$$F_{up} = \gamma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Weight of the lifted liquid column (downward):**
The volume of liquid in the cylindrical column is $V = \pi r^2 h$. Its weight is:

$$W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \pi r^2 h$$

**Step 3 — Force balance at equilibrium:**

$$\gamma \cdot 2\pi r \cos\theta = \rho g \pi r^2 h$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the capillary rise $h$:**

$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **Jurin's Law**. Note the inverse relationship with $r$: narrower tubes produce higher rises. This principle governs **wicking in textiles**, **xylem transport in plants**, and **capillary microfluidics**.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Total Cell Surface Tension (Membrane + Cortex)

**Setup:** A spherical cell of radius $R_{cell}$ is held in equilibrium by the combined action of membrane tension $\sigma_m$ (passive, elastic) and cortical tension $\sigma_c$ (active, contractile).

**Step 1 — Apply Young–Laplace to the cell:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\sigma_{total}}{R_{cell}}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the total tension:**

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{membrane} + \sigma_{cortex}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2(\sigma_{membrane} + \sigma_{cortex})}{R_{cell}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- If a drug (e.g., cytochalasin D) disrupts the actin cortex, then $\sigma_{cortex} \to 0$, leaving only the membrane contribution. The cell becomes flaccid.
- If myosin-II activity is enhanced, $\sigma_{cortex}$ rises, and the cell rounds up, generating a higher internal turgor pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This dual-tension framework is the cornerstone of **modern cell mechanics** and is examined extensively in KTU Module 1 transport-phenomena derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Relationship Between Surface, Membrane, and Cortical Tension

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surface Tension gamma] --> A1[Energy per unit area J per m2]
    A --> A2[Force per unit length N per m]
    A --> A3[Laplace Pressure Delta P equals 2 gamma over R]
    B[Membrane Tension sigma m] --> B1[Passive elastic tension]
    B --> B2[Lipid bilayer stretching]
    B --> B3[Typical 0.001 to 0.1 mN per m]
    C[Cortical Tension sigma c] --> C1[Active contractile tension]
    C --> C2[Actin myosin cortex]
    C --> C3[ATP dependent myosin II]
    D[Total Cell Surface Tension] --> D1[sigma total equals sigma m plus sigma c]
    A --> D
    B --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E[Cell Shape and Mechanics]
    E --> E1[Mitosis rounding]
    E --> E2[Cell migration]
    E --> E3[Cytokinesis furrow ingression]
```

## 4.2 Force-Balance Block Architecture at a Curved Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inside_Region
        Pi[Internal Pressure Pi]
    end
    subgraph Interface_Region
        G1[Surface Tension gamma]
        G2[Curvature 1 over R]
        G3[Contact Angle theta]
    end
    subgraph Outside_Region
        Po[External Pressure Po]
    end
    Pi --> DP1[Pressure Force Delta P times Area]
    Po --> DP1
    G1 --> FT1[Surface Tension Force gamma times Length]
    G2 --> FT1
    G3 --> FT1
    DP1 --> EQ[Equilibrium Condition]
    FT1 --> EQ
    EQ --> RES[Result Young Laplace Equation]
    RES --> OUT1[Single surface Delta P equals 2 gamma over R]
    RES --> OUT2[Bubble two surfaces Delta P equals 4 gamma over R]
    RES --> OUT3[Cell Delta P equals 2 sigma total over R cell]
```

## 4.3 Cellular Architecture: Membrane + Cortex

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OUT[Extracellular Space] --> MEM[Lipid Bilayer Plasma Membrane]
    MEM --> CORTEX[Actin Cortex 100 to 500 nm thick]
    CORTEX --> CYTO[Actin Filaments F actin]
    CORTEX --> MYO[Myosin II Motors]
    CORTEX --> XL[Crosslinkers alpha actinin fascin]
    MYO --> CON[Active Contractile Stress]
    CORTEX --> SC[Cortical Tension sigma c]
    MEM --> SM[Membrane Tension sigma m]
    SC --> TOT[sigma total]
    SM --> TOT
    TOT --> SHAPE[Cell Shape Equilibrium]
    SHAPE --> PR[Internal Turgor Pressure]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define surface tension. State the Young–Laplace equation for a spherical droplet and a soap bubble, clearly identifying the difference.**

**Model Answer:**

Surface tension $\gamma$ is the force per unit length acting along a liquid surface, equivalent to the work required to increase the surface area by one unit. It arises from the imbalance of cohesive intermolecular forces at the interface. **Units: N/m (or J/m²).**

- For a **spherical droplet** (single surface):

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$

- For a **soap bubble** (two surfaces, inner and outer):

$$\Delta P = \frac{4\gamma}{R}$$

**The difference:** A soap bubble has **two** liquid–air interfaces, doubling the surface-tension force. Hence, at the same radius, a soap bubble sustains **twice** the Laplace pressure of a simple droplet. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between membrane tension and cortical tension in a biological cell.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Membrane Tension $\sigma_m$ | Cortical Tension $\sigma_c$ |
|---------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| Origin | Lipid bilayer stretching | Actomyosin cortex contractility |
| Nature | Passive, elastic | Active, ATP-driven |
| Location | Plasma membrane plane | Sub-membranous actin meshwork |
| Typical magnitude | 0.001 – 0.1 mN/m | 0.01 – 1 mN/m |
| Regulation | Bilayer composition, area | RhoA/ROCK, myosin-II activity |

The total apparent cell surface tension is their sum: $\sigma_{total} = \sigma_m + \sigma_c$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### ⭐ Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]

#### (a) Derive the Young–Laplace equation for a spherical droplet. State how it is modified for a soap bubble. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Consider a spherical liquid droplet of radius $R$ surrounded by gas. Let $P_i$ and $P_o$ be the internal and external pressures. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2:** Imagine the droplet cut into two hemispheres. The pressure difference $\Delta P = P_i - P_o$ exerts a net outward force on a hemisphere across its circular cross-section of area $A = \pi R^2$: **(1 mark)**

$$F_{pressure} = \Delta P \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Step 3:** Surface tension $\gamma$ acts along the circular edge (circumference $= 2\pi R$) tangentially, pulling the surface inward: **(1 mark)**

$$F_{tension} = \gamma \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 4:** At static equilibrium, the forces balance: **(1 mark)**

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2 = \gamma \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 5:** Simplify: **(0.5 mark)**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$

**Step 6 — Modification for a soap bubble:** A soap bubble is a thin liquid film enclosing a gas; it has **two** gas–liquid interfaces (inner and outer). The total surface-tension force is therefore doubled, giving: **(2 marks)**

$$\Delta P_{bubble} = \frac{4\gamma}{R}$$

**Conclusion:** The Young–Laplace equation shows that smaller droplets have a higher internal pressure, which is why small bubbles in a foam disappear while larger ones grow (Ostwald ripening). **(1 mark)**

---

#### (b) A glass capillary tube of inner radius $r = 0.5 \, \text{mm}$ is dipped into water at 25 °C. The contact angle is $\theta = 0°$. Calculate the capillary rise. Given: $\gamma_{water} = 72 \, \text{mN/m}$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write Jurin's Law:** **(1 mark)**

$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:** **(1 mark)**

$$h = \frac{2 \times (72 \times 10^{-3}) \times \cos 0°}{1000 \times 9.81 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})}$$

**Step 3 — Compute step-by-step:** **(3 marks)**

- Numerator: $2 \times 72 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 = 0.144 \, \text{N/m}$
- Denominator: $1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0005 = 4.905 \, \text{N/m}^2$

$$h = \frac{0.144}{4.905} = 0.02936 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to centimeters:** **(1 mark)**

$$h \approx 2.94 \, \text{cm}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:** A narrower tube would produce a higher rise. This is exploited in plant xylem transport, lab-on-a-chip devices, and absorbent wound dressings. **(1 mark)**

---

### ⭐ Question B [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]

#### (a) Explain the role of membrane and cortical tension in determining the shape and internal pressure of a biological cell. Derive the relevant equation. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction:** A living cell is a complex mechanical system in which the **lipid bilayer** (plasma membrane) and the underlying **actin cortex** jointly determine cell shape and turgor. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Membrane tension $\sigma_m$:** This is the in-plane tension sustained by the lipid bilayer. It is largely **passive** and depends on the area difference between the two leaflets and the bilayer's elastic stretching. Typical magnitude: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ mN/m. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Cortical tension $\sigma_c$:** This is the **active** contractile tension generated by the actin cortex — a crosslinked meshwork of actin filaments with myosin-II molecular motors. Myosin hydrolyses ATP to slide actin filaments, generating an isotropic contractile stress. Typical magnitude: $10^{-2}$ to $1$ mN/m. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4 — Decomposition and Young–Laplace:** The total apparent cell surface tension is the sum: **(1 mark)**

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{membrane} + \sigma_{cortex}$$

Applying Young–Laplace to a spherical cell of radius $R_{cell}$: **(1 mark)**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2(\sigma_{membrane} + \sigma_{cortex})}{R_{cell}}$$

**Step 5 — Significance:** This dual-tension framework explains how cells (i) round up during mitosis (increased myosin-II contractility), (ii) generate traction forces during migration, and (iii) drive cytokinesis furrow ingression. **(1 mark)**

---

#### (b) A soap bubble of radius $R = 2 \, \text{cm}$ has a surface tension $\gamma = 25 \, \text{mN/m}$. Calculate (i) the Laplace pressure inside the bubble, and (ii) the work required to inflate the bubble from $R = 1 \, \text{cm}$ to $R = 2 \, \text{cm}$. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Laplace Pressure:** **(3 marks)**

For a soap bubble (two surfaces):

$$\Delta P = \frac{4\gamma}{R}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{4 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}}{0.02} = \frac{0.1}{0.02} = 5 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Part (ii) — Work of inflation:** **(4 marks)**

The Helmholtz free energy stored in a soap bubble of two surfaces is:

$$G = 2 \cdot (4\pi R^2) \cdot \gamma = 8\pi \gamma R^2$$

The work done to inflate from $R_1$ to $R_2$:

$$W = G_2 - G_1 = 8\pi \gamma (R_2^2 - R_1^2)$$

Substituting $R_1 = 0.01 \, \text{m}$, $R_2 = 0.02 \, \text{m}$:

$$W = 8\pi \times (25 \times 10^{-3}) \times ((0.02)^2 - (0.01)^2)$$

$$W = 8\pi \times 0.025 \times (0.0004 - 0.0001)$$

$$W = 8\pi \times 0.025 \times 0.0003$$

$$W = 6\pi \times 10^{-6} \, \text{J}$$

$$W \approx 1.885 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{J} \approx 18.85 \, \mu\text{J}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for a soap bubble** — a soap bubble has *two* surfaces; if you write $\Delta P = 2\gamma/R$ for a bubble, you lose **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — surface tension is in **N/m**, not N/m². Energy per unit area has the same numerical value, but the unit is J/m². Always specify the system.
> 3. **Ignoring the contact angle in capillary problems** — if the problem states $\theta = 0°$, you must explicitly state $\cos 0° = 1$ in your solution.
> 4. **Forgetting the active nature of cortical tension** — examiners want you to note that $\sigma_c$ is **ATP-driven**, not merely elastic. Stating it is "contractile" earns full marks.
> 5. **Missing the additive nature of total cell tension** — always write the full decomposition $\sigma_{total} = \sigma_m + \sigma_c$ before applying Young–Laplace.
> 6. **Sign errors in pressure difference** — $\Delta P$ is always defined as *inside minus outside*; the concave side has higher pressure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Surface tension** $\gamma$ [N/m]: arises from unbalanced cohesive forces at a liquid surface; it is the energy cost per unit area of new surface.
- **Young–Laplace equation** governs the pressure jump across a curved interface:
  - Droplet: $\Delta P = 2\gamma/R$
  - Soap bubble: $\Delta P = 4\gamma/R$
  - General: $\Delta P = \gamma(1/R_1 + 1/R_2)$
- **Capillary rise (Jurin's Law):** $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta / (\rho g r)$ — narrow tubes → higher rise; critical for microfluidics, plant biology, and wicking materials.
- **Membrane tension** $\sigma_m$: passive, elastic, located in the lipid bilayer.
- **Cortical tension** $\sigma_c$: active, ATP-driven, located in the sub-membranous actomyosin cortex.
- **Total cell surface tension:** $\sigma_{total} = \sigma_m + \sigma_c$ — the quantity appearing in the cell-level Young–Laplace equation.
- **Sign convention:** Concave side (inside a droplet or bubble) is at higher pressure.
- **Engineering relevance:** Lung surfactant therapy, microfluidics, cell mechanics, hemorheology, drug delivery.
- **Units discipline:** Always quote $\gamma$ in **N/m** (or mN/m for cells), $\Delta P$ in **Pa** (or kPa), radii in **m**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids, Laminar and Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT863 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids, Laminar and Turbulent Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Newtonian Fluids — The Formal Definition

A **Newtonian fluid** is a fluid that obeys **Newton's Law of Viscosity**, in which the shear stress $(\tau)$ at any point within the fluid is directly and linearly proportional to the local **shear rate** $(\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy})$ at that same point, with the constant of proportionality being the **dynamic viscosity** $(\mu)$. Mathematically, this constitutive relation is expressed as:

$$\tau_{yx} = -\mu \frac{du_x}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau_{yx}$ is the shear stress acting on a plane perpendicular to the y-axis in the x-direction (Pa)
- $\mu$ is the **dynamic (absolute) viscosity** of the fluid (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\dfrac{du_x}{dy}$ is the velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow (s⁻¹)
- The **negative sign** denotes that the shear stress acts in a direction opposing the relative motion (i.e., momentum diffuses from high-velocity regions to low-velocity regions).

The **kinematic viscosity** $(\nu)$ is defined as:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid mass density (kg/m³). The SI unit of $\nu$ is m²/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Diagnostic Property of Newtonian Fluids:** The ratio $\dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \mu$ is **constant** and **independent** of the shear rate, time of shearing, or the kinematic history of the fluid. In other words, if you plot shear stress (y-axis) versus shear rate (x-axis), you obtain a **straight line passing through the origin** with slope equal to $\mu$.

**Common Newtonian Fluid Examples with Typical Viscosities at 20 °C:**
- Water: $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- Air: $\mu \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- Blood plasma: $\mu \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s (plasma alone is essentially Newtonian)
- Ethanol: $\mu \approx 1.20 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- Mercury: $\mu \approx 1.55 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- Glycerol (25 °C): $\mu \approx 0.95$ Pa·s

### 1.2 Non-Newtonian Fluids — The Formal Definition

A **Non-Newtonian fluid** is any fluid that does **not** satisfy Newton's law of viscosity. In such fluids, the apparent viscosity $\mu_a$ is a function of the shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$, the duration of shearing (time), and/or the previous kinematic history of the fluid:

$$\tau = \mu_a(\dot{\gamma}, t, \text{history}) \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

These materials are broadly classified based on their rheological (flow-deformation) response, which has profound significance in **biomedical engineering** because most biological fluids (whole blood, mucus, synovial fluid, vitreous humor, cytoplasm) display non-Newtonian behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biorheological Significance:** Whole human blood exhibits pronounced **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behavior because red blood cells (erythrocytes) deform, align, and aggregate at different shear rates. At very low shear rates, red cells stack into **rouleaux** aggregates, increasing viscosity; at high shear rates, these aggregates disperse and cells deform into streamlined shapes, drastically reducing viscosity.

### 1.3 Laminar and Turbulent Flow — Formal Definitions

- **Laminar Flow:** A flow regime in which the fluid moves in **smooth, parallel, well-defined layers (laminae or streamlines)** with no macroscopic mixing between adjacent layers. The fluid motion is highly ordered and predictable; viscous forces dominate over inertial forces. The **Reynolds number** is below a critical value.
- **Turbulent Flow:** A flow regime characterized by **chaotic, irregular, three-dimensional fluctuations** in velocity and pressure, with the formation of **eddies, vortices, and random mixing**. Inertial forces dominate; viscous effects are confined to thin boundary layers near walls.
- **Transition Flow:** An **unstable, intermittent** regime that exists between fully laminar and fully turbulent flow.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **The Honey vs. Ketchup vs. Water Analogy:**
> Imagine you are pouring three liquids — **water, honey, and ketchup** — off a spoon. **Water** flows immediately and the same way whether you tip the spoon gently or sharply; that is Newtonian and rate-independent. **Honey** is also Newtonian, but it pours slowly because its viscosity is intrinsically high. **Ketchup**, however, sits stubbornly on the spoon when tipped slowly (it acts almost solid — it has a **yield stress**), but the moment you tap or shake the bottle (a large shear rate is suddenly applied), it flows out freely. This rate-dependent behavior is the hallmark of a **non-Newtonian** (specifically **Bingham plastic** or **shear-thinning**) fluid.
>
> **The Garden Hose Analogy for Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow:** Open a garden tap just a tiny bit and the water exits the nozzle as a **smooth, glassy, transparent column** (laminar). Open the tap fully, and the water exits as a **frothy, irregular, white-jet** stream with visible swirls and splashing (turbulent). The transition is governed by the **Reynolds number**.

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Biomedical Engineering

- **Blood flow** in arteries, arterioles, venules, and capillaries is dominantly non-Newtonian, especially in small vessels where shear rates are low.
- **Airway mucus** clearance (mucociliary transport) depends on the viscoelastic properties of mucus.
- **Drug delivery** through syringe needles, catheters, and stents requires accurate prediction of pressure drops, which depends on the flow regime (laminar/turbulent) and rheological model.
- **Dialysis, hemofiltration, oxygenator design,** and **IV infusion systems** all involve tube flow with non-Newtonian working fluids.
- **Microfluidics and Lab-on-a-Chip** devices operate predominantly in the laminar regime (low Re), where diffusion and not convection governs mixing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Fluids — Hierarchical Breakdown

#### A. Time-Independent Non-Newtonian Fluids (Generalized Newtonian Fluids — GNF)

In these fluids, $\mu_a$ depends only on the **instantaneous** shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$, not on the history of deformation.

1. **Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning):** Apparent viscosity **decreases** with increasing shear rate.
   - Examples: **Whole blood, polymer solutions (polyethylene oxide), nail polish, paint, ketchup (partially).**
   - Mathematical model: **Power-Law (Ostwald-de Waele) Model**
   
   $$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}, \quad 0 < n < 1$$
   
   where $K$ is the **consistency index** (Pa·sⁿ) and $n$ is the **flow behavior index** (dimensionless). For pseudoplastics, $n < 1$.

2. **Dilatant (Shear-Thickening):** Apparent viscosity **increases** with increasing shear rate.
   - Examples: **Cornstarch-in-water suspensions, wet sand, sugar in water (highly concentrated), some ceramic suspensions.**
   - Same power-law equation but with:
   
   $$n > 1$$

3. **Bingham Plastic:** Behaves like a solid up to a critical **yield stress** $\tau_0$, beyond which it flows like a Newtonian fluid with constant plastic viscosity $\mu_p$.
   - Examples: **Toothpaste, drilling mud, mayonnaise, chocolate, fresh cement paste, blood at very low shear (approximation).**
   - Mathematical model:
   
   $$\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}, \quad \text{for } \tau > \tau_0$$

4. **Casson Fluid:** A yield-stress fluid used specifically to model **blood rheology** in the low-shear regime. It accounts for the finite size of red blood cell aggregates.
   - Mathematical model:
   
   $$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$$
   
   where $\tau_0$ is the Casson yield stress and $\mu_\infty$ is the asymptotic viscosity at infinite shear rate.

5. **Herschel–Bulkley Fluid:** A generalized model combining both a yield stress and power-law behavior above the yield stress.
   
   $$\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^{n}, \quad \text{for } \tau > \tau_0$$

#### B. Time-Dependent Non-Newtonian Fluids

6. **Thixotropic:** Apparent viscosity **decreases** over time when subjected to constant shear rate; structure breaks down progressively and rebuilds when shearing stops.
   - Example: **Gels, cytoplasm, some paints, yogurt.**

7. **Rheopectic (Antithixotropic):** Apparent viscosity **increases** with time at constant shear.
   - Example: **Some printer inks, gypsum paste.**

#### C. Viscoelastic Fluids

8. **Viscoelastic:** Exhibit both viscous (energy dissipation) and elastic (energy storage) responses; they "remember" their deformation history and partially rebound after stress removal.
   - Examples: **Saliva, synovial fluid, DNA solutions, polymer melts, arterial wall tissue.**
   - Modeled using **Maxwell**, **Oldroyd-B**, or **Kelvin–Voigt** constitutive equations.

### 2.2 The Apparent (Effective) Viscosity

For a generalized Newtonian fluid obeying the power-law model, the apparent viscosity is:

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

This is the viscosity you would **measure** in a viscometer at a particular shear rate. It is **not** a true material constant for non-Newtonian fluids.

### 2.3 Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow — Quantitative Criterion

The **Reynolds number** is the dimensionless group that quantifies the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** in a flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $v$ = mean (or characteristic) velocity (m/s)
- $D$ = characteristic length — for pipe flow, the **inner diameter** (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m²/s)

**Critical Reynolds Number Ranges for Internal (Pipe) Flow:**

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Physical Description |
|-------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Laminar | $Re < 2300$ | Streamline flow, viscous forces dominate, no radial mixing |
| Transitional | $2300 \leq Re \leq 4000$ | Unstable, intermittent, neither fully laminar nor fully turbulent |
| Turbulent | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic, eddies, inertial forces dominate, vigorous radial mixing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reynolds Number Caveat:** The values 2300 and 4000 are **engineering approximations** valid for **steady, fully developed, internal flow in a smooth, circular, straight pipe with a uniform inlet profile**. The exact transition value depends on inlet conditions, pipe roughness, vibrations, and geometry. For biomedical applications involving pulsatile flow, the **Womersley number** ($\alpha = R\sqrt{\omega/\nu}$) is often more appropriate than the steady Reynolds number.

### 2.4 The Navier–Stokes Equation (The Governing Equation)

For an incompressible Newtonian fluid, the **momentum conservation** (Cauchy momentum equation) reduces to the **Navier–Stokes equation**:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}$$

The term $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{v}$ represents the **viscous (diffusive) momentum transport** — the only term that contains $\mu$. It is the mathematical embodiment of Newton's law of viscosity within a general fluid-mechanics framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Laminar vs. Turbulent in the N–S Framework:** The non-linear **inertial term** $\rho(\vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v})$ is responsible for flow instabilities and turbulence. When $\rho(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}) \ll \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v}$ (low Re), the equation becomes **linear** and the flow is laminar. When the inertial term dominates (high Re), the non-linearity spawns chaos → turbulent flow.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this entire table for KTU 2024 ESE. These are the equations that recur every year.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Pa; $u$ varies linearly with $y$ for simple shear |
| 2 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s |
| 3 | Power-Law Model | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $K$ in Pa·sⁿ; $n$ dimensionless |
| 4 | Apparent Viscosity | $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Pa·s |
| 5 | Bingham Model | $\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_0$ = yield stress (Pa) |
| 6 | Casson Model | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ | Used for blood |
| 7 | Herschel–Bulkley | $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Generalized yield-stress fluid |
| 8 | Reynolds Number (pipe) | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | Dimensionless; $D$ = pipe diameter |
| 9 | Critical Re (laminar) | $Re < 2300$ | Pipe flow, smooth, steady |
| 10 | Critical Re (turbulent) | $Re > 4000$ | Pipe flow, smooth, steady |
| 11 | Friction Factor (laminar) | $f = 64 / Re$ | **Fanning friction factor** $f$; Darcy factor = $4f$ |
| 12 | Hagen–Poiseuille Pressure Drop | $\Delta p = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$ | Newtonian, laminar, fully developed |
| 13 | Hagen–Poiseuille Volumetric Flow | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^4 \Delta p}{128 \mu L}$ | Steady, laminar, Newtonian |
| 14 | Power-Law Flow Rate (pipe) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{32} \cdot \dfrac{n}{3n+1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{D \Delta p}{4 L K}\right)^{1/n}$ | For non-Newtonian power-law fluid |
| 15 | Power-Law Velocity Profile (pipe) | $u(r) = u_{max} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{(n+1)/n}\right]$ | Plug-like for $n \to 0$; parabolic for $n=1$ |
| 16 | Navier–Stokes (incomp.) | $\rho \left( \dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}$ | Vector form |
| 17 | Womersley Number | $\alpha = R \sqrt{\omega / \nu}$ | Pulsatile flow parameter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latex in Tables — Notice:** Throughout these tables, vertical bars (e.g., for $\vert \cdot \vert$) are avoided; if absolute value is needed, use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ to keep markdown parsers happy. In all my LaTeX above, only standard characters are used inside equations.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of This Framework

- **Cardiovascular Hemodynamics:** Determining whether blood flow in the aorta, femoral artery, or coronary arteries is laminar or turbulent. Aortic flow is **transitional-to-turbulent** at peak systole; capillary flow is firmly **laminar**.
- **Hemodialyzer Design:** Hollow-fiber dialyzers contain thousands of narrow tubes; knowing the Re helps predict whether solute (urea, creatinine) transport is by diffusion-dominant laminar flow or augmented by secondary flows.
- **Heart-Lung Machines & Oxygenators:** Designers must avoid hemolysis (shear-induced RBC damage), which depends on accurate shear stress estimation in non-Newtonian blood.
- **Microfluidic Diagnostics:** Lab-on-chip devices operate at $Re \ll 1$ — strictly laminar, with mixing only by molecular diffusion.
- **Respiratory Therapy & Ventilator Tubing:** Turbulent airflow in jets and endotracheal tubes determines aerosol drug delivery efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Newton's Law of Viscosity from Momentum Flux

**Goal:** Show that the viscous shear stress equals the **molecular momentum flux** in the direction of decreasing velocity.

**Setup (Couette Flow Model):**
Consider two infinite parallel plates separated by a distance $L$. The lower plate is stationary; the upper plate moves with velocity $V$ in the $+x$ direction. The fluid between is assumed to be a continuum in steady, laminar, fully developed flow.

**Step 1 — Velocity field by no-slip boundary conditions:**
At $y = 0$ (lower plate): $u_x(0) = 0$ (no-slip).
At $y = L$ (upper plate): $u_x(L) = V$ (no-slip).
For steady Couette flow with negligible pressure gradient, the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:

$$\frac{d^2 u_x}{dy^2} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Integrate twice:**

$$\frac{du_x}{dy} = C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_x(y) = C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions:**

$$u_x(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$$
$$u_x(L) = V \Rightarrow C_1 = \frac{V}{L}$$

Therefore:

$$u_x(y) = V \frac{y}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Force balance on an elemental fluid slab:**
Consider a fluid element of area $A$ and thickness $dy$. The momentum entering from below per unit time per unit area is $\rho u_x$ (momentum flux). The momentum leaving from the top is $\rho (u_x + du_x)$. The net outflow of x-momentum per unit area per unit time is:

$$\phi_{px} = \rho \frac{d(u_x \cdot u_x/2)}{dy} - \mu \frac{du_x}{dy}$$

Wait — the rigorous momentum-flux derivation uses the **Newtonian constitutive assumption** as the starting point. Let us instead derive from a **force-balance on a control volume**.

**Step 5 — Newton's postulate:**
Newton postulated that for a "simple fluid," the **shear stress** is linearly proportional to the **velocity gradient** normal to the direction of shear:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

This is taken as a **constitutive assumption** (a defining material property). It cannot be derived from first principles — it is an **empirical law** confirmed by experiment for Newtonian fluids. Hence it is properly called **Newton's Law of Viscosity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Limits:** This law holds only for **laminar, incompressible, isotropic, Newtonian** fluids. It fails for non-Newtonian fluids, suspensions, polymeric liquids, and at very high shear rates where continuum assumptions break down.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Number via Dimensional Analysis

**Goal:** Show that the ratio of inertial to viscous forces yields the dimensionless Reynolds number.

**Step 1 — Identify governing variables for pipe flow:**
For flow in a straight circular pipe of diameter $D$ at mean velocity $v$, the pressure drop $\Delta p$ over length $L$ depends on:

$$\Delta p = f(\rho, \mu, v, D, L)$$

This is **5 physical variables**. Using the **Buckingham Pi Theorem** with $3$ fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), we expect $5 - 3 = 2$ dimensionless groups.

**Step 2 — Choose repeating variables:** $\rho$, $v$, $D$ (these contain M, L, T independently).

**Step 3 — Form the Pi groups:**

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho^a v^b D^c}$$

Demanding dimensionless:
- M: $-1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1$
- L: $-1 - 3a + b + c = 0 \Rightarrow -1 - 3 + b + c = 0 \Rightarrow b + c = 4$
- T: $-2 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -2$
- Therefore $c = 6$

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho v^2 D^6 / D^6} \to \text{check: } \frac{\Delta p}{\rho v^2} \cdot \frac{L}{D}$$

After proper normalization with $L$:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{\Delta p \, D}{\rho v^2 L} \quad \text{(Euler number times geometry)}$$

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = Re$$

**Step 4 — Conclude:**
The pressure drop in pipe flow is governed by:

$$\frac{\Delta p}{\rho v^2} \cdot \frac{D}{L} = f\!\left(Re\right)$$

This is the **Darcy–Weisbach relation** in dimensionless form. The Reynolds number $Re$ is the **only** dimensionless group governing the flow (assuming negligible gravity, compressibility, and surface tension effects).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation of Re:**
> 
> $$Re = \frac{\rho v^2 / D}{\mu v / D^2} = \frac{\text{Inertial force per unit volume}}{\text{Viscous force per unit volume}}$$
> 
> - $Re \ll 1$ → viscous forces dominate → **laminar** flow.
> - $Re \gg 1$ → inertial forces dominate → **turbulent** flow.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hagen–Poiseuille Flow (Parabolic Velocity Profile for a Newtonian Fluid in a Pipe)

**Goal:** Derive the velocity profile and pressure drop for steady, laminar, fully developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal pipe of radius $R$.

**Step 1 — Geometric setup:**
- Cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, x)$ with $x$ along the pipe axis.
- Pipe radius $R$, length $L$, pressure $p(0) = p_1$ at inlet, $p(L) = p_2$ at outlet, with $p_1 > p_2$.
- Steady, axisymmetric, fully developed flow: $v_r = v_\theta = 0$, $v_x = v_x(r)$ only.

**Step 2 — Apply continuity (incompressible):** Automatically satisfied.

**Step 3 — Navier–Stokes in cylindrical coordinates (axial component):**

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_r \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial \theta} + v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} \right] + \rho g_x$$

**Step 4 — Apply simplifications:**
- Steady: $\partial/\partial t = 0$
- Axisymmetric: $\partial/\partial \theta = 0$
- Fully developed: $\partial v_x/\partial x = 0$
- $v_r = v_\theta = 0$
- Horizontal pipe: $g_x = 0$

The equation reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \cdot \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_x}{dr}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Pressure gradient:**
Since $v_x = v_x(r)$ only and the equation is satisfied for all $r$, the pressure gradient must be a constant:

$$\frac{dp}{dx} = \frac{p_2 - p_1}{L} = -\frac{\Delta p}{L} \quad (\text{constant, negative})$$

**Step 6 — Solve the ODE:**

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_x}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

Multiply by $r$ and integrate once:

$$r \frac{dv_x}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} r^2 + C_1$$

Divide by $r$:

$$\frac{dv_x}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} r + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

**Step 7 — Boundary condition at $r = 0$:** Velocity must be finite, so $C_1 = 0$ (otherwise the second term diverges at the centerline).

Integrate again:

$$v_x(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} r^2 + C_2$$

**Step 8 — Apply no-slip at the wall:** $v_x(R) = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} R^2 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} R^2$$

**Step 9 — Final velocity profile:**

$$v_x(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(R^2 - r^2\right) = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

This is a **parabolic** (Poiseuille) profile with maximum velocity at the centerline ($r = 0$):

$$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta p \, R^2}{4 \mu L} = \frac{\Delta p \, D^2}{16 \mu L}$$

**Step 10 — Volumetric flow rate (Hagen–Poiseuille Equation):**

$$Q = \int_0^R v_x(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\int_0^R (R^2 - r^2)\, r\, dr$$

$$= \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 \mu L} = \frac{\pi D^4 \Delta p}{128 \mu L}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the D⁴ dependence:** The volumetric flow rate is **extremely sensitive** to vessel diameter. Halving the diameter reduces flow to 1/16 of the original. This is the biophysical basis for the dramatic downstream resistance changes in **stenotic arteries** and for the **Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect** in microvessels.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Velocity Profile for a Power-Law Fluid in a Pipe (Non-Newtonian Generalization)

**Goal:** Generalize the parabolic profile to a power-law fluid.

**Step 1 — Start from force balance on a cylindrical element of fluid of radius $r$:**

The shear stress at radius $r$ is given by the power-law model (here, the shear stress at the pipe wall is the maximum):

$$\tau_{rx} = -K\left|\frac{dv_x}{dr}\right|^{n-1}\frac{dv_x}{dr}$$

**Step 2 — Force balance:**
The pressure force on the cross-section $\pi r^2$ must equal the shear force on the cylindrical surface $2\pi r L$:

$$\Delta p \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau_{rx} \cdot 2\pi r L$$

$$\tau_{rx} = \frac{\Delta p \, r}{2 L}$$

**Step 3 — Equate with the power-law model:**

$$-K\left(\frac{-dv_x}{dr}\right)^{n-1}\frac{dv_x}{dr} \cdot (-1) = \frac{\Delta p \, r}{2 L}$$

Since $v_x$ decreases with $r$ (maximum at center, zero at wall), $-dv_x/dr > 0$, and the power-law gives:

$$K \left(-\frac{dv_x}{dr}\right)^{n} = \frac{\Delta p \, r}{2 L}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the velocity gradient:**

$$-\frac{dv_x}{dr} = \left(\frac{\Delta p \, r}{2 K L}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $r$ to $R$ with $v_x(R) = 0$:**

$$v_x(r) = \int_r^R \left(\frac{\Delta p}{2 K L}\right)^{1/n} s^{1/n} ds = \left(\frac{\Delta p}{2 K L}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1}\left(R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Final velocity profile:**

$$\boxed{v_x(r) = \left(\frac{\Delta p}{2 K L}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} \cdot R^{(n+1)/n} \left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{(n+1)/n}\right]}$$

**Step 7 — Limiting cases:**
- For $n = 1$ (Newtonian), $K = \mu$, the profile reduces to the parabolic Poiseuille profile.
- For $n \to 0$ (highly shear-thinning, e.g., Bingham plastic limit), the exponent $(n+1)/n \to \infty$ and the profile becomes a **plug** (flat velocity across the center with thin shear zone near the wall). This is the **plug-flow** behavior characteristic of **blood in large vessels** and **slurries**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Flow Behaviors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** This kind of comparison table is precisely the type of structured answer that earns full 14-mark credit in an ESE (End Semester Exam) Part B question.

| Property | Newtonian (e.g., water) | Pseudoplastic (e.g., blood) | Dilatant (e.g., cornstarch) | Bingham Plastic (e.g., toothpaste) |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Constitutive equation | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$, $n<1$ | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$, $n>1$ | $\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ |
| Apparent viscosity | Constant | Decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$ | Increases with $\dot{\gamma}$ | Constant above $\tau_0$ |
| Yield stress | Zero | Zero | Zero | Finite $\tau_0$ |
| Velocity profile in pipe | Parabolic | Blunter than parabolic | Sharper than parabolic | Plug-like |
| Effect of increasing pressure gradient | Proportional increase in flow | Disproportionately large flow (low $n$) | Disproportionately small flow | Flow begins only after $\tau > \tau_0$ |
| Biomedical example | Water, plasma, saline | Whole blood, mucus, synovial fluid | Not common in body | None directly; clotted blood approx. |
| Storage/flow behavior | Linear, no memory | Rate-dependent, no memory | Rate-dependent, no memory | Threshold-dependent |
