## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Mark Distribution (Module 1, this topic):** This topic typically contributes 7–14 marks (out of 100) in a full-syllabus ESE paper. Part A questions are 3 marks each, Part B are 14 marks each with **internal choice** (you attempt ONE of two alternatives). Bloom's levels tested: **L2 (Understand)**, **L3 (Apply)**, **L4 (Analyze)**.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define a Newtonian fluid. Give two examples and state Newton's law of viscosity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Newtonian fluid is one for which the shear stress is directly proportional to the rate of shear deformation (shear rate), with the constant of proportionality — the dynamic viscosity $\mu$ — being independent of the shear rate. Mathematically:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Examples (any two):** Water, air, glycerol, ethanol, blood plasma, kerosene, gasoline, light oils, mercury.

**Newton's Law of Viscosity Statement:** *The shear stress between adjacent fluid layers is proportional to the negative velocity gradient normal to the direction of flow.* For a Newtonian fluid, $\mu$ is constant at a given temperature and pressure.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Mentioning "constant of proportionality independent of shear rate" earns the full 3 marks. Drawing a straight line through the origin on a $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ plot would earn a bonus mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**What is a non-Newtonian fluid? Classify non-Newtonian fluids with one biomedical example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A non-Newtonian fluid does not obey Newton's law of viscosity — its apparent viscosity $\mu_a$ depends on shear rate, time of shearing, or prior deformation history.

**Classification with one biomedical/real example:**

1. **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning, $n < 1$):** Viscosity decreases with shear rate. Example: **whole blood** (red cells deform and align with flow at high shear).
2. **Dilatant (shear-thickening, $n > 1$):** Viscosity increases with shear rate. Example: **concentrated synovial fluid suspensions** (rare in vivo; more common in industrial slurries).
3. **Bingham Plastic:** Has a yield stress $\tau_0$. Example: **toothpaste, fresh blood clot.**
4. **Thixotropic:** Viscosity decreases over time at constant shear. Example: **mucus, cytoplasm.**
5. **Viscoelastic:** Combines viscous and elastic behavior. Example: **saliva, synovial fluid, DNA solution, arterial wall tissue.**

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** Most students write only 3 categories. Listing at least 4 with distinct biomedical examples fetches full marks.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Regulations:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with two sub-parts typically of 7 marks each. You are given an **internal choice** — answer EITHER "Question A" OR "Question B" in full. Read both choices before deciding.

---

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 3]**

**(a) Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for the steady, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular pipe. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**(b) A catheter of inner diameter 2.0 mm and length 30 cm is used to deliver a saline solution (viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) at a flow rate of 5 mL/min. Determine the pressure drop across the catheter and the Reynolds number of the flow. Comment on the flow regime. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks** (Refer to **Derivation 3 in Section 3** above for the complete step-by-step proof.)

**Assumptions to state explicitly (1 mark):**
1. Steady flow
2. Incompressible fluid (constant $\rho$)
3. Newtonian fluid (constant $\mu$)
4. Laminar, fully developed flow
5. Horizontal pipe (no gravity component along axis)
6. No-slip at the wall
7. Axisymmetric flow

**Governing equation (1 mark):** Axial component of Navier–Stokes in cylindrical coordinates:

$$0 = -\frac{dp}{dx} + \mu \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \frac{dv_x}{dr}\right)$$

**Integration steps (3 marks):** Show all the algebraic steps from the second-order ODE to the velocity profile:

$$v_x(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

**Volumetric flow rate (2 marks):** Integrate $Q = \int_0^R v_x \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$ to get:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8\mu L} = \frac{\pi D^4 \Delta p}{128 \mu L}$$

---

**(b) — 7 marks** Numerical problem.

**Given:**
- $D = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 0.30$ m
- $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $Q = 5$ mL/min = $\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{60}$ m³/s = $8.33 \times 10^{-8}$ m³/s

**Step 1 — Mean velocity:**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-8}}{\frac{\pi}{4}(2.0\times10^{-3})^2} = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-8}}{3.142 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.0265 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.0265 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.053}{0.001} = 53$$

**Step 3 — Pressure drop (Hagen–Poiseuille, applicable since $Re = 53 \ll 2300$):**

$$\Delta p = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4} = \frac{128 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.30 \times 8.33\times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times (2.0\times 10^{-3})^4}$$

Compute denominator: $\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-11} = 5.027 \times 10^{-11}$ m⁴.

Compute numerator: $128 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.30 \times 8.33 \times 10^{-8} = 3.20 \times 10^{-9}$.

$$\Delta p = \frac{3.20 \times 10^{-9}}{5.027 \times 10^{-11}} = 63.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 64 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Flow regime comment:**
$Re = 53$ is well below the critical value of 2300, hence the flow is **firmly laminar** with a parabolic velocity profile. Hagen–Poiseuille law is valid; no entrance length correction is needed for a 30 cm tube at this Re.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Q(b):**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** $Q = 5$ mL/min must be converted to m³/s. Many students forget this, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to check laminar assumption:** Computing $\Delta p$ using Hagen–Poiseuille without verifying $Re < 2300$ costs 1 mark. Always **compute Re first**, then justify the choice of friction-factor formula.
> 3. **Final answer without comment:** Stating the flow regime is mandatory. "Flow is laminar because $Re = 53$" is the required concluding statement.

---

#### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 4]**

**(a) Define the Reynolds number and explain its physical significance. Discuss the criteria for distinguishing laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow in a circular pipe. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a pseudoplastic (power-law) fluid with consistency index $K = 0.5$ Pa·sⁿ and flow behavior index $n = 0.4$, flowing in a pipe of diameter 4 cm and length 2 m under a pressure drop of 10 kPa, determine (i) the apparent viscosity at the wall, (ii) the maximum velocity at the pipe centerline, and (iii) the volumetric flow rate. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Reynolds number is a dimensionless quantity representing the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

where $v$ is mean velocity, $D$ is pipe diameter, $\rho$ is density, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $\nu = \mu/\rho$ is kinematic viscosity.

**Physical significance (3 marks):**
- $Re$ is a **measure of the relative dominance** of momentum convection (inertia) versus momentum diffusion (viscosity).
- Low $Re$ → viscous forces damp out disturbances → smooth, laminar flow.
- High $Re$ → inertial forces amplify disturbances → flow transitions to turbulence.
- It is the **scaling parameter** that allows laboratory experiments (e.g., in wind tunnels or water flumes) to predict behavior of full-scale systems.

**Flow regime criteria for circular pipes (2 marks):**

| Regime | Criterion | Physical Character |
|--------|-----------|--------------------|
| Laminar | $Re < 2300$ | Streamlines parallel, no radial mixing |
| Transitional | $2300 \le Re \le 4000$ | Intermittent, unstable |
| Turbulent | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic, eddies, vigorous mixing |

The values 2300 and 4000 are approximate; the exact transition depends on inlet conditions, pipe roughness, and external disturbances.

---

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $K = 0.5$ Pa·sⁿ, $n = 0.4$
- $D = 0.04$ m → $R = 0.02$ m
- $L = 2$ m
- $\Delta p = 10{,}000$ Pa

**Step 1 — Wall shear stress (force balance on fluid cylinder of radius $R$):**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta p \cdot R}{2 L} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 0.02}{2 \times 2} = 50 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Wall shear rate from the power-law model:**
The power-law model gives $\tau_w = K \dot{\gamma}_w^n$, so:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \left(\frac{\tau_w}{K}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{50}{0.5}\right)^{1/0.4} = (100)^{2.5}$$

Compute: $\log_{10}(100) = 2$, so $\log_{10}(100^{2.5}) = 5$, hence $100^{2.5} = 10^5 = 100{,}000$ s⁻¹.

**Step 3 — Apparent viscosity at the wall (Part i):**

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_w} = K \dot{\gamma}_w^{n-1} = 0.5 \times (10^5)^{-0.6} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

Compute: $10^{-0.6} = 10^{-3/5} = 1/10^{0.6}$. Since $10^{0.6} \approx 3.98$, $10^{-0.6} \approx 0.2512$. So $10^{-3} = 10^{-0.6} \times 10^{-2.4} = 0.2512 \times 3.98 \times 10^{-3} \approx 10^{-3}$. Hence:

$$\mu_a \approx 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum velocity at centerline (Part ii):**
From the power-law velocity profile:

$$v_{max} = \frac{n}{n+1} \cdot R \cdot \dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{0.4}{1.4} \times 0.02 \times 10^5 = 0.2857 \times 0.02 \times 10^5$$

$$v_{max} = 0.2857 \times 2000 = 571.4 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **High-Speed Sanity Check:** A centerline velocity of 571 m/s is **physically unrealistic** for a biological or industrial pipe flow. This signals to the examiner that the student has applied the formula mechanically without dimensional checking. The KTU examiner will deduct 1 mark for **not commenting on physical plausibility** or for not sanity-checking the result.

**Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate (Part iii):**

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^3 \dot{\gamma}_w}{3 + 1/n} = \frac{\pi \times (0.02)^3 \times 10^5}{3 + 2.5} = \frac{3.142 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^5}{5.5}$$

$$= \frac{3.142 \times 0.8}{5.5} = \frac{2.514}{5.5} = 0.457 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

This too is unrealistically high. The exercise demonstrates that **non-Newtonian pseudoplastic behavior with very low $n$ can produce extreme velocity/flow rates for a modest pressure drop** when the consistency index is also moderate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Q(b):**
> 1. **Sign error in $\Delta p$:** Use the magnitude $|\Delta p| = 10$ kPa; the sign is automatically absorbed.
> 2. **Incorrect formula for $v_{max}$:** Students often confuse $v_{max} = \dfrac{n}{n+1} R \dot{\gamma}_w$ with the Newtonian form $v_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta p R^2}{4\mu L}$. For a non-Newtonian fluid, **use the power-law expression**.
> 3. **Forgetting the $1/n$ exponent:** The formula for $Q$ in a power-law fluid is $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^3}{3+1/n}\left(\dfrac{R \Delta p}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}$. Missing the $1/n$ term costs 2 marks.
> 4. **No units in final answer:** Always state units. $Q$ in m³/s or L/s, $\mu_a$ in Pa·s, $v_{max}$ in m/s.

---

### 5.3 KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP COMMON MISTAKES — What Loses Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Newtonian" with "viscous":** ALL fluids have viscosity; "Newtonian" specifically means viscosity is **shear-rate independent**. Writing "Newtonian fluids are viscous" is technically correct but misses the defining criterion.
> 2. **Forgetting the $D^4$ dependence in Hagen–Poiseuille:** This is the single most famous scaling in cardiovascular physiology. Examiners love to test it.
> 3. **Mixing up the Fanning ($f$) and Darcy ($f_D$) friction factors:** They differ by a factor of 4: $f_D = 4 f$. KTU typically uses the Fanning factor unless stated otherwise.
> 4. **Treating blood as Newtonian:** It is a **non-Newtonian** pseudoplastic fluid (with a small Casson yield stress at very low shear). Examiners deduct marks for treating it as Newtonian.
> 5. **Citing the wrong critical $Re$:** It is $2300$ (laminar-turbulent transition onset) and $4000$ (fully turbulent) for **internal pipe flow**, not for external flow over flat plates ($Re_x \sim 5\times10^5$).
> 6. **Forgetting dimensional consistency:** A classic KTU trap — using inconsistent units (mixing cP, mm, mL/min) in numerical problems.
> 7. **Skipping the "why":** KTU 2024 scheme (OBE/NEP 2020) emphasizes **understanding**, not rote. Always answer "why" a particular formula applies (e.g., "Hagen–Poiseuille applies because $Re = 53 \ll 2300$").
> 8. **Missing the biomedical context:** In a course titled *Biomedical Transport Phenomena*, answers that **connect theory to body physiology** earn higher marks. E.g., "the $D^4$ dependence explains why even small atherosclerotic narrowing in arteries dramatically reduces downstream perfusion."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final-Revision Flash Card for KTU 2024 ESE — Read this on exam morning.**

- ✅ **Newtonian fluid:** $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$; viscosity constant; $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a straight line through origin; examples: water, air, plasma.
- ✅ **Non-Newtonian fluid:** viscosity depends on shear rate / time / history.
- ✅ **Pseudoplastic** (shear-thinning): $n < 1$ in $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^n$; **blood** is the key biomedical example.
- ✅ **Dilatant** (shear-thickening): $n > 1$; cornstarch suspension.
- ✅ **Bingham plastic:** yield stress $\tau_0$, then linear flow; toothpaste, drilling mud.
- ✅ **Casson model:** specialized for blood at low shear rates; $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$.
- ✅ **Herschel–Bulkley:** yield stress + power-law post-yield.
- ✅ **Apparent viscosity:** $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$; not a true material constant.
- ✅ **Viscoelastic:** viscous + elastic; Maxwell, Oldroyd-B models; examples: saliva, synovial fluid, arterial wall.
- ✅ **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho v D / \mu$; ratio of inertial to viscous forces; **dimensionless**.
- ✅ **Critical Re (pipe):** $2300$ (laminar–transitional) and $4000$ (transitional–turbulent).
- ✅ **Laminar:** smooth, streamlines, viscous-dominated, predictable; Hagen–Poiseuille valid.
- ✅ **Turbulent:** chaotic, eddies, inertia-dominated, requires Moody-chart friction factor.
- ✅ **Hagen–Poiseuille:** $Q = \pi D^4 \Delta p / (128 \mu L)$; **valid only for steady, laminar, Newtonian, fully developed flow in a straight circular pipe**.
- ✅ **D⁴ dependence:** doubling $D$ → 16× flow; physiologically crucial in stenosis.
- ✅ **Power-law velocity profile:** $u(r) = u_{max}\!\left[1 - (r/R)^{(n+1)/n}\right]$; plug-like for $n \to 0$, parabolic for $n=1$.
- ✅ **Power-law flow rate:** $Q = (\pi D^3 / 32) \cdot [n/(3n+1)] \cdot (D \Delta p / 4 L K)^{1/n}$.
- ✅ **Navier–Stokes (incompressible):** $\rho(D\vec{v}/Dt) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}$.
- ✅ **Biomedical relevance of Re:**
  - Aorta at peak systole: $Re \sim 4000$–$12000$ → **turbulent** (audible murmur in stenosis).
  - Arteries: $Re \sim 500$–$2000$ → **laminar**.
  - Arterioles: $Re \sim 0.01$–$1$ → firmly **laminar**, viscous-dominated.
  - Capillaries: $Re \sim 10^{-3}$ → **creeping (Stokes) flow**; Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect reduces apparent viscosity.
- ✅ **Womersley number** $\alpha = R \sqrt{\omega / \nu}$ governs pulsatile flow; relevant for arterial hemodynamics.
- ✅ **Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect:** apparent blood viscosity **decreases** in tubes smaller than ~300 $\mu$m due to RBC migration away from walls.
- ✅ **Master equations to memorize cold:** Newton's law, power-law model, Reynolds number, Hagen–Poiseuille, Navier–Stokes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rheology of Blood Flow: Large Vessels, Small Tubes, and Capillaries, Regulation of Blood Flow, Flow in Branching Vessels, Flow in Specific Arteries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT863 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rheology of Blood Flow: Large Vessels, Small Tubes, and Capillaries, Regulation of Blood Flow, Flow in Branching Vessels, Flow in Specific Arteries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Rheology of Blood?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Rheology** is the branch of continuum mechanics that studies the deformation and flow behavior of matter, particularly the flow of liquids that are not purely Newtonian. The **Rheology of Blood** specifically describes the non-Newtonian flow characteristics of blood arising from its complex nature as a suspension of deformable red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), and platelets suspended in plasma.

Blood is a **non-Newtonian, shear-thinning (pseudoplastic), thixotropic fluid** because of the following constituents:
- **Plasma** (~55% by volume): A Newtonian fluid, water-like matrix containing proteins (albumin, globulin, fibrinogen).
- **Erythrocytes / RBCs** (~45% by volume): Biconcave, deformable cells (~7–8 µm diameter) that aggregate at low shear and deform/orient at high shear.
- **Leukocytes (WBCs)** and **Platelets**: Negligible volumetric contribution but important for microcirculation.

The **Hematocrit (Hct)** is the volume fraction of RBCs in blood, with a normal physiological value of **~45%** in adult humans.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Ketchup in a Bottle":**
> Imagine trying to pour a thick bottle of ketchup. At first, nothing comes out (it behaves like a solid), but once you apply a strong shake (shear stress), it suddenly flows easily. This is **shear-thinning** behavior.
> 
> **Blood behaves similarly:**
> - When the heart pumps vigorously (high shear in arteries), RBCs align and elongate, and blood flows easily.
> - In stagnant regions (low shear in veins), RBCs stack into **rouleaux** (coin-like rolls), increasing effective viscosity.
> 
> In **tiny capillaries** (diameter ~ 5–10 µm, smaller than the RBC itself), cells must squeeze through in **single file**, almost touching the wall — making the apparent viscosity *decrease* again, an effect called the **Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Blood Vessels

| Vessel Type | Approximate Diameter | Wall Structure | Flow Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Large Arteries** (Aorta, Carotid) | > 3 mm | Thick, elastic, muscular | Pulsatile, inertial, turbulent at peaks |
| **Medium Arteries** (Renal, Femoral) | 0.1 – 3 mm | Muscular, elastic lamina | Pulsatile, transitional |
| **Arterioles** | 10 – 100 µm | Smooth muscle dominant | Steady, viscous (Re < 1) |
| **Capillaries** | 5 – 10 µm | Single endothelial layer | Single-file RBCs, low Re |
| **Venules & Veins** | 10 µm – 5 mm | Thin, compliant | Low pressure, possible backflow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Blood density **ρ ≈ 1060 kg/m³**
> - Plasma viscosity **μp ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
> - Whole blood viscosity (large vessel) **μ ≈ 3.5 × 10⁻³ – 4.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
> - Heart pulse frequency **f ≈ 1.2 Hz** (72 bpm)
> - Cardiac output **CO ≈ 5 L/min** at rest

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Blood is Non-Newtonian — Structural Reasoning

The non-Newtonian behavior emerges from three coupled micro-structural phenomena:

1. **RBC Aggregation (Rouleaux Formation):**
   At low shear rates (γ̇ < 50 s⁻¹), RBCs clump together into stacked coin-like structures called *rouleaux*, mediated by plasma proteins (especially **fibrinogen**). This increases apparent viscosity.

2. **RBC Deformation and Orientation:**
   At high shear rates (γ̇ > 100 s⁻¹), RBCs elongate, orient their long axis parallel to the flow, and reduce flow resistance — lowering apparent viscosity.

3. **Plasma Skimming and Marginal Layer:**
   Near vessel walls, a cell-depleted plasma layer of thickness ~2–5 µm forms, providing a "lubricating" zone that reduces wall friction.

---

## 2.2 Constitutive Rheological Models for Blood

### (a) Newtonian Model (Inadequate for blood, but used for plasma)
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

### (b) Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Model
$$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dr}\right)^{n}$$

- **n < 1** → Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic). For blood, **n ≈ 0.6 – 0.8**.
- **K** → Consistency index (Pa·sⁿ).

### (c) Casson Model (Most widely used for blood)
$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \cdot \frac{du}{dr}}$$

- **τy** → Yield stress (~0.005 – 0.05 Pa).
- **μ∞** → Viscosity at infinite shear rate (~0.0035 Pa·s).

### (d) Carreau Model (Best for whole shear range)
$$\mu_{app} = \mu_\infty + (\mu_0 - \mu_\infty)\left[1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2\right]^{(n-1)/2}$$

Where **μ₀** is zero-shear viscosity, **μ∞** is infinite-shear viscosity, **λ** is a time constant.

---

## 2.3 Flow in Large Vessels — Poiseuille Flow with Pulsatility

For a **rigid, straight, cylindrical tube** (approximation for large arteries if averaged over a cycle), the **Hagen–Poiseuille** equation gives steady volumetric flow:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

Where:
- **R** = vessel radius
- **ΔP** = pressure drop over length **L**
- **μ** = dynamic viscosity

**Velocity profile** (parabolic):
$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

**Wall Shear Stress (WSS):**
$$\tau_w = \frac{R \Delta P}{2 L} = \frac{4 \mu \bar{u}}{R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Observation:** Wall shear stress in large vessels is **~1.5 – 2.0 Pa**, which is critical for **endothelial cell mechanotransduction** — too low causes atherosclerosis, too high causes damage.

**Pulsatility in Arteries — Womersley Number:**
The dimensionless **Womersley number (α)** quantifies the ratio of transient inertial forces to viscous forces:

$$\alpha = R \sqrt{\frac{\omega \rho}{\mu}}$$

Where **ω = 2πf** is the angular frequency of the heart beat.

- **α < 1** → Viscous-dominated (quasi-steady, Poiseuille-like). True in capillaries and small arterioles.
- **α > 1** → Inertia-dominated, blunted "plug-flow" profile, with phase lag between pressure and flow. True in aorta (α ≈ 10–20).

---

## 2.4 Flow in Small Tubes — The Fahraeus–Lindqvist Effect

In tubes with diameter **D < ~300 µm**, the apparent viscosity of blood **decreases** with decreasing diameter — counter-intuitive from the macroscopic view.

$$\mu_{app}(D) = \mu_\infty \left[1 + \left(\frac{D_0}{D}\right)^2\right]$$

Where **D₀ ≈ 30 – 50 µm** is a characteristic dimension.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Origin:** As vessel diameter shrinks toward RBC size, RBCs migrate axially (the **Segre–Silberberg effect**), leaving a cell-free plasma sleeve near the wall. The "effective tube" carrying RBCs is now narrower, and the wall is bathed in low-viscosity plasma, reducing overall resistance.

**Tube Hematocrit (HctT) vs Discharge Hematocrit (HctD):**
$$Hct_T \approx Hct_D \times \left[1 - e^{-(D - D_{min})/k}\right]$$

The tube hematocrit is always lower than the discharge (feed) hematocrit.

---

## 2.5 Flow in Capillaries — Single-File Erythrocyte Motion

In capillaries (D = 5–10 µm), each RBC deforms into a **parachute / bullet shape** and traverses in single file. The flowing RBC nearly fills the lumen.

**Apparent viscosity in capillaries** is very low (~0.0015 – 0.002 Pa·s) because:
- RBC deformation reduces resistance.
- Plasma gap between cell and endothelium is the lubrication layer.
- The endothelial surface layer (glycocalyx, ~0.5–1 µm thick) further reduces friction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In vivo effective viscosity** in capillaries is approximately equal to plasma viscosity. This is why the **Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect** continues into the capillary range.

---

## 2.6 Regulation of Blood Flow

| Mechanism | Location | Function | Time Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Myogenic (Bayliss effect)** | Arterioles | Smooth muscle contracts in response to ↑ pressure | Seconds |
| **Metabolic** | Arterioles, capillaries | ↑ CO₂, ↓ O₂, ↑ H⁺, adenosine, K⁺ → vasodilation | Seconds – minutes |
| **Endothelial (NO, endothelin)** | All vessels | Nitric oxide (NO) → vasodilation; endothelin → vasoconstriction | Seconds |
| **Neurogenic (Sympathetic)** | Arterioles, large arteries | α-adrenergic → vasoconstriction; β-adrenergic → vasodilation (skeletal muscle) | < 1 s |
| **Humoral (Hormonal)** | Whole system | Adrenaline, noradrenaline, vasopressin, angiotensin II | Minutes – hours |

**Poiseuille's Law Combined with Active Regulation:**
$$Q = \frac{\pi R(x,t)^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

The radius **R** is now a *time- and location-dependent* control variable. A 2-fold change in radius yields a **16-fold change** in flow, making arteriolar radius the principal regulator.

**Autoregulation Equation (Bayliss):**
$$R(t) = R_0 \left[1 + k(P(t) - P_0)\right]$$

---

## 2.7 Flow in Branching Vessels — Murray's Law and Bifurcation Analysis

At each bifurcation, the parent vessel (radius **r₀**) splits into two daughters (**r₁** and **r₂**).

### Murray's Law (Minimum Work Hypothesis, 1926):
$$r_0^3 = r_1^3 + r_2^3$$

This minimizes the total power expenditure (pumping cost + metabolic cost of blood volume). The optimal **diameter ratio** for a symmetric bifurcation is:

$$\frac{r_1}{r_0} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1/3} \approx 0.794$$

So the **area ratio** (daughter area / parent area) is:
$$\frac{2 r_1^2}{r_0^2} = 2 \times (0.794)^2 \approx 1.26$$

This area ratio > 1 causes a **flow disturbance** at the bifurcation — the basis for the development of endothelial shear gradients and the preferential localization of atherosclerotic plaques at bifurcations.

### Pressure Drop Across a Bifurcation:
$$\Delta P_{bif} = K \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2$$

Where **K** is a loss coefficient (typically 0.5 – 1.5 depending on bifurcation angle).

### Optimal Bifurcation Angle (Cohn's formula):
$$\cos \theta_1 = \frac{r_0^4 + r_1^4 - r_2^4}{2 r_0^2 r_1^2}$$

---

## 2.8 Flow in Specific Arteries (Examples)

### (a) Coronary Arteries
- Perfusion during **diastole** (compression during systole).
- Intramyocardial pressure causes time-varying extravascular compression.
- **Critical stenosis** at ~70% diameter reduction restricts flow.

### (b) Cerebral Arteries
- Protected by **autoregulation** between mean arterial pressures 60 – 150 mmHg.
- Located on the **Circle of Willis** at the base of the brain — a network of collateral arteries providing redundancy.

### (c) Renal Arteries
- Two capillary beds in series (glomerular and peritubular).
- **Autoregulation** is highly developed (40 – 200 mmHg).
- **Myogenic response** dominant.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — KTU Board Exam Ready**

| # | Formula | Application | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | Volumetric flow (Poiseuille) | R [m], ΔP [Pa], μ [Pa·s], L [m] |
| 2 | $u(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$ | Parabolic velocity profile | u [m/s], r [m] |
| 3 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2L} = \dfrac{4\mu \bar{u}}{R}$ | Wall shear stress | τw [Pa] |
| 4 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{2 \rho Q}{\pi R \mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\alpha = R \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \rho}{\mu}}$ | Womersley number | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\mu_{app}(D) = \mu_\infty \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{D_0}{D}\right)^2\right]$ | Fahraeus–Lindqvist | D [m] |
| 7 | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ | Casson model | τ [Pa], γ̇ [s⁻¹] |
| 8 | $r_0^3 = r_1^3 + r_2^3$ | Murray's bifurcation law | r [m] |
| 9 | $\cos \theta_1 = \dfrac{r_0^4 + r_1^4 - r_2^4}{2 r_0^2 r_1^2}$ | Cohn's bifurcation angle | θ [rad] |
| 10 | $H = f\left(\frac{\rho v^2}{2}, \frac{\mu v}{D}\right)$ | Head loss | H [m] |
| 11 | $P_{pump} = Q \Delta P$ | Cardiac power output | P [W] |
| 12 | $C = \dfrac{Q}{A\sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}}$ | Stenosis pressure-flow coefficient | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations power the design of **LVADs (Left Ventricular Assist Devices)**, **coronary stents**, **vascular grafts**, and **computer fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations** for surgical planning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hagen–Poiseuille Flow (Cylindrical Coordinates)

We start from the **Navier–Stokes equation** for steady, fully-developed, axisymmetric, incompressible flow in a horizontal pipe:

### Step 1 — Define the velocity field
- Steady, fully-developed, axial only:
$$\vec{V} = (0, 0, u_z(r))$$
- Continuity is automatically satisfied: $\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}(r v_r) + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$ → satisfied.

### Step 2 — Reduce the z-momentum equation
The Navier–Stokes z-momentum equation in cylindrical coordinates:

$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial z^2}\right] + \rho g_z$$

### Step 3 — Apply the simplifications
- Steady: $\partial v_z / \partial t = 0$
- Fully developed: $\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$
- Axisymmetric: $\partial v_z / \partial \theta = 0$
- $v_r = v_\theta = 0$

The equation reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply constant pressure gradient
For fully developed flow, $\partial P / \partial z$ is a constant. Let:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \frac{P_0 - P_L}{L} = \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

(The pressure decreases linearly along the pipe.)

Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{du}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

### Step 5 — Integrate once
Multiply both sides by r and integrate:

$$r \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} r^2 + C_1$$

$$\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} r + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the regularity condition at r = 0
The velocity gradient cannot be infinite at the centerline (physical requirement), so **C₁ = 0**.

$$\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} r$$

### Step 7 — Integrate again
$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} r^2 + C_2$$

### Step 8 — Apply no-slip boundary condition at wall: u(R) = 0
$$0 = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} R^2 + C_2 \Rightarrow C_2 = -\frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 9 — Final velocity profile
$$\boxed{u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)}$$

This is the **parabolic Poiseuille profile**.

### Step 10 — Compute volumetric flow rate
$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}\int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}\left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}\left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right]$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}}$$

### Step 11 — Maximum velocity (at r = 0) and mean velocity
$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}, \quad \bar{u} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{8\mu L} = \frac{u_{max}}{2}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Casson Equation Velocity Profile

Starting from the **Casson constitutive equation**:

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$$

For fully developed flow in a tube, the shear stress varies linearly with r:

$$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{2L} r$$

This is obtained by a force balance on a cylindrical fluid element. Let **τy** be reached at radius **r_y**:

$$\tau(r_y) = \tau_y \Rightarrow r_y = \frac{2 L \tau_y}{\Delta P}$$

For **r > r_y** (annular region), the fluid flows. For **r ≤ r_y** (core), the fluid moves as a rigid plug (since τ < τy).

The shear rate is:

$$\dot{\gamma} = -\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{1}{\mu_\infty}\left(\sqrt{\tau} - \sqrt{\tau_y}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{1}{\mu_\infty}\left(\sqrt{\tau} - \sqrt{\tau_y}\right)^2$$

Substituting $\tau = (\Delta P/2L) r$ and integrating from **r** to **R** (where u = 0):

$$u(r) = \frac{1}{\mu_\infty}\int_r^R \left[\sqrt{\frac{\Delta P \cdot s}{2L}} - \sqrt{\tau_y}\right]^2 ds$$

Carrying out the integration:

$$\boxed{u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu_\infty L}\left[R^2 - r^2\right] - \frac{2\sqrt{\tau_y}}{\mu_\infty}\left[\frac{\Delta P}{2L}\right]^{1/2}\left[R^{3/2} - r^{3/2}\right] + \frac{\tau_y}{\mu_\infty}\left[R - r\right]}$$

For the **central plug region (0 ≤ r ≤ r_y)**:
$$u(r) = u(r_y) = \text{constant plug velocity}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** The Casson profile is "blunted" at the center compared to Poiseuille — a major difference seen in experimental measurements of blood flow in arteries.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Murray's Law (Energy Minimization)

Consider a parent vessel of radius **r₀** splitting into **n** daughter vessels of radius **rᵢ**.

The total power required is the sum of the **pumping power** (proportional to flow × pressure drop) and the **metabolic power** (proportional to blood volume):

$$P_{total} = \underbrace{\sum_i \frac{8 \mu L_i Q_i^2}{\pi r_i^4}}_{\text{Pumping loss}} + \underbrace{k_m \sum_i \pi r_i^2 L_i}_{\text{Metabolic cost}}$$

For steady, mass-conserving flow with constant pressure drop per unit length and total length scaling as $L_i \propto r_i$:

$$P_{total} \propto \sum_i \left(\frac{Q_i^2}{r_i^3} + m r_i^3\right)$$

Minimizing with respect to **rᵢ** (treating Qᵢ as fixed):

$$\frac{\partial P_{total}}{\partial r_i} = 0 \Rightarrow -\frac{3 Q_i^2}{r_i^4} + 3 m r_i^2 = 0$$

$$\frac{Q_i}{r_i^3} = \sqrt{m} = \text{constant for all branches}$$

By continuity, $Q_i \propto r_i^3$ at each branch. For a bifurcation ($n=2$):
$$Q_0 = Q_1 + Q_2 \Rightarrow r_0^3 = r_1^3 + r_2^3$$

This is **Murray's Law**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Vessel Behavior Summary

| Vessel | D | μapp | Re | α | Flow Type | Dominant Force |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aorta | 25 mm | 3.5 mPa·s | 1500–3500 | 15–22 | Pulsatile, turbulent peaks | Inertia + pressure |
| Carotid | 5 mm | 3.5 mPa·s | 500 | 6 | Pulsatile | Inertia + viscous |
| Renal Artery | 3 mm | 3.5 mPa·s | 300 | 4 | Pulsatile | Inertia + viscous |
| Small Artery | 500 µm | 4 mPa·s | 80 | 2 | Pulsatile–transitional | Viscous emerging |
| Arteriole | 50 µm | 2.5 mPa·s | 0.5 | 0.3 | Steady | Viscous |
| Capillary | 8 µm | 1.5 mPa·s | 0.001 | 0.005 | Single-file RBC | Viscous (lubrication) |
| Venule | 30 µm | 2 mPa·s | 0.3 | — | Steady | Viscous |
| Vena Cava | 25 mm | 3.5 mPa·s | 800 | — | Steady, possible backflow | Inertia |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hemodynamic Regulation Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Central Nervous System] --> B[Sympathetic Nervous System]
    A --> C[Parasympathetic Vagus]
    B --> D[Adrenal Medulla]
    D --> E[Circulating Catecholamines]
    E --> F[Arteriolar Smooth Muscle]
    C --> G[Heart Rate Modulation]
    G --> H[Cardiac Output CO]
    F --> I[Systemic Vascular Resistance SVR]
    H --> J[Mean Arterial Pressure MAP]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Tissue Perfusion Pressure]
    K --> L[Local Metabolic Demand]
    L --> M[Endothelial NO Release]
    M --> N[Local Vasodilation]
    N --> O[Microvascular Flow Adjustment]
    L --> P[Adenosine, K+, H+ Accumulation]
    P --> N
    F --> Q[Myogenic Response]
    K --> Q
    Q --> O
    O --> R[Capillary Exchange]
    R --> S[Tissue Oxygen Delivery DO2]
```

## 4.2 Vessel Tree Hierarchy and Flow Regime

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Aorta 25mm] --> B[Large Artery 5mm]
    B --> C[Medium Artery 1mm]
    C --> D[Small Artery 200um]
    D --> E[Arteriole 50um]
    E --> F[Capillary 8um]
    F --> G[Venule 30um]
    G --> H[Small Vein 500um]
    H --> I[Vena Cava 25mm]
    A -- Re 3000 --> A
    B -- Re 500 --> B
    C -- Re 100 --> C
    D -- Re 10 --> D
    E -- Re 0.5 --> E
    F -- Re 0.001 --> F
    G -- Re 0.3 --> G
```

## 4.3 Bifurcation Geometry and Murray's Law Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Parent Vessel r0] --> B1[Bifurcation Apex]
    B1 --> D1[Daughter r1]
    B1 --> D2[Daughter r2]
    P --> P1[Flow Q0 in]
    P1 --> B1
    B1 --> F1[Flow Q1 out]
    B1 --> F2[Flow Q2 out]
    P -.Murray Condition.-> ML[r0^3 = r1^3 + r2^3]
    D1 -.Angle theta1.-> ML
    D2 -.Angle theta2.-> ML
    ML --> PR[Pressure Drop deltaP]
    PR --> SS[Wall Shear Stress Distribution]
    SS --> EN[Endothelial Response]
    EN --> AT[Atherogenic Site Selection]
```

## 4.4 Shear Rate–Viscosity Relationship (Rheogram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LR[Low Shear less than 50 s-1] --> AGG[RBC Aggregation Rouleaux]
    AGG --> HV[High Apparent Viscosity 10-100 mPa s]
    MS[Medium Shear 50 to 200 s-1] --> TRANS[Transition Deformation Begins]
    TRANS --> MV[Moderate Viscosity 4-6 mPa s]
    HS[High Shear greater than 200 s-1] --> DEF[Full RBC Deformation and Alignment]
    DEF --> LV[Low Viscosity 3-4 mPa s Approaching Newtonian Plateau]
```

## 4.5 Fahraeus–Lindqvist Effect Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LARGE[Large Vessel D greater than 300um] --> NORM[Normal Hematocrit Hct 45 percent]
    NORM --> V1[Apparent Viscosity 3.5 mPa s]
    SMALL[Small Vessel D less than 300um] --> MIG[RBC Axial Migration]
    MIG --> PL[Cell Free Plasma Layer 2 to 5 um]
    PL --> DROP[Reduced Apparent Viscosity]
    DROP --> V2[Apparent Viscosity 2 to 3 mPa s]
    CAP[Capillary D 5 to 10um] --> SF[Single File RBC Parachute Shape]
    SF --> GLY[Glycocalyx Lubrication]
    GLY --> V3[Apparent Viscosity 1.5 mPa s]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect. Explain its physical origin and its significance in microcirculation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect** describes the phenomenon in which the **apparent viscosity of blood decreases** as the vessel diameter decreases below approximately **300 µm**, contrary to expectations from macroscopic fluid mechanics. **[1 mark]**

**Physical Origin:** As the vessel diameter approaches the size of red blood cells (~7–8 µm), RBCs migrate toward the centerline (the **Segre–Silberberg effect**), leaving a thin, cell-depleted plasma layer adjacent to the vessel wall. This plasma layer acts as a lubrication zone, dramatically reducing the effective shear stress at the wall. **[1 mark]**

**Significance:** The effect lowers vascular resistance in the microcirculation, ensuring adequate perfusion of tissues despite the small vessel diameters. In capillaries, the apparent viscosity approaches that of pure plasma (~1.2 mPa·s), which is essential for efficient nutrient and gas exchange. **[1 mark]**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the Womersley number? What does it indicate about the flow regime in a blood vessel?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Womersley number (α)** is a dimensionless parameter defined as:

$$\alpha = R \sqrt{\frac{\omega \rho}{\mu}}$$

where R is the vessel radius, ω is the angular frequency of the pulsatile flow, ρ is blood density, and μ is viscosity. **[1 mark]**

It represents the **ratio of transient inertial forces to viscous forces** in pulsatile flow. **[1 mark]**

- When **α < 1**, viscous forces dominate, the velocity profile is quasi-steady and parabolic (Poiseuille-like). This is the case in small arterioles and capillaries.
- When **α > 1**, inertial forces dominate, the velocity profile is **blunted** and exhibits a **phase lag** with the pressure waveform. In the aorta, α ≈ 15–20, indicating strongly pulsatile flow. **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`
**(a)** Derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for steady, laminar, fully developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal cylindrical tube, starting from the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A blood vessel of radius **R = 2 mm** and length **L = 0.1 m** carries blood with a mean velocity of **ū = 0.2 m/s**. Blood viscosity is **μ = 3.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** and density is **ρ = 1060 kg/m³**. Calculate:
  - (i) Volumetric flow rate Q
  - (ii) Pressure drop ΔP
  - (iii) Wall shear stress τw
  - (iv) Reynolds number Re and comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions** (state 5–6):
1. Steady flow (∂/∂t = 0).
2. Incompressible, Newtonian fluid.
3. Laminar, axisymmetric flow.
4. Fully developed (∂vz/∂z = 0).
5. Unidirectional axial flow (vr = vθ = 0).
6. Rigid, horizontal, constant-diameter tube.
7. No body forces (gravity neglected or horizontal).

**Derivation steps** (summarized as required, full derivation given in §3.1):
- Reduce Navier–Stokes to: $\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{du}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\Delta P}{L}$ **(2 marks)**
- Integrate once, apply regularity at r=0, C₁=0. **(1 mark)**
- Integrate again, apply no-slip u(R)=0. **(1 mark)**
- Obtain parabolic profile: $u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$ **(1 mark)**
- Integrate to obtain $Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** R = 2 × 10⁻³ m, L = 0.1 m, ū = 0.2 m/s, μ = 3.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, ρ = 1060 kg/m³.

**(i) Volumetric flow rate:**
$$Q = \bar{u} \cdot A = \bar{u} \cdot \pi R^2 = 0.2 \times \pi \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2$$

$$Q = 0.2 \times \pi \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 2.513 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 2.51 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 2.51 \, \text{mL/s}}$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**(ii) Pressure drop:** From Poiseuille: $\Delta P = \frac{8\mu L Q}{\pi R^4}$

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1 \times 2.513 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^4}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{7.036 \times 10^{-9}}{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-11}} = \frac{7.036 \times 10^{-9}}{5.027 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 140 \, \text{Pa} \approx 1.05 \, \text{mmHg}}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Wall shear stress:**
$$\tau_w = \frac{4 \mu \bar{u}}{R} = \frac{4 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_w = \frac{2.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.4 \, \text{Pa}}$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**(iv) Reynolds number:**
$$Re = \frac{2 \rho \bar{u} R}{\mu} = \frac{2 \times 1060 \times 0.2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{0.848}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 242.3$$

$$\boxed{Re \approx 242}$$

**Comment:** Since Re << 2300 (the critical Reynolds number for pipe flow), the flow is firmly **laminar**. The Poiseuille assumption is justified. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**(a)** State and explain **Murray's law** for branching blood vessels. Derive the law using the principle of minimum work (pumping + metabolic power). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect**. Plot qualitatively the variation of apparent viscosity with vessel diameter for blood. Discuss the physiological significance. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Statement:** Murray's law states that at any bifurcation in the vascular tree, the cube of the parent vessel's radius equals the sum of the cubes of the daughter vessels' radii:
$$r_0^3 = r_1^3 + r_2^3 + \dots + r_n^3$$
For a symmetric bifurcation, $r_1/r_0 = (1/2)^{1/3} \approx 0.794$. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation:**
- Total power = Pumping power + Metabolic power:
$$P_{total} = \sum_i \left(\frac{8 \mu L_i Q_i^2}{\pi r_i^4}\right) + k_m \sum_i \pi r_i^2 L_i$$
- For a single branch with $L_i \propto r_i$ and $Q_i \propto r_i^3$ (continuity):
$$P_i = a \cdot \frac{r_i^6}{r_i^4} + b \cdot r_i^2 \cdot r_i = a r_i^2 + b r_i^3$$
- Minimizing $\partial P_i / \partial r_i = 2 a r_i + 3 b r_i^2 = 0$ gives a non-trivial solution only if r_i is optimized. **(2 marks)**
- Setting $\partial P_{total} / \partial r_i = 0$ for each branch:
$$-\frac{3 Q_i^2}{r_i^4} + 3 m r_i^2 = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{Q_i}{r_i^3} = \text{constant} = k$$
- For a bifurcation with $Q_0 = Q_1 + Q_2$ and $k r_0^3 = k r_1^3 + k r_2^3$:
$$\boxed{r_0^3 = r_1^3 + r_2^3}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Significance:**
- Explains the design of natural vascular trees.
- The diameter ratio of ~0.79 minimizes biological work.
- Deviations from Murray's law indicate pathological remodeling (e.g., in tumors, aneurysms). **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Sketch description** (no actual drawing — describe verbally):
- Plot μapp (y-axis) vs vessel diameter D (x-axis) on log-log or semi-log scale.
- For D > 300 µm: μapp ≈ constant (~3.5 mPa·s).
- For 10 µm < D < 300 µm: μapp decreases as D decreases.
- Minimum around D ≈ 8–10 µm.
- For D < 5–7 µm: μapp slightly rises (capillary inversion effect) but remains close to plasma viscosity. **(2 marks)**

**Physiological significance** (5 points, 5 marks):
1. **Reduced microvascular resistance** — blood flows easily in arterioles and capillaries, ensuring tissue perfusion.
2. **Plasma skimming** — explains why the hematocrit decreases at bifurcations and in small vessels.
3. **Protection of capillaries** — low effective viscosity prevents capillary damage.
4. **In vivo vs in vitro viscosity** — measured in vivo viscosity is much lower than in vitro at the same Hct, due to the effect.
5. **Clinical relevance** — abnormal Fahraeus–Lindqvist behavior is observed in diabetes, sickle cell disease, and malaria (rigidified RBCs). **(5 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid These in the Exam Hall:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units in Poiseuille derivations.** Convert mmHg ↔ Pa correctly: 1 mmHg = 133.32 Pa. Mixing units loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Treating blood as Newtonian in large vessels.** Although the deviation is small in large arteries, KTU examiners expect you to **state the assumption and acknowledge the non-Newtonian nature** of blood.
> 
> 3. **Confusing tube hematocrit (HctT) with discharge hematocrit (HctD).** They are not equal — HctT < HctD due to the Fåhræus effect.
> 
> 4. **Writing Murray's law as r² = r₁² + r₂².** This is **WRONG**. The correct exponent is **3 (cubic)**, not 2. The 2-power relation holds for electrical resistance analogy, NOT for blood vessels.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the plug flow region in the Casson model.** Examiners specifically test whether you understand that blood has a yield stress and a central plug in low-shear regions.
> 
> 6. **In bifurcation problems, forgetting the area ratio of ~1.26.** This is the basis for the "disturbed flow" region and atherogenic sites.
> 
> 7. **Womersley number is NOT the same as Reynolds number.** They are related but **not interchangeable**. KTU examiners will deduct marks if you use Re where α is required.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
> 
> **Rheology fundamentals:**
> - Blood = non-Newtonian, shear-thinning, thixotropic, with yield stress.
> - Hematocrit (Hct) ≈ 45% in adult males, 40% in females.
> - Plasma is Newtonian; non-Newtonian behavior comes from RBCs.
> - Three rheological models to remember: **Power-law, Casson, Carreau**.
> 
> **Large vessels (D > 3 mm):**
> - Hagen–Poiseuille flow: $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8\mu L)$.
> - Parabolic velocity profile, $u_{max} = 2\bar{u}$.
> - Wall shear stress: $\tau_w = 4\mu\bar{u}/R$ — critical for endothelial health.
> - Pulsatility characterized by **Womersley number α**; in aorta, α ≈ 15–20.
> - Re ~ 1500–3500 in aorta, peak systolic may be turbulent.
> 
> **Small tubes (D = 10 µm – 300 µm):**
> - **Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect**: μapp decreases with D.
> - RBC axial migration → plasma-rich marginal layer.
> - Tube hematocrit < discharge hematocrit.
> 
> **Capillaries (D = 5–10 µm):**
> - Single-file RBC flow, parachute/bullet shape.
> - Glycocalyx lubrication layer (~0.5–1 µm).
> - μapp approaches plasma viscosity.
> 
> **Regulation of blood flow:**
> - **Arteriolar radius is the principal control knob** (4th power dependence).
> - Mechanisms: myogenic, metabolic, endothelial (NO), neurogenic, humoral.
> - 2× radius change = 16× flow change.
> - Autoregulation range: 60–150 mmHg in brain, 40–200 mmHg in kidney.
> 
> **Branching vessels:**
> - **Murray's law**: $r_0^3 = r_1^3 + r_2^3$.
> - Optimal symmetric daughter/parent radius ratio = 0.794.
> - Area ratio ≈ 1.26 → disturbed flow at bifurcation.
> - Atherogenic sites are at bifurcations (low oscillating shear).
> 
> **Specific arteries:**
> - **Coronary**: Perfused during diastole; susceptible to stenosis.
> - **Cerebral**: Strong autoregulation; Circle of Willis provides redundancy.
> - **Renal**: Two capillary beds in series; myogenic autoregulation dominant.
> 
> **Key dimensionless numbers — don't confuse them:**
> - **Re** = ρvD/μ — ratio of inertial to viscous forces.
> - **α (Womersley)** = R√(ωρ/μ) — ratio of transient inertia to viscous forces.
> - **Stokes number** — ratio of particle response time to flow time.
> - **Dean number** — curvature effects in curved vessels.
> 
> **Memory hook — "Shear 3, Pressure 4":**
> - τw scales as 1/R (Poiseuille WSS).
> - ΔP scales as 1/R⁴ (Poiseuille flow).
> - This is why small arterioles are major resistance sites.

---

topics.title: Transvascular Transport: Introduction, Pathways for Trans-Endothelial Transport, Rates of Transvascular Transport, Osmotic Pressure, Starling's Law of Filtration, Kedem-Katchalsky Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT863 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transvascular Transport: Introduction, Pathways for Trans-Endothelial Transport, Rates of Transvascular Transport, Osmotic Pressure, Starling's Law of Filtration, Kedem-Katchalsky Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Transvascular Transport — Formal Definition

**Transvascular transport** is the physiological process by which fluids, dissolved solutes, gases, macromolecules, and plasma proteins cross the **endothelial cell barrier** that separates the **intravascular compartment** (blood plasma inside capillaries) from the **interstitial compartment** (interstitial fluid bathing the tissue cells). This exchange is fundamental to microcirculatory homeostasis, nutrient delivery, metabolic waste removal, and the maintenance of tissue fluid balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Transvascular transport is the cornerstone of microvascular exchange. The endothelial layer is *not* a passive wall — it is a selectively permeable, biologically active barrier whose transport properties vary with tissue type, pathological state, and mechanical stimuli (shear stress, pressure).

In quantitative terms, transvascular transport comprises three simultaneous transport modes:
1. **Convective (bulk) transport** — driven by hydraulic and osmotic pressure gradients.
2. **Diffusive (passive) transport** — driven by chemical potential (concentration) gradients.
3. **Active/vesicular transport** — pinocytosis, transcytosis, receptor-mediated endocytosis.

The net exchange rate is governed by the **Starling forces** (hydrostatic and oncotic pressures on both sides of the membrane) and the **Kedem–Katchalsky phenomenological coefficients** (hydraulic conductivity, solute permeability, and reflection coefficient).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Brick Wall with Toll Gates"

Imagine a long garden wall made of bricks. Between the bricks are **mortar gaps (paracellular clefts)**, and through some bricks are **punched-out holes (fenestrations)**. A water hose (blood) is pumping water against one side of the wall, while on the other side a sponge-filled garden (tissue) is soaking it up.

- The **water pressure** in the hose (capillary hydrostatic pressure) tries to push water through the gaps → **filtration**.
- The **dissolved salt/protein concentration difference** (oncotic pressure difference) pulls water back to retain volume → **reabsorption**.
- Small ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻) slip easily through the gaps (high permeability).
- Large proteins (albumin, **MW ≈ 69,000 Da**) are largely **rejected** and bounce back.

The wall is *leaky but selective* — it does not behave like a perfect semipermeable membrane, and a portion of the protein (or solute) leaks through. The fraction rejected is quantified by the **reflection coefficient (σ)**, a number between **0** (no rejection, fully leaky) and **1** (perfect semipermeable membrane, complete rejection).

## 1.3 Pathways for Trans-Endothelial Transport — Taxonomy

The endothelial barrier offers **four major routes** for solute and fluid movement:

| # | Pathway | Location | Typical Cargo | Governing Physics |
|---|---------|----------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Paracellular (intercellular clefts)** | Between adjacent endothelial cells | Water, small ions (< 1 nm), small solutes | Convection + diffusion through slit of width ≈ 6–7 nm |
| 2 | **Transcellular (through cell membrane)** | Across lipid bilayer of endothelial cell | Lipid-soluble gases (O₂, CO₂), small nonpolar molecules | Simple diffusion (Fick's law) |
| 3 | **Fenestrations** | Transcellular pores (in fenestrated capillaries of kidney, intestine, endocrine glands) | Water, small solutes | Pressure-driven bulk flow |
| 4 | **Vesicular (transcytosis)** | Caveolae — fluid-phase and receptor-mediated | Large molecules, proteins, hormones | Active/adsorptive transcytosis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous capillaries** (found in muscle, skin, lung, CNS) lack fenestrations and have tight junctions → low permeability. **Fenestrated capillaries** (kidney glomerulus, intestinal villi) and **sinusoidal/discontinuous capillaries** (liver, spleen, bone marrow) have large pores → high permeability.

## 1.4 Structural Anatomy of the Capillary Wall

The classical three-layer model of the capillary wall:
- **Endothelial cell glycocalyx** (luminal side) — a polymer brush ≈ **0.5–1 μm thick** that filters proteins sterically and colloidally. It is now recognized as a *primary* determinant of microvascular permeability.
- **Endothelial cell body** (with caveolae ≈ 70 nm diameter).
- **Basement membrane** (basal lamina) — collagen IV, laminin, proteoglycans; thickness ≈ **20–50 nm**.

The total hydraulic resistance is the sum of these three resistances in series:

$$ \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{R_{glycocalyx}} + \frac{1}{R_{cell}} + \frac{1}{R_{basement}} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Modern Revisions to Starling:** The 2007–2012 work by Levick, Michel, and Weinbaum revealed that under normal physiological conditions, the **interstitial colloid osmotic pressure (πᵢ) acts at the glycocalyx, not at the endothelial cleft exit**. This led to a "revised Starling principle" emphasizing glycocalyx integrity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Driving Forces for Transvascular Exchange

A solute (or water) moves across the endothelial barrier only when a **thermodynamic driving force** is non-zero. The total driving force is the gradient in **electrochemical potential**:

$$ \Delta \mu_i = RT \ln\left(\frac{C_{i,2}}{C_{i,1}}\right) + z_i F \Delta \psi + \bar{V}_i \Delta P $$

Where:
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K) — body temperature ≈ **310 K**
- $C_i$ = molar concentration of species *i* on each side
- $z_i$ = valence (charge number) of ion *i*
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $\Delta \psi$ = electrical potential difference across membrane
- $\bar{V}_i$ = partial molar volume
- $\Delta P$ = hydrostatic pressure difference

The **three classical driving forces** in microcirculation:

1. **Hydrostatic pressure gradient (ΔP)** — pushes fluid out (filtration direction).
2. **Osmotic/colloid osmotic (oncotic) pressure gradient (Δπ)** — pulls fluid in (reabsorption direction).
3. **Concentration gradient of permeant solutes (ΔC)** — drives diffusion.

## 2.2 Osmotic Pressure — Theory

**Osmotic pressure (π)** is the pressure that must be applied to a solution to prevent the inward flow of solvent across a **perfectly semipermeable membrane**.

For an **ideal dilute solution** of a non-permeating solute, the **van't Hoff equation** applies:

$$ \pi = RT c $$

Where $c$ is the molar solute concentration (mol/m³). For plasma at body temperature (T = 310 K):

- Total plasma osmolarity ≈ **285–295 mOsm/L** → total osmotic pressure ≈ **5,800 mmHg** (mostly Na⁺, Cl⁻, glucose, urea — all freely permeable to the capillary wall → *crystalloid osmotic pressure*).
- **Plasma protein (albumin) concentration ≈ 40 g/L** → **colloid osmotic (oncotic) pressure πc ≈ 25 mmHg** (proteins are largely retained by the capillary wall).

Since crystalloids equilibrate rapidly across the endothelium, the **effective** osmotic pressure that drives fluid movement is the **colloid osmotic pressure difference (Δπ)**, dominated by plasma proteins (albumin: **MW ≈ 69,000 Da**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oncotic pressure** is the *clinically relevant* osmotic pressure in Starling's equation. It is generated almost entirely by **albumin** because of its high plasma concentration (≈ 0.6 mM) and relatively low molecular weight compared to globulins.

The oncotic pressure of plasma proteins deviates from ideal van't Hoff behavior due to the **Donnan effect** (charged proteins attract counter-ions). A semi-empirical correlation commonly used:

$$ \pi = a_1 C + a_2 C^2 + a_3 C^3 $$

Where $C$ is the protein concentration (g/dL) and $a_1, a_2, a_3$ are empirical coefficients (Landis-Pappenheimer equation for plasma proteins).

## 2.3 Starling's Law of Filtration — The Original Statement

**Ernest Henry Starling (1896)** proposed that the net fluid movement across the capillary wall depends on the balance between hydrostatic and oncotic pressures on the two sides:

$$ J_v = L_p S \left[ (P_c - P_i) - \sigma (\pi_c - \pi_i) \right] $$

Where:
- $J_v$ = volumetric fluid flow rate (m³/s or mL/min) — outward (filtration) when positive
- $L_p$ = **hydraulic conductivity** of the membrane (m/Pa·s or mL/min/mmHg/cm²)
- $S$ = effective surface area available for exchange (m²)
- $P_c$ = capillary hydrostatic pressure (Pa or mmHg)
- $P_i$ = interstitial hydrostatic pressure (Pa or mmHg)
- $\pi_c$ = capillary (plasma) oncotic pressure (Pa or mmHg) ≈ **25 mmHg** in humans
- $\pi_i$ = interstitial oncotic pressure (Pa or mmHg) ≈ **1–3 mmHg**
- $\sigma$ = **Staverman reflection coefficient** (dimensionless, 0 ≤ σ ≤ 1)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention:** Outward flow (from capillary to interstitium) is *positive*. Typical net filtration rate in a systemic capillary ≈ **0.05 mL/min per 100 g tissue**.

## 2.4 The Reflection Coefficient (σ) — Physical Meaning

The **reflection coefficient** quantifies the membrane's *selectivity* for a given solute. It is the ratio of the **actual osmotic pressure exerted** by the solute to the **ideal (van't Hoff) osmotic pressure**:

$$ \sigma = \frac{\pi_{observed}}{\pi_{ideal}} $$

- σ = 1 → membrane completely rejects the solute (ideal semipermeable).
- σ = 0 → solute passes freely (no osmotic effect).

For plasma albumin in most continuous capillaries: **σ ≈ 0.75–0.95**. For small ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻): **σ ≈ 0.0–0.1** (negligible).

## 2.5 Kedem–Katchalsky Equations — The Phenomenological Framework

**Oran Kedem and Aharon Katchalsky (1958)** developed a **linear non-equilibrium thermodynamics** framework describing the coupled flows of volume ($J_v$) and solute ($J_s$) across a membrane. The transport is described by two coupled linear equations:

$$ J_v = L_p S (\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi) $$

$$ J_s = P S \Delta C + (1 - \sigma) \bar{C} J_v $$

Where:
- $J_s$ = solute flux (mol/s)
- $P$ = solute permeability coefficient (m/s)
- $\Delta C$ = solute concentration difference across the membrane
- $\bar{C}$ = mean solute concentration in the membrane (often approximated as the arithmetic mean of $C_1$ and $C_2$)
- $(1-\sigma)\bar{C} J_v$ = **convective (solvent-drag) contribution** to solute transport
- $P S \Delta C$ = **diffusive contribution** to solute transport

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Kedem–Katchalsky equations are the **gold-standard exam answer** when KTU asks for the formal description of transvascular transport. They unify (a) Starling filtration, (b) Fickian diffusion, and (c) solvent drag in a single linear phenomenological framework.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Numerical Value |
|---|---------|------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | $J_v = L_p S [(P_c - P_i) - \sigma(\pi_c - \pi_i)]$ | Net volumetric fluid flow (Starling) | $L_p S$ ≈ 0.5–1 mL/min/mmHg per 100 g |
| 2 | $\pi = R T c$ | van't Hoff osmotic pressure (ideal) | Plasma πc ≈ 25 mmHg |
| 3 | $\sigma = \pi_{obs}/\pi_{ideal}$ | Reflection coefficient | 0.75–0.95 for albumin |
| 4 | $J_v = L_p S (\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi)$ | Kedem–Katchalsky volume flow | — |
| 5 | $J_s = P S \Delta C + (1-\sigma)\bar{C} J_v$ | Kedem–Katchalsky solute flow | $P S$ for albumin ≈ 0.01 mL/min/100 g |
| 6 | $\Delta \mu_i = RT \ln(C_2/C_1) + z_i F \Delta \psi + \bar{V}_i \Delta P$ | Electrochemical potential difference | Body T = 310 K |
| 7 | $1/R_{total} = 1/R_{glyc} + 1/R_{cell} + 1/R_{BM}$ | Hydraulic resistance in series | — |
| 8 | $D = k_B T / (6 \pi \mu r)$ | Stokes–Einstein diffusivity | Albumin D ≈ 6 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit trap:** KTU exam answers must use **consistent units**. If you use mmHg for pressure, then $L_p S$ must be in mL/min/mmHg, and $J_v$ comes out in mL/min. If you use SI (Pa), then $L_p S$ is in m³/s/Pa, and $J_v$ in m³/s. Do NOT mix.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering & Clinical Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Dialysis / Hemofiltration** | Designing hollow-fiber membranes with controlled $L_p$, $P$, and $\sigma$ |
| **Drug delivery** | Liposomal/nanoparticle transport across tumor vasculature (EPR effect) |
| **Tissue engineering** | Designing perfusable scaffolds mimicking capillary transport |
| **Edema pathophysiology** | Quantifying burn, sepsis, and heart-failure edema via Starling imbalances |
| **Microfluidic organ-on-chip** | Recapitulating endothelial barriers in vitro with measurable $L_p$ and $P$ |
| **Ocular drug delivery** | Crossing the blood–retinal barrier (analogous to BBB) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the van't Hoff Osmotic Pressure Equation

The derivation begins with the **chemical potential** of the solvent (water) in an ideal solution:

$$ \mu_{water}^{solution} = \mu_{water}^{pure} + RT \ln(x_{water}) $$

Where $x_{water}$ is the mole fraction of water. For a dilute solution:

$$ x_{water} = 1 - x_{solute} \approx 1 - \frac{n_{solute}}{n_{water}} $$

Expanding the logarithm (Taylor series for small $x_{solute}$):

$$ \ln(1 - x_{solute}) \approx -x_{solute} = -\frac{n_{solute}}{n_{water}} $$

The chemical potential of water in solution is therefore:

$$ \mu_{water}^{solution} = \mu_{water}^{pure} - RT \frac{n_{solute}}{n_{water}} $$

To **prevent water inflow** (osmotic equilibrium), an external pressure $\pi$ must be applied to the solution side, raising its chemical potential:

$$ \mu_{water}^{pure} = \mu_{water}^{pure} - RT \frac{n_{solute}}{n_{water}} + \bar{V}_{water} \pi $$

Equilibrium condition $\Rightarrow$:

$$ RT \frac{n_{solute}}{n_{water}} = \bar{V}_{water} \pi $$

Using molar concentration $c = n_{solute}/V$ and $\bar{V}_{water} \approx V_{water}/n_{water}$:

$$ \pi = \frac{n_{solute}}{V} RT = cRT $$

$$ \boxed{\pi = c R T} $$

This is the **van't Hoff equation** — a thermodynamic necessity for dilute solutions. For plasma proteins (albumin at 0.6 mM, T = 310 K):

$$ \pi_{albumin}^{ideal} = (0.6 \text{ mol/m}^3)(8.314)(310) \approx 1.55 \text{ kPa} \approx 11.6 \text{ mmHg} $$

Observed π is higher (≈ 25 mmHg) due to **Donnan effect** of charged proteins.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kedem–Katchalsky Equations from Linear Non-Equilibrium Thermodynamics

The Kedem–Katchalsky equations are derived from the **Onsager reciprocal relations** in irreversible thermodynamics. For a membrane separating two solutions, two flows ($J_v$ = volume flow, $J_s$ = solute flow) are driven by two conjugate forces ($X_v$ and $X_s$).

The **rate of entropy production per unit membrane area** is:

$$ \Phi = J_v \cdot X_v + J_s \cdot X_s $$

The driving forces are the gradients of chemical potential:

$$ X_v = -\frac{1}{T} \Delta \mu_{water} $$

$$ X_s = -\frac{1}{T} \Delta \mu_{solute} $$

**Linear phenomenological laws** (Onsager):

$$ J_v = L_{vv} X_v + L_{vs} X_s $$

$$ J_s = L_{sv} X_v + L_{ss} X_s $$

**Onsager reciprocal relation:** $L_{vs} = L_{sv}$.

Substituting the explicit forms of $\Delta \mu_{water}$ and $\Delta \mu_{solute}$ in terms of pressure and concentration, and introducing the **three practical phenomenological coefficients** ($L_p$, $\sigma$, $\omega$):

$$ L_{vv} \rightarrow L_p, \quad L_{ss} \rightarrow \omega, \quad L_{vs} = L_{sv} \rightarrow \sigma \text{ coupling} $$

The final coupled equations emerge:

$$ J_v = L_p S (\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi) \quad \text{...(Volume flow)} $$

$$ J_s = P S \Delta C + (1 - \sigma) \bar{C} J_v \quad \text{...(Solute flow)} $$

Where:
- $L_p$ = hydraulic conductivity (volume flux per unit pressure)
- $\omega$ = solute mobility (permeability × RT)
- $P = \omega R T$ = solute permeability
- $\sigma$ = reflection coefficient (coupling parameter)

> [!NOTE]
> When $\sigma = 1$ (ideal semipermeable membrane), the solute flow equation reduces to $J_s = 0$ (no solute passes), and the volume equation is pure Starling filtration. When $\sigma = 0$ (solute freely permeable), the volume equation has no osmotic term, and the solute flow is purely diffusive with no solvent drag.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Starling Filtration in a Muscle Capillary

**Problem:** A resting skeletal muscle capillary has the following parameters:
- Capillary hydrostatic pressure: $P_c = 35$ mmHg
- Interstitial hydrostatic pressure: $P_i = -2$ mmHg (subatmospheric)
- Plasma oncotic pressure: $\pi_c = 25$ mmHg
- Interstitial oncotic pressure: $\pi_i = 2$ mmHg
- Reflection coefficient for albumin: $\sigma = 0.9$
- Hydraulic conductivity × area: $L_p S = 0.5$ mL/min/mmHg per 100 g tissue

**Calculate the net filtration rate $J_v$.**

**Step 1 — Compute hydrostatic pressure difference:**

$$ \Delta P = P_c - P_i = 35 - (-2) = 37 \text{ mmHg} $$

This is the force pushing fluid *out* of the capillary.

**Step 2 — Compute oncotic pressure difference:**

$$ \Delta \pi = \pi_c - \pi_i = 25 - 2 = 23 \text{ mmHg} $$

**Step 3 — Compute effective oncotic pressure difference (weighted by σ):**

$$ \sigma \Delta \pi = 0.9 \times 23 = 20.7 \text{ mmHg} $$

**Step 4 — Compute net filtration pressure (NFP):**

$$ NFP = \Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi = 37 - 20.7 = 16.3 \text{ mmHg} $$

This is positive → **net filtration** (outward flow).

**Step 5 — Compute net filtration rate:**

$$ J_v = L_p S \times NFP = 0.5 \times 16.3 = 8.15 \text{ mL/min per 100 g tissue} $$

**Step 6 — Check against physiological reality:**

Observed net filtration in resting muscle is ≈ 0.05 mL/min per 100 g — much lower than this calculation. The discrepancy is explained by:
- The **revised Starling principle** (glycocalyx acts as the primary osmotic barrier, not the cleft exit).
- The fraction of $L_p S$ effectively contributing to filtration is smaller than total.
- Lymphatic drainage returns most of the filtered fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This example illustrates why the **classical Starling equation over-predicts filtration** in many tissues. The modern revised Starling principle explains the discrepancy by recognizing that the **glycocalyx** — not the endothelial cleft exit — is the effective site of oncotic pressure sensing.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Transport Pathways

| Pathway | Dominant Force | Reflection Coefficient (σ) | Hydraulic Conductivity ($L_p$) | Typical Permeability ($P$) |
|---------|----------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Continuous capillary (muscle) | Starling | 0.75–0.95 (albumin) | Low (10⁻¹⁰ m/Pa·s) | Albumin: ~10⁻⁸ m/s |
| Fenestrated capillary (kidney glomerulus) | Pressure | 0.05 (albumin, due to size + charge barrier) | High (10⁻⁸ m/Pa·s) | Very high (P/albumin) |
| Sinusoidal capillary (liver) | Pressure | ~0 (large pores) | Very high | Albumin passes freely |
| Tight BBB endothelium | Active + selective | 0.99+ | Very low | Extremely low (tight junctions) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Starling Force Balance Across Capillary Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Blood Plasma Compartment] -->|Pc = 35 mmHg| B[Endothelial Barrier]
    B -->|Pi = -2 mmHg| C[Interstitial Compartment]
    A2[Plasma Proteins] -->|πc = 25 mmHg| B2[Reflection σ times π]
    C2[Interstitial Proteins] -->|πi = 2 mmHg| B2
    B2 --> D[Net Filtration Pressure NFP]
    B --> D
    D --> E{Jv positive?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Filtration Outward]
    E -->|No| G[Reabsorption Inward]
    F --> H[Lymphatic Drainage]
    G --> H
```

```mermaid
    A1[Driving Force ΔP minus sigma times Δπ] --> B1[Volume Flow Jv]
    A2[Concentration Gradient ΔC] --> B2[Diffusive Solute Flux Ps times ΔC]
    B1 --> C1[Convective Solute Flux: 1 minus sigma times Cbar times Jv]
    B2 --> D1[Solute Flow Js]
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E[Tissue and Cell Exchange]

```
