# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **Student guidance:** Every line of algebra below is shown explicitly. KTU board examiners award **partial credit for intermediate steps** — never skip a substitution.

---

## DERIVATION 3.1 — Zero-Order Release Rate of an Elementary Osmotic Pump

**Starting point:** Semi-permeable membrane (SPM) separates the reservoir (interior, side 1) from the surrounding aqueous medium (side 2). Water is the only species that crosses the membrane.

**Step 1 — Write the volumetric water flux from side 2 → side 1 through the SPM using the Kedem–Katchalsky equation (irreversible thermodynamics):**

$$J_v = L_p \, (\sigma \, \Delta\pi - \Delta P)$$

Because the rigid housing of the osmotic pump **does not deform**, the hydrostatic pressure inside the device equilibrates with that outside at steady state:

$$\Delta P \approx 0$$

Substituting:

$$J_v = L_p \, \sigma \, \Delta\pi$$

**Step 2 — Relate $J_v$ to the volumetric flow rate of drug solution out of the orifice:**

Since the reservoir volume is conserved (incompressible liquid + rigid housing), the water entering through the SPM equals the saturated drug solution leaving through the orifice:

$$\frac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dt} = J_v \cdot A = L_p \, \sigma \, \Delta\pi \cdot A$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to mass of drug using the saturated drug concentration $C_s$ (assumed constant while solid drug is present):**

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = C_s \cdot \frac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dt} = \frac{A}{1} \cdot L_p \, \sigma \, \Delta\pi \cdot C_s$$

Introducing membrane thickness $h$ explicitly via the relationship $L_p = \dfrac{K_{\text{hyd}}}{h}$ (where $K_{\text{hyd}}$ is the intrinsic hydraulic permeability of the membrane material):

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{dM}{dt} = \dfrac{A}{h} \, L_p \, \sigma \, \Delta\pi \, C_s\;}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Van 't Hoff equation to express $\Delta\pi$ in terms of the osmotic agent concentration (often the saturated NaCl concentration):**

$$\Delta\pi = i \, C_{\text{osm}} \, R \, T$$

For NaCl at saturation ($C_{\text{osm}} \approx 6.14$ mol/L, $i = 2$) at body temperature $T = 310$ K:

$$\Delta\pi = 2 \times 6.14 \times 10^{3} \, \text{mol/m}^3 \times 8.314 \times 310$$

$$\Delta\pi = 2 \times 6.14 \times 10^{3} \times 2577.3 \approx 3.16 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \approx 31.6 \text{ MPa}$$

This is the *physiological* osmotic pressure driving the pump — comparable to the osmotic pressure of seawater (≈ 2.5 MPa), illustrating why osmotic pumps are such powerful natural engines.

---

## DERIVATION 3.2 — Higuchi Equation for Drug Release from a Planar Monolithic Matrix

**Setup:** A slab of polymer (thickness $L$, area $A$) contains a uniform dispersion of solid drug particles at loading $A_{\text{load}} > C_s$ (excess drug). Drug dissolves in the polymer, diffuses outward, and is removed by a perfect sink at the surface $x = 0$.

**Step 1 — Apply Fick's Second Law in 1D to the dissolved drug in the polymer:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 2 — Boundary & initial conditions:**

$$C(x=0, t) = 0 \quad \text{(perfect sink)}$$

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}(x=L, t) = 0 \quad \text{(symmetry, slab centre)}$$

$$C(x, 0) = C_s \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le L \quad \text{(initial dissolved drug = solubility)}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the *pseudo-steady-state* (Higuchi) approximation:** Because solid drug particles act as a *moving front* replenishing the dissolved drug, and because the front moves slowly compared to diffusion, the concentration profile is approximately linear:

$$C(x, t) \approx C_s \left( 1 - \frac{x}{\xi(t)} \right)$$

where $\xi(t)$ is the position of the *depletion front* separating the still-loaded region (solid drug present) from the depleted region (no solid).

**Step 4 — Mass balance on the dissolved drug in the depleted zone:**

The total mass released per unit area at time $t$ equals the integral of the linear profile:

$$Q(t) = \int_0^{\xi(t)} C(x,t) \, dx = \frac{1}{2} \, C_s \, \xi(t)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Fick's first law at the sink face ($x = 0$):**

$$J(t) = \left. -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0} = D \, \frac{C_s}{\xi(t)}$$

**Step 6 — Equate $J$ to $\frac{dQ}{dt}$ and solve the differential equation for $\xi(t)$:**

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} C_s \frac{d\xi}{dt} = \frac{D C_s}{\xi}$$

$$\xi \, d\xi = 2D \, dt$$

Integrating from $t = 0$ (where $\xi = 0$):

$$\frac{\xi^2}{2} = 2 D t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \xi(t) = 2 \sqrt{Dt}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\xi(t)$ back into $Q(t)$:**

$$Q(t) = \frac{1}{2} C_s \times 2\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{C_s^2 \, D \, t}$$

Generalizing for a matrix with initial drug loading $A > C_s$ (accounting for the fact that both dissolved and solid drug contribute to the released amount):

$$\boxed{\;Q(t) = \sqrt{ D \, C_s \, (2A - C_s) \, t }\;}$$

This is the **Higuchi square-root-of-time law**, valid when $A \gg C_s$ (excess drug loading).

---

## DERIVATION 3.3 — Steady-State Flux Through a Transdermal Patch (Reservoir Type)

**Setup:** Patch layers in series: drug reservoir (concentration $C_R$) → rate-controlling membrane (thickness $h_m$, partition coefficient $K_m$, diffusivity $D_m$) → skin surface (sink).

**Step 1 — At the reservoir–membrane interface, the equilibrium partition sets the membrane surface concentration:**

$$C_{m,\text{left}} = K_m \, C_R$$

**Step 2 — At the membrane–skin interface, partition into the skin surface (with partition $K_{m \to s}$):**

$$C_{s,\text{surface}} = K_{m \to s} \, C_{m,\text{right}}$$

**Step 3 — Assuming a *perfect sink* in the dermal capillaries**, $C_{s,\text{deep}} = 0$.

**Step 4 — Apply Fick's first law across the rate-controlling membrane (the rate-limiting layer):**

$$J = \frac{D_m \, (C_{m,\text{left}} - C_{m,\text{right}})}{h_m} \approx \frac{D_m K_m C_R}{h_m}$$

(assuming $C_{m,\text{right}} \approx 0$ because the skin layer beyond the membrane is a good sink).

**Step 5 — Introduce the permeability $P_m = D_m K_m / h_m$:**

$$\boxed{\;J = P_m \, C_R = \frac{D_m K_m C_R}{h_m}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** $J$ depends on the **membrane permeability and reservoir concentration** — it does NOT depend on the patch size (once geometry is set). This is why reservoir patches deliver a *constant* dose *per unit area* — scaling the area scales the dose linearly.

---

## DERIVATION 3.4 — Permeation Through a Two-Layer Composite (Membrane + Skin)

Many advanced transdermal formulations consider the **rate-controlling membrane AND the skin** in series. The total resistance is the sum:

$$\frac{1}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{P_m} + \frac{1}{P_{sc}}$$

where $P_{sc} = D_{sc} K_{sc} / h_{sc}$ is the permeability of the **stratum corneum** (typically the dominant resistance for many drugs).

**Step 1 — For a transdermal fentanyl patch** with $P_m = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ cm/s and $P_{sc} = 1 \times 10^{-7}$ cm/s:

$$\frac{1}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} + \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\frac{1}{P_{\text{total}}} = 2 \times 10^{5} + 1 \times 10^{7} = 1.02 \times 10^{7} \text{ s/cm}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 9.8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm/s} \approx 10^{-7} \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 2 — The steady-state flux with $C_R = 10$ mg/mL = 0.01 mg/cm³:**

$$J = 9.8 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.01 = 9.8 \times 10^{-10} \text{ mg/(cm}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

$$J = 9.8 \times 10^{-10} \times 3600 \times 24 \times 1000 = 0.085 \text{ μg/(cm}^2 \cdot \text{h)}$$

This shows why transdermal doses are typically **< 100 μg/h** and why drug candidates must be **highly potent** (e.g., fentanyl, nicotine, nitroglycerin).

---

## DERIVATION 3.5 — Drug Release from a Non-Erodible Reservoir Implant (Finite Drug)

For a spherical reservoir implant of inner radius $r_i$ and membrane outer radius $r_o$:

**Step 1 — Radial diffusion through a spherical shell:**

$$J(r) = -D_m \frac{dC}{dr}$$

**Step 2 — At steady state, the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ is constant through any spherical shell:**

$$\dot{m} = J(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2 = -D_m \cdot 4\pi r^2 \frac{dC}{dr}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate from $r_i$ to $r_o$:**

$$\dot{m} \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi D_m \int_{C_{i}}^{0} dC$$

$$\dot{m} \left( \frac{1}{r_i} - \frac{1}{r_o} \right) = 4\pi D_m C_i$$

where $C_i$ is the (constant) drug concentration in the core. Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m} = \dfrac{4\pi D_m C_i}{\dfrac{1}{r_i} - \dfrac{1}{r_o}}\;}$$

This is a *spherical analogue* of the planar reservoir equation. For $r_o \to r_i$ (thin membrane), the geometry reduces to the planar case.

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"What are the essential components of an elementary osmotic pump? Explain how the osmotic pressure difference is generated and utilized for drug delivery."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The elementary osmotic pump (Rose–Nelson, 1955) has three essential components:
1. **A semipermeable membrane (SPM)** — typically cellulose acetate, permeable to water but not to the drug/osmotic agent.
2. **An osmotic engine** — a saturated solution or solid salt (e.g., NaCl, KCl) that generates a high osmotic pressure difference ($\Delta\pi$) with respect to the external body fluid.
3. **A drug reservoir** with a **laser-drilled delivery orifice** (≈ 0.1–1 mm) through which drug is expelled.

**Mechanism:** Body water is osmotically imbibed through the SPM into the osmotic engine, driven by $\Delta\pi$ (Van 't Hoff: $\pi = iCRT$). The incoming water increases the hydrostatic pressure inside the rigid housing, forcing a saturated drug solution out through the orifice at a *constant volumetric rate* governed by $\frac{dM}{dt} = \frac{A}{h} L_p \sigma \Delta\pi C_s$. Because the drug reservoir remains saturated (solid drug present), the release is **zero-order** until the solid drug is depleted.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for listing components, 1 mark for $\Delta\pi$ mechanism, 1 mark for the zero-order release equation.)*

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Differentiate between matrix-type and reservoir-type transdermal patches based on their release kinetics and governing equations."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Reservoir Type | Matrix Type |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| **Architecture** | Drug in liquid/gel core separated by a rate-controlling membrane | Drug dispersed uniformly within a polymer matrix |
| **Governing Equation** | $J = D_m K_m C_R / h_m$ (Fick's 1st law) | $Q(t) = \sqrt{D C_s (2A - C_s)\,t}$ (Higuchi) |
| **Release Kinetics** | **Zero-order** (constant rate) | **$\sqrt{t}$** (declining rate) |
| **Risk on Membrane Rupture** | Dose dumping | No dose dumping |
| **Examples** | Transderm-Nitro®, Duragesic® | Nitro-Dur®, Climara® |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for architectural difference, 1 mark for kinetics difference, 1 mark for citing the correct equation.)*

---

## PART B — MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE (14 MARKS)

> Choose **EITHER Question A OR Question B** and answer fully.

---

### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 MARKS)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**"A pharmaceutical company is developing an implantable drug delivery device based on a cylindrical PLGA (poly-lactic-co-glycolic acid) reservoir implant (length 2 cm, diameter 0.4 cm) loaded with a drug at an initial concentration of 200 mg/cm³. The drug has a saturation solubility of 5 mg/cm³ in the PLGA matrix, and its diffusivity in the matrix is $2 \times 10^{-8}$ cm²/s."**

**(a) Apply the Higuchi model to derive the cumulative drug release per unit area as a function of time. Calculate the cumulative release at $t = 10$ hours, $t = 100$ hours, and $t = 1000$ hours. (7 marks)**

**(b) Calculate the time required to release 80% of the drug load, and comment on whether the matrix device is best classified as a diffusion-controlled or chemically-controlled system. (7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 SOLUTION TO (a) — Higuchi Model Derivation & Numerical Evaluation

**Step 1 — Recall the Higuchi equation** (derived in Section 3.2):

$$Q(t) = \sqrt{ D \, C_s \, (2A - C_s) \, t }$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given data.** All values in **CGS** units:
- $D = 2 \times 10^{-8}$ cm²/s
- $C_s = 5$ mg/cm³
- $A = 200$ mg/cm³ (initial drug loading)
- $2A - C_s = 400 - 5 = 395$ mg/cm³ $\approx 2A$ (since $A \gg C_s$, simplify)

**Simplified Higuchi constant:**

$$K_H = \sqrt{ D \cdot C_s \cdot 2A } = \sqrt{ 2 \times 10^{-8} \times 5 \times 400 }$$

$$K_H = \sqrt{ 4 \times 10^{-5} } = 6.32 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mg/(cm}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{1/2}\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Q(t)$ at the three times:**

At $t = 10$ h $= 3.6 \times 10^4$ s:

$$Q(10\,\text{h}) = 6.32 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{3.6 \times 10^4} = 6.32 \times 10^{-3} \times 189.7 = 1.20 \, \text{mg/cm}^2$$

At $t = 100$ h $= 3.6 \times 10^5$ s:

$$Q(100\,\text{h}) = 6.32 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{3.6 \times 10^5} = 6.32 \times 10^{-3} \times 600 = 3.79 \, \text{mg/cm}^2$$

At $t = 1000$ h $= 3.6 \times 10^6$ s:

$$Q(1000\,\text{h}) = 6.32 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{3.6 \times 10^6} = 6.32 \times 10^{-3} \times 1897.4 = 12.0 \, \text{mg/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Convert to cumulative drug mass released over the entire cylindrical surface area.**

Surface area of cylinder (lateral only, neglecting end caps as approximation):

$$A_{\text{cyl}} = \pi d L = \pi \times 0.4 \times 2 = 2.513 \, \text{cm}^2$$

Total drug released at each time:

- $M(10\,\text{h}) = 1.20 \times 2.513 = 3.02$ mg
- $M(100\,\text{h}) = 3.79 \times 2.513 = 9.52$ mg
- $M(1000\,\text{h}) = 12.0 \times 2.513 = 30.1$ mg

**Note the $\sqrt{t}$ dependence:** $M(100)/M(10) = 9.52/3.02 = 3.15 \approx \sqrt{10}$. ✔ Verified.

*(Full 7 marks: 2 marks for equation, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for numerical results, 1 mark for $\sqrt{t}$ verification.)*

---

#### 🔹 SOLUTION TO (b) — 80% Release Time & System Classification

**Step 1 — Total drug load in the cylinder:**

Volume of cylinder:

$$V = \pi r^2 L = \pi (0.2)^2 \times 2 = 0.2513 \, \text{cm}^3$$

Total drug loaded:

$$M_{\text{total}} = A \times V = 200 \times 0.2513 = 50.27 \, \text{mg}$$

**Step 2 — 80% release target:**

$$M_{80\%} = 0.8 \times 50.27 = 40.21 \, \text{mg}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the limit of the Higuchi model.** The Higuchi equation is valid as long as **solid drug remains** in the matrix (i.e., $A \gg C_s$). When cumulative release $\approx$ the dissolved-drug component ($C_s \times L$), the solid is exhausted and the release transitions to a *first-order* exponential decay of the dissolved reservoir.

The dissolved drug in the matrix at $t = 0$:

$$M_{\text{dissolved},0} = C_s \times V = 5 \times 0.2513 = 1.257 \, \text{mg}$$

This is only 2.5% of the total load — so the Higuchi regime is applicable for the *bulk* of the release.

**Step 4 — Solve for the time at which 80% has been released.** Using the simple Higuchi approximation up to its validity limit (until $Q$ approaches $\approx A L$, the total loading per unit area):

$$A L = 200 \times 2 = 400 \, \text{mg/cm}^2 \text{ (volume per unit area)}$$

So $Q_{80\%}/A_L = 0.8$ at:

$$0.8 \times 400 = 320 \, \text{mg/cm}^2 \text{ (target cumulative per unit area)}$$

From $Q(t) = K_H \sqrt{t}$:

$$\sqrt{t} = \frac{Q}{K_H} = \frac{320}{6.32 \times 10^{-3}} = 50633 \, \text{s}^{1/2}$$

$$t = (50633)^2 = 2.563 \times 10^9 \, \text{s} = 7.21 \times 10^3 \, \text{h} = \mathbf{300} \, \text{days}$$

> **Caveat:** In reality, PLGA undergoes hydrolytic chain scission with a *degradation half-life* of typically **30–100 days**. The 300-day Higuchi time is **comparable to or longer than the polymer degradation time**, so chemical control becomes significant.

**Step 5 — Classification of the device:**

The release is **initially diffusion-controlled** (Higuchi $\sqrt{t}$ dominates the early and intermediate phases) but **later transitions to chemically-controlled** (PLGA bulk erosion accelerates release in the final stage, often producing a "triphasic" release profile: burst → slow → accelerating). 

**Conclusion:** The system is a **diffusion–erosion coupled (chemo-diffusive) device**, characteristic of PLGA-based systems. Pure Higuchi behavior is observed only during the intermediate regime.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for total load, 2 marks for time calculation, 1 mark for comparing with degradation time, 2 marks for classification and discussion.)*

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 MARKS)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"An elementary osmotic pump is fabricated with a cellulose acetate semipermeable membrane of area $A = 1.5$ cm² and thickness $h = 0.025$ cm. The hydraulic permeability of the membrane is $L_p = 4.2 \times 10^{-14}$ m³/(m²·s·Pa), and the reflection coefficient $\sigma = 0.98$. The reservoir contains a saturated drug solution at $C_s = 300$ mg/mL. The osmotic engine uses saturated NaCl at 37 °C."**

**(a) Compute the osmotic pressure difference using the Van 't Hoff equation. Hence determine the steady-state volumetric water influx and the drug release rate (mg/h). (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare the predicted release rate with the in vivo measurement of 8.5 mg/h. Discuss two possible reasons for the discrepancy and explain how the design can be modified to reduce the deviation. (7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 SOLUTION TO (a) — Osmotic Pressure, Water Influx, Drug Release Rate

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta\pi$ for saturated NaCl.**

Saturated NaCl at 37 °C: $C_{\text{osm}} \approx 6.14$ mol/L $= 6.14 \times 10^3$ mol/m³. Van 't Hoff factor $i = 2$ (NaCl fully dissociated). $T = 310$ K.

$$\Delta\pi = i \, C_{\text{osm}} \, R \, T = 2 \times 6.14 \times 10^3 \times 8.314 \times 310$$

$$\Delta\pi = 2 \times 6.14 \times 10^3 \times 2577.3 = 3.165 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} \approx 31.65 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric water influx through the SPM.**

Convert units: $A = 1.5$ cm² $= 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²; $L_p = 4.2 \times 10^{-14}$ m³/(m²·s·Pa); $\sigma = 0.98$.

$$J_v = L_p \, \sigma \, \Delta\pi = 4.2 \times 10^{-14} \times 0.98 \times 3.165 \times 10^7$$

$$J_v = 1.302 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m/s} = 1.302 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm/s}$$

Volumetric flow rate:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = J_v \times A = 1.302 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.953 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

In mL/h:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = 1.953 \times 10^{-10} \times 10^6 \times 3600 = 0.7032 \, \text{mL/h}$$

**Step 3 — Drug release rate.**

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = C_s \times \frac{dV}{dt} = 300 \, \text{mg/mL} \times 0.7032 \, \text{mL/h}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dM}{dt} = 210.96 \, \text{mg/h} \approx 211 \, \text{mg/h}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for $\Delta\pi$ calculation, 2 marks for $J_v$, 1 mark for $dV/dt$, 2 marks for $dM/dt$.)*

---

#### 🔹 SOLUTION TO (b) — Discrepancy Analysis & Design Modifications

**Observed discrepancy:**

$$\text{Measured} = 8.5 \, \text{mg/h} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Predicted} = 211 \, \text{mg/h}$$

Ratio: measured is **~25× lower** than predicted.

**Reason 1 — Reflection coefficient in vivo is not 0.98.**

The reflection coefficient $\sigma$ is a property of the *membrane–solute pair*. In a complex in vivo environment (plasma proteins, cells, bio-fouling of the SPM), the effective $\sigma$ may drop to 0.3–0.6. Even halving $\sigma$ to 0.5 cuts the release by ~50%. Bio-fouling (protein adsorption, fibroblast overgrowth, collagen capsule formation around an implant) is a major *unmodelled* resistance.

**Reason 2 — Local osmotic equilibration and tissue compliance.**

The equation $\frac{dM}{dt} \propto \Delta\pi$ assumes $\Delta\pi$ remains at the saturated-NaCl value. In practice:
- The external body fluid may have a non-zero osmotic pressure (especially in inflamed or fibrotic tissue), reducing $\Delta\pi$.
- The "rigid housing" assumption fails in soft polymeric pumps; $\Delta P$ is not zero and opposes $\Delta\pi$.

**Design modifications to reduce discrepancy:**

1. **Increase membrane thickness $h$** to reduce $J_v$ — this is the *most direct* way to lower the release rate by a predictable factor. Doubling $h$ halves $dM/dt$.

2. **Increase reservoir drug dilution** — if $C_s$ is reduced (e.g., by co-formulating with an inert soluble filler), $\frac{dM}{dt}$ scales linearly with $C_s$.

3. **Use a partial barrier coating or "asymmetric" membrane** with a thicker dense skin, raising effective $h$.

4. **Add a flow-restrictor at the orifice** (e.g., a sintered plug) to add a *viscous* resistance term that is independent of $\Delta\pi$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Loss Points:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** ($A$ in cm² but $L_p$ in SI → must convert both consistently). Students often mix CGS and SI; this **loses 2–3 marks** outright.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $\sigma$** in the Kedem–Katchalsky equation. The Van 't Hoff equation alone does NOT give the release rate.
> 3. **Confusing $C_s$ (saturation concentration in reservoir) with drug dose or dose/area.** State explicitly what $C_s$ represents.
> 4. **Not justifying the discrepancy with a *physical* mechanism.** Generic statements like "experimental error" fetch **zero marks** — name the mechanism (bio-fouling, $\sigma$ change, non-rigid housing, etc.).
> 5. **In the Higuchi problem, students often write $Q(t) = \sqrt{DCt}$ without the $(2A - C_s)$ factor** — this is a *board-favorite error* and costs 1 mark minimum.

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this 12-point checklist before walking into the KTU ESE:**

- ☐ **Osmotic Pump Release Equation:** $\frac{dM}{dt} = \frac{A}{h} L_p \sigma \Delta\pi C_s$ — independent of GI pH, food, agitation.
- ☐ **Van 't Hoff Equation:** $\pi = i C R T$ — gives ~31.6 MPa for saturated NaCl at 37 °C.
- ☐ **Reflection Coefficient $\sigma$** quantifies membrane selectivity (1 = perfectly semipermeable, 0 = non-selective).
- ☐ **Kedem–Katchalsky:** $J_v = L_p (\sigma \Delta\pi - \Delta P)$ — the master transport equation for SPM.
- ☐ **Elementary vs Push-Pull Pump:** Push-pull has TWO osmotic compartments (drug + push) to handle highly soluble drugs.
- ☐ **Transdermal Permeability:** $P = D K / h$; total resistance is the sum of series resistances $\frac{1}{P_{tot}} = \sum \frac{1}{P_i}$.
- ☐ **Stratum Corneum = Rate-Limiting Barrier** for most transdermal drugs; typical $D_{sc} \sim 10^{-13}$ to $10^{-12}$ m²/s.
- ☐ **Higuchi Equation:** $Q(t) = \sqrt{D C_s (2A - C_s) t}$ — valid for monolithic matrix with $A \gg C_s$; gives $\sqrt{t}$ kinetics.
- ☐ **Reservoir Device = Zero-Order; Matrix Device = $\sqrt{t}$ Order.**
- ☐ **PLGA Bulk Erosion** produces a triphasic release: (i) burst, (ii) slow lag, (iii) accelerating erosion phase.
- ☐ **Diffusion penetration depth:** $\delta \sim \sqrt{Dt}$ — basis for "characteristic diffusion time" $t_{1/2}^{diff} \sim L^2 / D$.
- ☐ **Iontophoresis enhancement flux** scales as $J_{ion} \propto D z F \Delta V / (R T h)$ — Nernst–Planck electromigration.
